title: Revenue-Maximizing Shill Bid in a Second-Price Auction
learning_objective: Analyze how an auctioneer can transform a second-price auction by submitting a shill bid after observing bids.
preliminaries: After observing real bids \((b_1,\ldots,b_N)\), the auctioneer may submit a bid to maximize revenue. The auctioneer has no value for keeping the object. If the auctioneer can tie the highest bid and choose a real highest bidder as winner, the supremum revenue is attained; otherwise use an arbitrarily small \(\varepsilon>0\).
question: Given real bids \((b_1,\ldots,b_N)\), what bid should the cheating auctioneer submit, who wins, and what does the winner pay?
solution: Let \(b_{(1)}=\max_i b_i\) be the highest real bid. The auctioneer wants the highest real bidder to win while making the second-highest bid as large as possible. If the auctioneer can bid \(b_{(1)}\) and then use the tie-breaking rule to select a real bidder as winner, he submits \(b_0=b_{(1)}\). The real highest bidder wins and pays \(b_{(1)}\), his own bid. If the rules require the auctioneer not to tie as a winning bidder, the auctioneer instead submits \(b_0=b_{(1)}-\varepsilon\), and the highest real bidder pays \(b_{(1)}-\varepsilon\). Thus the cheating auctioneer makes the winner pay essentially the highest real bid rather than the second-highest real bid.

source.original_question: Suppose the auctioneer announces a second-price auction but cheats by submitting his own bid after opening all real bids. Given real bids, what bid should the auctioneer submit, who wins, and what does the winner pay?
source.notes: Converted from the source answer and independently checked, adding the tie-breaking qualification.

validation.notes: The revenue-maximizing shill bid is at the highest real bid, or arbitrarily close below it if exact tying is unavailable.

title: Truthful Bidding Fails with a Cheating Auctioneer
learning_objective: Show that post-bid shill bidding destroys truthful bidding as an equilibrium.
preliminaries: In the cheating auctioneer version, after real bids are opened the auctioneer submits a shill bid that makes the highest real bidder pay essentially his own bid. Bidders know this rule.
question: Is the truthful Bayes-Nash equilibrium from the honest second-price auction still an equilibrium when the auctioneer cheats? Explain.
solution: No. With the shill bid, the winner pays essentially his own bid, so the strategic incentives are those of a first-price auction. Under truthful bidding, a bidder with value \(v_i>\underline V\) who wins with positive probability pays approximately \(v_i\), earning approximately zero surplus when he wins. A small underbid, such as \(v_i-\varepsilon\), still wins in states where the highest opposing bid is below \(v_i-\varepsilon\), but yields strictly positive surplus \(\varepsilon\) in those winning states. For continuous value distributions, this event has positive probability for interior types. Therefore truthful bidding is not a best response and cannot be a Bayes-Nash equilibrium in the cheating-auctioneer game.

source.original_question: In the cheating-auctioneer version of the announced second-price auction, is truthful bidding still a Bayes Nash equilibrium? Explain.
source.notes: Converted from the source answer and independently checked by constructing a profitable underbidding deviation.

validation.notes: The deviation argument correctly identifies the first-price-like incentives created by the shill bid.

title: Second-Price Auction as a Bayesian Game
learning_objective: State the players, types, actions, beliefs, allocation rule, and payoffs of a private-value auction.
preliminaries: There are \(N\) bidders. Bidder \(i\)'s value \(v_i\) is privately observed, and the values are i.i.d. with common distribution \(F\) on \([\underline V,\overline V]\). Bids must lie in \([\underline V,\overline V]\). The object goes to a highest bidder and the winner pays the second-highest bid.
question: Set up the sealed-bid second-price auction as a Bayesian game played by the bidders.
solution: The Bayesian game is as follows. The player set is \(\{1,\ldots,N\}\). Player \(i\)'s type is \(v_i\in[\underline V,\overline V]\). The common prior is the product distribution generated by independent draws from \(F\), so conditional on \(v_i\), bidder \(i\) believes the other values are independent with cdf \(F\). A pure strategy for bidder \(i\) is a measurable bidding function \(b_i:[\underline V,\overline V]\to[\underline V,\overline V]\). Given realized bids, a highest bidder wins, with ties resolved by the auctioneer's specified tie-breaking rule, and the winner pays the second-highest bid. If bidder \(i\) wins at price \(m_i\), his payoff is \(v_i-m_i\); if he loses, his payoff is \(0\). This specifies types, beliefs, actions, outcomes, and payoffs, so it is the requested Bayesian game.

source.original_question: A seller sells one unit by sealed-bid second-price auction to N bidders with iid private values from F on [V_lower,V_upper]. Each bidder observes only his own value. Set up the auction as a Bayesian game.

title: Truthful Bidding in a Second-Price Auction
learning_objective: Prove that truthful bidding is a Bayes-Nash equilibrium in a private-value second-price auction.
preliminaries: Use the sealed-bid second-price auction with private values \(v_i\in[\underline V,\overline V]\). A bidder's payoff is value minus price if he wins and zero if he loses.
question: Find a Bayes-Nash equilibrium of this second-price auction.
solution: The truthful strategy profile \(b_i(v_i)=v_i\) for every bidder \(i\) is a Bayes-Nash equilibrium. In fact, truthful bidding is weakly dominant. Fix bidder \(i\)'s value \(v_i\) and fix the opponents' bids. Let \(m\) be the highest opposing bid. If \(v_i\ge m\), bidding \(v_i\) wins and gives payoff \(v_i-m\ge 0\). Any higher bid also wins at the same price, and any lower bid either still wins at the same price or loses and gives \(0\), so it cannot improve payoff. If \(v_i<m\), bidding \(v_i\) loses and gives payoff \(0\). A lower bid also loses, while a higher bid that wins would require paying \(m>v_i\), giving negative payoff. Thus no bid yields a strictly higher payoff than bidding \(v_i\), for every value and every profile of opponents' bids. Therefore truthful bidding is a best response for every type when others bid truthfully, so it is a Bayes-Nash equilibrium.

source.original_question: Find a Bayes Nash equilibrium of the sealed-bid second-price auction with iid private values.
source.notes: Converted from the source answer and independently checked by the standard dominant-strategy argument.

validation.notes: The case split on the highest opposing bid proves weak dominance and hence BNE.

title: Bayesian Game Form of a First-Price Auction
learning_objective: Represent a symmetric private-value first-price auction as a static Bayesian game.
preliminaries: Two bidders compete for one indivisible good. Each bidder \(i\) has valuation \(\theta_i\), independently and uniformly distributed on \([0,x]\), with \(x>0\). Bids are simultaneous, nonnegative, and the highest bid wins and pays its bid. Ties are broken with probability one half for each bidder.
question: Represent the auction as a static game of incomplete information: specify players, actions, types, nature's strategy, and payoffs.
solution: The players are \(N=\{1,2\}\). Each action set is \(A_i=[0,\infty)\), where an action is a bid \(b_i\). Each type set is \(T_i=[0,x]\), and type \(\theta_i\in T_i\) is bidder \(i\)'s valuation. Nature independently draws \(\theta_1\) and \(\theta_2\) from the uniform distribution on \([0,x]\), and each bidder observes only her own type. Payoffs are \[u_i(b_i,b_j,\theta_i)=\begin{cases}\theta_i-b_i,& b_i>b_j,\\\frac{\theta_i-b_i}{2},& b_i=b_j,\\0,& b_i<b_j.\end{cases}\] A strategy for bidder \(i\) is a measurable function \(s_i:[0,x]\to[0,\infty)\). The appropriate solution concept is Bayesian Nash equilibrium.

title: Symmetric Linear Equilibrium in a First-Price Auction
learning_objective: Derive the symmetric linear bidding equilibrium in a two-bidder first-price auction with uniform private values.
preliminaries: Two risk-neutral bidders have independent valuations uniformly distributed on \([0,x]\). Bids are nonnegative, the high bid wins, and the winner pays her bid. Consider symmetric increasing strategies of the form \(s(\theta)=\beta\theta\), with \(\beta>0\).
question: Find an equilibrium of the game using strategies of the form \(s_i(\theta_i)=\beta_i\theta_i\).
solution: Suppose bidder 2 uses the increasing linear strategy \(s(\theta_2)=\beta\theta_2\). If bidder 1 with value \(v\) bids \(b\in[0,\beta x]\), she wins when \(\beta\theta_2<b\), which has probability \(b/(\beta x)\). Her expected payoff is \[\frac{b}{\beta x}(v-b).\] Maximizing with respect to \(b\) gives \[\frac{\partial}{\partial b}\left[\frac{b}{\beta x}(v-b)\right]=\frac{v-2b}{\beta x}=0,\] so the best response bid is \(b=v/2\). In a symmetric equilibrium this best response must equal \(\beta v\), so \(\beta=1/2\). Therefore \[s_i(\theta_i)=\frac{\theta_i}{2}\qquad i=1,2\] is the symmetric linear Bayesian Nash equilibrium. The candidate bid lies in \([0,x/2]\), so it is within the range assumed above.

source.original_question: Find an equilibrium using strategies of the form s_i(theta_i)=beta_i theta_i.
source.notes: Converted from the answer key's symmetric linear bidding derivation.

validation.notes: The best-response calculation yields beta equals one half for the symmetric uniform two-bidder first-price auction.

title: Truthful Bidding Is Not a First-Price Auction Equilibrium
learning_objective: Show why bidding one's valuation cannot be a Bayesian Nash equilibrium in a first-price auction.
preliminaries: In the first-price auction, bidder \(i\)'s payoff is \(\theta_i-b_i\) if she wins with bid \(b_i\), half that amount in a tie, and zero if she loses. Types are independently uniform on \([0,x]\).
question: Is it an equilibrium for both bidders to bid their own valuations? Explain.
solution: No. Suppose bidder \(j\) bids truthfully, \(b_j=\theta_j\). If bidder \(i\) of type \(\theta_i>0\) also bids truthfully, she wins when \(\theta_j<\theta_i\) but earns zero surplus whenever she wins because she pays \(b_i=\theta_i\). Her expected payoff is therefore zero. Instead, choose a small \(\varepsilon\in(0,\theta_i)\) and bid \(b_i=\theta_i-\varepsilon\). She wins with probability \((\theta_i-\varepsilon)/x>0\), and conditional on winning earns surplus \(\varepsilon>0\). Her expected payoff is \(\varepsilon(\theta_i-\varepsilon)/x>0\). This profitable deviation shows truthful bidding is not a Bayesian Nash equilibrium in a first-price auction.

source.original_question: Is it an equilibrium for players to bid their own valuations? Explain.
source.notes: Converted from the answer key's underbidding deviation.

validation.notes: A positive-surplus underbid is a profitable deviation for all positive types.

title: Counterexample to Truthfulness in a Two-Unit Second-Highest-Bid Auction
learning_objective: Disprove dominant-strategy truthfulness in a two-unit auction with second-highest-bid pricing.
preliminaries: There are two identical units and at least three unit-demand bidders. The two highest bidders each win one unit and each winner pays the second-highest bid. A bidder with value \(v_i\) who pays \(p\) receives payoff \(v_i-p\); a loser receives zero.
question: Is truthful bidding weakly dominant? If not, provide a counterexample.
solution: Truthful bidding is not weakly dominant. Take three bidders with values \(v_1=1/3\), \(v_2=1/2\), and \(v_3=1\). Suppose bidders 1 and 3 bid truthfully. If bidder 2 bids truthfully, the winning bids are \(1\) and \(1/2\), and the second-highest bid is \(1/2\). Bidder 2 wins and pays \(1/2\), giving payoff zero. If bidder 2 instead bids \(2/5\), the winning bids are \(1\) and \(2/5\), so bidder 2 still wins but pays \(2/5\). Her payoff is \(1/2-2/5=1/10>0\). Thus truthful bidding is not weakly dominant.

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the source counterexample.

validation.notes: Counterexample checked under the stated payment rule.

title: Truthfulness in a Two-Unit Third-Price Auction
learning_objective: Prove weak dominance of truthful bidding in a two-unit third-price auction.
preliminaries: There are two identical units and at least three unit-demand bidders. The two highest bidders win and each pays the third-highest bid. Fix bidder \(i\)'s value \(v_i\) and all opponents' bids. Let \(r\) be the second-highest bid among the opponents.
question: Is bidding \(b_i=v_i\) weakly dominant? Prove your answer.
solution: Yes. Bidder \(i\) wins exactly when her bid is high enough to enter the top two bids. The relevant threshold is \(r\), the second-highest opponent bid. If she wins, the third-highest overall bid is \(r\), so her payoff is \(v_i-r\). If she loses, her payoff is zero. If \(v_i>r\), truthful bidding wins and gives positive payoff, while losing would give zero and any other winning bid gives the same payoff. If \(v_i<r\), truthful bidding loses and gives zero, while any winning bid would require paying \(r>v_i\), giving negative payoff. If \(v_i=r\), winning and losing both give zero. Therefore truthful bidding is weakly optimal against every opponents' bid profile and is weakly dominant.

source.original_question: September 2024 (Retake) Question 2

validation.notes: Threshold proof checked.

title: Continuum of Equilibria in the Nash Demand Game
learning_objective: Show why simultaneous demands alone do not select the Nash bargaining solution.
preliminaries: Two players simultaneously demand payoffs \(x\in[0,1]\) and \(y\in[0,1]\). The feasible set is \(F=\{(x,y):x+y\le 1\}\). If \((x,y)\in F\), each player receives his demand. If \((x,y)\notin F\), both receive \(0\).
question: Show that every efficient feasible division \((x,y)\) with \(x+y=1\) is a pure Nash equilibrium outcome. Show also that no feasible division with \(x+y<1\) is a Nash equilibrium outcome. What does this imply about selection in the demand game?
solution:
Any efficient feasible pair \((x,y)\) with \(x+y=1\) is a Nash equilibrium outcome. If player 1 raises his demand, the demands become infeasible and he gets \(0\le x\); if he lowers his demand, he receives less. The same argument applies to player 2. Thus no player has a profitable deviation.

No inefficient feasible pair with \(x+y<1\) is a Nash equilibrium outcome, because either player can raise his demand slightly while preserving feasibility and obtain a higher payoff. Therefore the demand game supports a continuum of efficient equilibrium divisions and does not select a unique split. There is also a boundary artifact: the infeasible demand profile \((1,1)\) is a Nash equilibrium with disagreement payoff \((0,0)\), because no single player can restore feasibility and obtain a positive payoff. This artifact is one reason refinements or perturbed demand games are used when the goal is to implement a sharper bargaining prediction.

source.notes: Simplified linear feasible-set version of the Nash demand-game exercise.

validation.notes: Accepted: efficient demand equilibria and infeasible boundary artifact are correct.

title: Outside Option Principle in Alternating-Offers Bargaining
learning_objective: Determine when an outside option changes the equilibrium split.
preliminaries: Use the alternating-offers split-the-pie game with common discount factor \(\delta\in(0,1)\). Player 2 can opt out immediately after rejecting any offer and receive payoff \(b\in[0,1]\); player 1 then receives \(0\). If player 2 does not opt out, bargaining continues. Payoffs from agreement \((x,1-x)\) in period \(t\) are \(\delta^t x\) and \(\delta^t(1-x)\).
question: Find player 1's equilibrium offer in period 0 as a function of \(b\), focusing on stationary equilibria.
solution:
Let \(V_1\) be player 1's stationary payoff when player 1 proposes, and let \(V_2\) be player 2's stationary payoff when player 2 proposes. After rejecting player 1's offer, player 2 can either opt out for \(b\) or continue and become proposer next period, worth \(\delta V_2\). Thus player 1 must offer player 2 \(\max\{b,\delta V_2\}\). If the outside option is not binding, the usual Rubinstein equations give \(V_1=V_2=1/(1+\delta)\), so nonbinding requires \(b\le \delta/(1+\delta)\). Player 1's period-0 offer then gives player 2 \(\delta/(1+\delta)\).

If \(b>\delta/(1+\delta)\), the outside option binds at player 1's offer, so player 1 offers player 2 exactly \(b\) and keeps \(1-b\). The continuation values adjust consistently: \(V_1=1-b\) and, when player 2 proposes, player 1 must receive \(\delta V_1\), so \(V_2=1-\delta(1-b)\); the binding condition is equivalent to \(b\ge \delta V_2\). Therefore player 1's period-0 offer gives player 2 \[\max\left\{b,\frac{\delta}{1+\delta}\right\}\] and player 1 keeps the remainder. The outside option affects the split only when it exceeds player 2's continuation value without the option.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Simplified split-the-pie outside-option variant.

validation.notes: Accepted after adding binding-continuation calculation; period-0 offer formula is correct.

title: Bargaining with Risk of Breakdown
learning_objective: Derive the alternating-offers split when delay is costly because negotiations may break down.
preliminaries: Two players bargain over one unit. They do not discount time directly. After every rejected offer, bargaining breaks down with probability \(\alpha\in(0,1)\), giving both players payoff \(0\); with probability \(1-\alpha\), bargaining continues with the other player proposing. Players have linear utility over shares.
question: Find the stationary equilibrium offer made by player 1 in period 0.
solution: Let \(V_1\) be player 1's value when he is proposer and \(V_2\) player 2's value when she is proposer. If player 1's offer is rejected, player 2's expected continuation value is \((1-\alpha)V_2\), because breakdown gives \(0\). Therefore player 1 offers player 2 exactly \((1-\alpha)V_2\) and keeps \(V_1=1-(1-\alpha)V_2\). Symmetrically, \(V_2=1-(1-\alpha)V_1\). Solving yields \(V_1=V_2=1/(2-\alpha)\). Hence player 1 keeps \(1/(2-\alpha)\) and offers player 2 \((1-\alpha)/(2-\alpha)\). As \(\alpha\) rises, rejection becomes riskier, the responder's continuation value falls, and the proposer keeps a larger share.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Linear split-the-pie version of bargaining with stochastic breakdown.

validation.notes: Accepted: stochastic-breakdown continuation equations are correct.

title: Stationary Split in Alternating-Offers Bargaining
learning_objective: Derive the Rubinstein split from continuation values.
preliminaries: Two players bargain over one unit of surplus. In period 0 player 1 proposes a split \((x,1-x)\). If player 2 accepts, the game ends. If he rejects, period 1 begins with player 2 proposing, and so on alternating forever. Both players discount by \(\delta\in(0,1)\). Payoff from receiving share \(z\) in period \(t\) is \(\delta^t z\).
question: Derive the stationary subgame perfect equilibrium shares offered by each proposer.
solution: Let \(V_1\) be player 1's continuation payoff at a history where player 1 proposes, and \(V_2\) player 2's payoff at a history where player 2 proposes. By stationarity, when player 1 proposes he offers player 2 exactly the discounted value of rejecting and becoming proposer next period, namely \(\delta V_2\). Thus player 1 keeps \(1-\delta V_2\), so \(V_1=1-\delta V_2\). Similarly, when player 2 proposes, player 1 must receive \(\delta V_1\), so player 2 keeps \(1-\delta V_1\), and \(V_2=1-\delta V_1\). Solving the system gives \(V_1=V_2=1/(1+\delta)\). Therefore when player 1 proposes, he keeps \(1/(1+\delta)\) and offers player 2 \(\delta/(1+\delta)\). When player 2 proposes, she keeps \(1/(1+\delta)\) and offers player 1 \(\delta/(1+\delta)\). Acceptance rules accept any offer giving at least the responder's discounted continuation value. These strategies are sequentially optimal in every subgame by the same stationary calculation, so they form a stationary SPE.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Standalone derivation of the standard alternating-offers split.

validation.notes: Accepted: stationary continuation-value equations yield the Rubinstein split.

title: Asymmetric Nash Bargaining Weights
learning_objective: Compute the asymmetric Nash solution and compare it to the symmetric solution.
preliminaries: Use the feasible set \(S=\{(u_1,u_2):u_1,u_2\ge 0,u_1+u_2\le 1\}\) and disagreement point \((0,0)\). For bargaining weight \(\alpha\in(0,1)\), the asymmetric Nash solution maximizes \(u_1^\alpha u_2^{1-\alpha}\) over \(S\).
question: Find the asymmetric Nash solution and describe how it changes with \(\alpha\).
solution: The frontier binds at the optimum because the objective is strictly increasing in each utility when both are positive. Substitute \(u_2=1-u_1\) and maximize \(u_1^\alpha(1-u_1)^{1-\alpha}\) over \((0,1)\). The log objective is \(\alpha\log u_1+(1-\alpha)\log(1-u_1)\). The first-order condition is \(\alpha/u_1-(1-\alpha)/(1-u_1)=0\), which gives \(\alpha(1-u_1)=(1-\alpha)u_1\), hence \(u_1=\alpha\) and \(u_2=1-\alpha\). The log objective is strictly concave, so this maximizer is unique. As \(\alpha\) rises, player 1's utility share increases one-for-one, while player 2's decreases. At \(\alpha=1/2\), the symmetric Nash solution \((1/2,1/2)\) is recovered.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Computational asymmetric bargaining variant.

validation.notes: Accepted: weighted Nash product solution is correct.

title: Computing the Nash Bargaining Solution on a Linear Frontier
learning_objective: Compute the Nash bargaining solution and interpret disagreement payoffs.
preliminaries: A bargaining problem has feasible utility set \(S=\{(u_1,u_2):u_1\ge 0,u_2\ge 0,u_1+u_2\le 1\}\) and disagreement point \(d=(0,0)\). The Nash bargaining solution maximizes \((u_1-d_1)(u_2-d_2)\) over feasible utilities that weakly dominate \(d\).
question: Find the Nash bargaining solution. Then repeat for disagreement point \(d=(d_1,d_2)\) with \(d_1,d_2\ge 0\) and \(d_1+d_2<1\).
solution:
With \(d=(0,0)\), the product is \(u_1u_2\). At the optimum the resource constraint binds; otherwise both utilities can be raised. Set \(u_2=1-u_1\). Maximize \(u_1(1-u_1)\) over \([0,1]\). The first-order condition is \(1-2u_1=0\), so \(u_1=u_2=1/2\), and strict concavity gives the unique maximizer.

For general \(d\), write surplus above disagreement as \(z_i=u_i-d_i\ge 0\). The feasible frontier becomes \(z_1+z_2=1-d_1-d_2\). The Nash product is \(z_1z_2\), uniquely maximized by equal surplus shares: \(z_1=z_2=(1-d_1-d_2)/2\). Therefore \[u_1^*=d_1+\frac{1-d_1-d_2}{2},\qquad u_2^*=d_2+\frac{1-d_1-d_2}{2}.\] The solution splits the gains from agreement equally, not necessarily the final utility levels.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Basic computational variant for Nash bargaining solution coverage.

validation.notes: Accepted: Nash product optimization and disagreement comparative statics are correct.

title: Bayesian Battle of the Sexes with One-Sided Preference Uncertainty
learning_objective: Compute Bayesian Nash equilibria when one player's coordination preference is private information.
preliminaries: Players choose \(B\) or \(S\). Player 1 always prefers coordinated \(B\): payoffs for player 1 are \(2\) at \((B,B)\), \(1\) at \((S,S)\), and \(0\) otherwise. Player 2 has type \(b\) with probability \(p\in(0,1)\), with payoff \(2\) at \((B,B)\), \(1\) at \((S,S)\), and \(0\) otherwise; or type \(s\) with probability \(1-p\), with payoff \(1\) at \((B,B)\), \(2\) at \((S,S)\), and \(0\) otherwise. Player 2 observes his type; player 1 only knows \(p\).
question: Find all pure-strategy Bayesian Nash equilibria as a function of \(p\).
solution:
A pure strategy of player 2 is a pair \((a_b,a_s)\in\{B,S\}^2\). If player 1 chooses \(B\), type \(b\) of player 2 chooses \(B\), and type \(s\) compares payoff \(1\) from \(B\) with \(0\) from \(S\), so also chooses \(B\). Thus \((B;(B,B))\) is always a Bayesian Nash equilibrium if player 1 best responds to both types choosing \(B\), which he does because \(B\) gives \(2\) and \(S\) gives \(0\).

If player 1 chooses \(S\), both types of player 2 choose \(S\): type \(b\) gets \(1\) rather than \(0\), and type \(s\) gets \(2\) rather than \(0\). Then player 1 best responds to both types choosing \(S\) by choosing \(S\), which gives \(1\) rather than \(0\). Thus \((S;(S,S))\) is always an equilibrium.

Could there be a separating pure equilibrium with player 2 type \(b\) choosing \(B\) and type \(s\) choosing \(S\)? Player 1's expected payoff from \(B\) is \(2p\), and from \(S\) is \(1-p\). If player 1 chooses \(B\), type \(s\) would prefer \(B\) to \(S\); if player 1 chooses \(S\), type \(b\) would prefer \(S\) to \(B\). Hence no separating pure equilibrium exists. The reversed separation is also impossible because each type wants to coordinate given player 1's pure action. Therefore the only pure Bayesian Nash equilibria are the two pooling equilibria above, for every \(p\in(0,1)\).

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: One-sided uncertainty variant of the Bayesian BoS exercise.

validation.notes: Accepted after making p interior; pure BNE classification is correct.

title: Bayesian Entry with an Unknown Incumbent Cost
learning_objective: Derive a cutoff equilibrium in a two-type entry game.
preliminaries: An entrant chooses \(In\) or \(Out\). If \(Out\), payoffs are \((0,\pi_I)\). If \(In\), an incumbent privately knows cost type \(\theta\in\{L,H\}\), with \(\Pr(L)=p\). The incumbent chooses \(Fight\) or \(Accommodate\). Fighting gives entrant payoff \(-1\) and incumbent payoff \(1-c_\theta\). Accommodating gives entrant payoff \(2\) and incumbent payoff \(0\). Assume \(c_L<1<c_H\).
question: Find the incumbent's type-contingent optimal action after entry and the entrant's entry condition.
solution:
After entry, type \(L\) compares fighting payoff \(1-c_L>0\) with accommodating payoff \(0\), so it fights. Type \(H\) compares \(1-c_H<0\) with \(0\), so it accommodates. These choices are strictly optimal and therefore must be used in any Bayesian Nash equilibrium after entry.

Anticipating this, the entrant's expected payoff from entering is \(p(-1)+(1-p)2=2-3p\). The payoff from staying out is \(0\). Therefore the entrant chooses \(In\) if \(2-3p>0\), chooses \(Out\) if \(2-3p<0\), and is indifferent if \(p=2/3\). Thus for \(p<2/3\) the equilibrium has entry, low-cost incumbents fight, and high-cost incumbents accommodate; for \(p>2/3\) the entrant stays out with the same incumbent continuation strategies; for \(p=2/3\), any mixture by the entrant is optimal, with the incumbent types still playing their strict continuation best responses.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.locator: Chapter 2 Bayesian game definition and Chapter 12 chain-store perturbation topic
source.notes: Simple private-cost entry model inspired by Bayesian entry/reputation examples.

validation.notes: Accepted: incumbent type actions and entrant cutoff are correct.

title: No Trade Above the Lowest Prize in an Exchange Game
learning_objective: Prove a no-trade result in a symmetric private-information exchange game.
preliminaries: Each of two players privately observes a prize value from a finite set \(S=\{s_1<\cdots<s_K\}\subset[0,1]\). Values are independently drawn with full support. Simultaneously, each player says \(Y\) or \(N\) to exchanging prizes. Trade occurs iff both say \(Y\); otherwise each keeps his own prize. Players maximize expected prize value.
question: Show that in any Bayesian Nash equilibrium, a player with value above \(s_1\) says \(N\). Equivalently, only the lowest type can be willing to exchange.
solution:
Suppose, toward a contradiction, that some type above \(s_1\) says \(Y\) with positive probability in equilibrium. Let \(s_m>s_1\) be the highest value type of either player that says \(Y\) with positive probability. First note that some type below \(s_m\) must also say \(Y\) with positive probability. If no lower type did so, then the lowest type \(s_1\) could deviate to \(Y\): whenever trade occurred, it would receive \(s_m\) or some value above \(s_1\), and otherwise it would keep \(s_1\), so the deviation would be strictly profitable because the opponent says \(Y\) with positive probability.

Now consider type \(s_m\). Conditional on trade after saying \(Y\), the opponent's value is at most \(s_m\) by the definition of \(s_m\), and with positive probability it is strictly below \(s_m\) by the previous paragraph. Hence the expected value received conditional on trade is strictly less than \(s_m\). If type \(s_m\) says \(N\), it keeps \(s_m\) for sure. Since trade occurs with positive probability when it says \(Y\), saying \(Y\) is strictly worse than saying \(N\), contradicting equilibrium. Therefore no type above \(s_1\) says \(Y\) with positive probability. The lowest type may be willing to exchange, because conditional trade cannot give less than \(s_1\).

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Standalone no-trade proof inspired by the Bayesian exchange exercise.

validation.notes: Accepted after repairing highest-trading-type argument; no-trade claim is correct.

title: Truthful Bidding in a Bayesian Second-Price Auction
learning_objective: Show that type-contingent truthful bidding is a Bayesian Nash equilibrium because truthfulness is dominant type by type.
preliminaries: There are \(n\ge 2\) bidders. Bidder \(i\)'s value \(v_i\) is privately observed and lies in a finite set \(V\subset \mathbb R_+\). Values are independently drawn from a common distribution with full support on \(V\). Bidders submit nonnegative bids in a second-price auction with deterministic tie-breaking by lowest index.
question: Prove that the strategy \(b_i(v_i)=v_i\) for every bidder and every type is a Bayesian Nash equilibrium.
solution: Fix bidder \(i\), type \(v_i\), and any profile of bidding strategies of the other bidders. Conditional on any realized bids of the other bidders, let \(m\) be the highest other bid. The same pointwise dominance argument as in a complete-information second-price auction applies. If \(m\ge v_i\), winning at price \(m\) gives nonpositive payoff while losing gives \(0\); bidding \(v_i\) guarantees payoff at least as high as any alternative. If \(m<v_i\), bidding \(v_i\) wins and gives \(v_i-m>0\), while any alternative either wins at the same price or loses and gives \(0\). Thus for every possible realization of the other bids, bidding \(v_i\) is weakly optimal. Taking expectations over the other bidders' types preserves weak optimality. Therefore every type of every bidder is best responding when it bids its value, so the truthful type-contingent strategy profile is a Bayesian Nash equilibrium.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Standalone proof of the Bayesian version of truthful bidding.

validation.notes: Accepted after adding n>=2; typewise dominance implies BNE.

title: Why Always Playing \(S\) Is Not a PBE
learning_objective: Check a proposed PBE by testing the leader's deviation incentive.
preliminaries: Player 1 chooses \(S\) or \(C\). Player 2 observes a noisy signal and then chooses \(S\) or \(C\). Payoffs are \((S,S)=(5,2)\), \((S,C)=(3,1)\), \((C,S)=(6,3)\), and \((C,C)=(4,4)\). The proposed strategy profile is Player 1 chooses \(S\), and Player 2 chooses \(S\) after every signal.
question: For \(0<\varepsilon<1\), is the proposed strategy profile a Perfect Bayesian equilibrium?
solution: No. Given beliefs that put probability one on Player 1 having chosen \(S\), Player 2 is willing to choose \(S\) after either signal, because against \(S\), Player 2 gets \(2\) from \(S\) and \(1\) from \(C\). But Player 1's strategy is not sequentially optimal given Player 2's strategy. If Player 1 chooses \(S\), Player 2 chooses \(S\), and Player 1 gets \(5\). If Player 1 deviates to \(C\), Player 2 still chooses \(S\) after every signal, and Player 1 gets \(6\). Since \(6>5\), Player 1 has a profitable deviation. Therefore the proposed profile is not a PBE for any \(0<\varepsilon<1\).

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the proposed-PBE rejection.

validation.notes: Leader deviation check verified.

title: Bayesian Beliefs in a Noisy-Leader Game
learning_objective: Compute posterior beliefs after noisy signals and derive the follower's indifference condition.
preliminaries: Player 1 chooses \(S\) with probability \(p\) and \(C\) with probability \(1-p\). The signal satisfies \(\Pr(s\mid S)=\Pr(c\mid C)=1-\varepsilon\). Player 2's payoffs are \(2\) from \((S,S)\), \(1\) from \((S,C)\), \(3\) from \((C,S)\), and \(4\) from \((C,C)\).
question: Compute \(\Pr(S\mid s)\) and \(\Pr(S\mid c)\). What value of \(p\) makes Player 2 indifferent after signal \(c\)?
solution: Bayes' rule gives \[\Pr(S\mid s)=\frac{(1-\varepsilon)p}{(1-\varepsilon)p+\varepsilon(1-p)}\] and \[\Pr(S\mid c)=\frac{\varepsilon p}{\varepsilon p+(1-\varepsilon)(1-p)}.\] After signal \(c\), Player 2's expected payoff from \(S\) is \(2\Pr(S\mid c)+3\Pr(C\mid c)\), and from \(C\) is \(1\Pr(S\mid c)+4\Pr(C\mid c)\). Indifference requires \(\Pr(S\mid c)=\Pr(C\mid c)\), which is equivalent to \[\varepsilon p=(1-\varepsilon)(1-p).\] Solving gives \(p=1-\varepsilon\).

source.locator: May 2025 Question 4(e) beliefs and follower indifference

title: A Mixed PBE with Signal-Contingent Mixing
learning_objective: Construct a mixed PBE by combining follower and leader indifference conditions.
preliminaries: Use the noisy-leader game with payoffs \((S,S)=(5,2)\), \((S,C)=(3,1)\), \((C,S)=(6,3)\), \((C,C)=(4,4)\), and signals satisfying \(\Pr(s\mid S)=\Pr(c\mid C)=1-\varepsilon\). Assume \(0<\varepsilon<1/4\).
question: Find a PBE where Player 2 plays \(S\) after signal \(s\) and mixes after signal \(c\).
solution: From Player 2's indifference after signal \(c\), Player 1 must choose \(S\) with probability \(p=1-\varepsilon\). This also makes Player 2 willing to play \(S\) after signal \(s\), since \(p\ge \varepsilon\). Let Player 2 choose \(S\) after signal \(c\) with probability \(q\). Player 1's expected payoff from \(S\) is \[(1-\varepsilon)5+\varepsilon(5q+3(1-q))=5-2\varepsilon(1-q).\] Player 1's expected payoff from \(C\) is \[\varepsilon 6+(1-\varepsilon)(6q+4(1-q))=4+2q+2\varepsilon(1-q).\] Indifference requires \[5-2\varepsilon(1-q)=4+2q+2\varepsilon(1-q),\] hence \[q=\frac{1-4\varepsilon}{2-4\varepsilon}.\] This lies in \((0,1)\) for \(0<\varepsilon<1/4\). The PBE is: Player 1 plays \(S\) with probability \(1-\varepsilon\); Player 2 plays \(S\) after \(s\); after \(c\), Player 2 plays \(S\) with probability \((1-4\varepsilon)/(2-4\varepsilon)\). Beliefs are determined by Bayes' rule on path.

validation.notes: Leader and follower mixing conditions checked for \(0<\varepsilon<1/4\).

title: No PBE with a Mixing Leader and Pure Follower
learning_objective: Rule out a class of PBEs by showing the leader cannot be indifferent.
preliminaries: In the noisy-leader game, Player 1's payoff is \(5\) at \((S,S)\), \(3\) at \((S,C)\), \(6\) at \((C,S)\), and \(4\) at \((C,C)\). A mixed strategy by Player 1 requires indifference between \(S\) and \(C\).
question: Can there be a PBE in which Player 1 mixes and Player 2 uses a pure action after every signal?
solution: No. If Player 2's pure strategy prescribes \(S\) after both signals, then Player 1 gets \(5\) from \(S\) and \(6\) from \(C\), so Player 1 strictly prefers \(C\). If Player 2's pure strategy prescribes \(C\) after both signals, then Player 1 gets \(3\) from \(S\) and \(4\) from \(C\), so Player 1 strictly prefers \(C\). If Player 2's pure strategy depends on the signal, Player 1's expected payoff from \(C\) exceeds that from \(S\) state by state by one whenever the same follower action is induced. In every pure follower strategy, Player 1 cannot be indifferent between \(S\) and \(C\). Therefore Player 1 cannot mix in a PBE with pure follower behavior.

title: A Correlated Equilibrium in Chicken
learning_objective: Verify correlated-equilibrium incentive constraints in a coordination-with-conflict game.
preliminaries: Two drivers choose \(Go\) or \(Yield\). Payoffs are \((Go,Yield)=(3,0)\), \((Yield,Go)=(0,3)\), \((Yield,Yield)=(1,1)\), and \((Go,Go)=(-10,-10)\). A mediator privately recommends actions according to a distribution over action profiles. A correlated equilibrium requires each player to prefer following his recommendation conditional on receiving it.
question: Show that the mediator distribution assigning probability \(1/2\) to \((Go,Yield)\) and \(1/2\) to \((Yield,Go)\) is a correlated equilibrium.
solution: Consider player 1. If he is recommended \(Go\), he knows the recommended profile is \((Go,Yield)\), so following gives \(3\). Deviating to \(Yield\) gives \(1\). Thus following is optimal. If player 1 is recommended \(Yield\), he knows the recommended profile is \((Yield,Go)\), so following gives \(0\). Deviating to \(Go\) would produce \((Go,Go)\) and payoff \(-10\). Thus following is optimal. The same symmetric argument applies to player 2. Therefore no player wants to deviate from the recommendation after either possible signal, and the distribution is a correlated equilibrium. The mediator avoids crashes while alternating the efficient asymmetric outcomes.

title: Weak Dominance in a Second-Price Auction
learning_objective: Prove the dominant-strategy property of truthful bidding and distinguish dominance from efficiency of all equilibria.
preliminaries: There are \(n\ge 2\) bidders. Bidder \(i\) has value \(v_i>0\). Bids are nonnegative. The highest bidder wins, ties are broken in favor of the lowest index, and the winner pays the highest bid submitted by another bidder. A losing bidder's payoff is \(0\); a winning bidder's payoff is value minus price.
question: Show that bidding \(b_i=v_i\) is weakly dominant for bidder \(i\). Then construct, for \(n=2\) and \(v_1>v_2>0\), a Nash equilibrium in which bidder 2 wins the object.
solution:
Fix bidder \(i\) and let \(m=\max_{j\ne i} b_j\). If \(m\ge v_i\), bidding \(v_i\) makes bidder \(i\) lose or tie at a price at least \(v_i\), so his payoff is \(0\) or nonpositive. Any other bid that wins pays \(m\ge v_i\) and gives nonpositive payoff, while losing gives \(0\). Thus truth-telling is at least as good. If \(m<v_i\), bidding \(v_i\) wins and gives payoff \(v_i-m>0\). Any other bid that wins gives the same payoff \(v_i-m\), and any bid that loses gives \(0\), so truth-telling is again at least as good. Hence \(b_i=v_i\) is weakly dominant.

For the inefficient equilibrium with two bidders, choose bids \(b_1=0\) and \(b_2=B\) where \(B>v_1\). Bidder 2 wins and pays \(0\), so his payoff is \(v_2\). Bidder 1 can win only by bidding at least \(B\), which would give payoff at most \(v_1-B<0\), so he does not profitably deviate. Bidder 2 already obtains the object at price \(0\); lowering his bid below \(0\) is impossible and any nonnegative bid that still wins gives the same payoff, while a bid of \(0\) loses by tie-breaking and gives \(0<v_2\). Thus the profile is a Nash equilibrium, even though the lower-value bidder wins.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.locator: Example 18.1 and Exercise 18.3; solutions manual Exercise 18.3
source.notes: Inspired by the second-price auction dominance exercise; rewritten as a standalone proof with a two-bidder inefficient equilibrium.

validation.notes: Accepted: dominance proof and inefficient Nash equilibrium are correct under deterministic tie-breaking.

title: Random Tie-Breaking in a Second-Price Auction
learning_objective: Check whether the truthful-bidding dominance argument survives a change in the tie-breaking rule.
preliminaries: There are two bidders with values \(v_1>v_2>0\). Bids are nonnegative. The highest bidder wins; if bids are equal, each bidder wins with probability \(1/2\). The winner pays the other bidder's bid. Payoffs are expected value minus expected payment.
question: Is truthful bidding still weakly dominant for each bidder under random tie-breaking? Prove your answer. Then decide whether \((b_1,b_2)=(0,B)\) with \(B>v_1\) is a Nash equilibrium.
solution:
Truthful bidding remains weakly dominant. Fix bidder \(i\) and let the other bid be \(m\). If \(m<v_i\), bidding \(v_i\) wins for sure and gives \(v_i-m>0\). Any higher bid gives the same payoff, any lower winning bid gives the same payoff, a bid equal to \(m\) gives \((v_i-m)/2\), and a losing bid gives \(0\). Thus no bid does better. If \(m>v_i\), bidding \(v_i\) loses and gives \(0\). Winning requires a bid above \(m\) and gives payoff \(v_i-m<0\); tying at \(m\) gives \((v_i-m)/2<0\); losing gives \(0\). If \(m=v_i\), bidding \(v_i\) gives \(0\), as does losing, while winning above \(m\) also gives \(0\). Hence truth-telling is weakly dominant.

The profile \((0,B)\) with \(B>v_1\) is still a Nash equilibrium. Bidder 2 wins and pays \(0\), obtaining \(v_2\). Bidder 1 would have to bid above \(B\), or tie at \(B\), to get the object; these deviations give negative expected payoff because the price is \(B>v_1\). Bidder 2 cannot improve on payoff \(v_2\), the maximum possible surplus when the price is nonnegative. Thus neither player has a profitable deviation.

title: Stackelberg Equilibrium by Backward Induction
learning_objective: Solve the observed-action sequential version of a two-action game.
preliminaries: Use the payoff matrix \((S,S)=(5,2)\), \((S,C)=(3,1)\), \((C,S)=(6,3)\), and \((C,C)=(4,4)\). Player 1 moves first; Player 2 observes Player 1's action and then chooses \(S\) or \(C\).
question: Find the subgame-perfect equilibrium of the sequential game.
solution: Use backward induction. If Player 1 chooses \(S\), Player 2 gets \(2\) from \(S\) and \(1\) from \(C\), so Player 2 chooses \(S\). If Player 1 chooses \(C\), Player 2 gets \(3\) from \(S\) and \(4\) from \(C\), so Player 2 chooses \(C\). Anticipating this, Player 1 compares \(5\) from choosing \(S\) with \(4\) from choosing \(C\). Player 1 chooses \(S\). The unique subgame-perfect equilibrium is: Player 1 chooses \(S\); Player 2 chooses \(S\) after \(S\) and \(C\) after \(C\).

source.original_question: May 2025 Question 4
source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the Stackelberg solution.

validation.notes: Backward induction verified.

title: Backward Induction in an Entry Deterrence Game
learning_objective: Compute a subgame perfect equilibrium and contrast it with noncredible threats.
preliminaries: An entrant first chooses \(In\) or \(Out\). If \(Out\), payoffs are \((0,2)\) for entrant and incumbent. If \(In\), the incumbent chooses \(Fight\) or \(Accommodate\). Payoffs after \(Fight\) are \((-1,-1)\); payoffs after \(Accommodate\) are \((1,1)\).
question: Find all Nash equilibria of the strategic form and identify which are subgame perfect.
solution:
The entrant's pure strategies are \(In\) and \(Out\). The incumbent's pure strategies are \(Fight\) and \(Accommodate\), specifying what to do after entry. The strategic-form payoffs are: \((Out,Fight)=(0,2)\), \((Out,Accommodate)=(0,2)\), \((In,Fight)=(-1,-1)\), and \((In,Accommodate)=(1,1)\). Best responses: if the incumbent plans \(Fight\), the entrant prefers \(Out\); if the incumbent plans \(Accommodate\), the entrant prefers \(In\). If the entrant chooses \(Out\), both incumbent strategies are best responses because the incumbent does not move. If the entrant chooses \(In\), the incumbent prefers \(Accommodate\). Thus the pure Nash equilibria are \((Out,Fight)\) and \((In,Accommodate)\).

Subgame perfection requires optimal play in the subgame after entry. In that subgame, the incumbent strictly prefers \(Accommodate\) to \(Fight\), because \(1>-1\). Therefore any subgame perfect equilibrium must prescribe \(Accommodate\). Given this, the entrant chooses \(In\). The unique subgame perfect equilibrium is \((In,Accommodate)\). The Nash equilibrium \((Out,Fight)\) relies on a noncredible threat to fight after entry.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.locator: Chapter 6 subgame perfect equilibrium and chain-store motivation
source.notes: Standalone entry-deterrence exercise inspired by the source's credibility examples.

validation.notes: Accepted: Nash equilibria and SPE refinement are correctly distinguished.

title: Backward Induction in a Four-Node Centipede Game
learning_objective: Use backward induction to solve a finite centipede game and identify the source of immediate stopping.
preliminaries: Player 1 moves first and chooses \(S\) or \(C\). If he chooses \(S\), payoffs are \((1,0)\). If \(C\), player 2 chooses \(S\) or \(C\). If player 2 chooses \(S\), payoffs are \((0,2)\). If \(C\), player 1 chooses again. At player 1's second node, \(S\) gives \((3,1)\), while \(C\) lets player 2 move one last time. At the final node, player 2's \(S\) gives \((2,4)\), and \(C\) gives \((3,3)\).
question: Find the unique subgame perfect equilibrium by backward induction. What is the equilibrium outcome?
solution: Start at the final node. Player 2 compares \(S\), which gives payoff \(4\), with \(C\), which gives payoff \(3\), so player 2 chooses \(S\). Move back to player 1's second node. If player 1 chooses \(S\), he gets \(3\); if he chooses \(C\), the final-node solution gives outcome \((2,4)\), so player 1 gets \(2\). Thus player 1 chooses \(S\). Move back to player 2's first node. If player 2 chooses \(S\), she gets \(2\); if she chooses \(C\), player 1's next-node solution gives \((3,1)\), so player 2 gets \(1\). Thus player 2 chooses \(S\). Finally, at the initial node, player 1 compares stopping for payoff \(1\) with continuing, which leads to \((0,2)\) and gives him \(0\). Thus player 1 chooses \(S\). Because every comparison is strict, the backward-induction strategy profile is the unique subgame perfect equilibrium. The equilibrium outcome is immediate stopping with payoffs \((1,0)\).

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.locator: Chapter 6 centipede game discussion near Exercise 108.1
source.notes: Finite centipede variant rewritten as a fully specified backward-induction exercise.

validation.notes: Accepted: strict backward-induction solution is correct.

title: Unreached Subgames Still Matter for SPE
learning_objective: Explain why subgame perfection restricts behavior after histories that are not reached in equilibrium.
preliminaries: Player 1 chooses \(L\) or \(R\). If \(L\), payoffs are \((2,2)\). If \(R\), player 2 chooses \(A\) or \(B\), yielding \((3,0)\) after \(A\) and \((0,1)\) after \(B\).
question: Find all Nash equilibria of the strategic form, including mixed equilibria, and identify which are subgame perfect. Explain why an unreached subgame can still restrict equilibrium behavior.
solution:
Player 2's strategy specifies \(A\) or \(B\) after \(R\). The strategic-form outcomes are \((L,A)=(2,2)\), \((L,B)=(2,2)\), \((R,A)=(3,0)\), and \((R,B)=(0,1)\). Let player 1 choose \(R\) with probability \(r\), and let player 2 choose \(A\) with probability \(q\). Player 1's payoff from \(L\) is \(2\), and from \(R\) is \(3q\). Player 2's payoff from \(A\) is \(2(1-r)\), and from \(B\) is \(2(1-r)+r\). Thus if \(r>0\), player 2 strictly prefers \(B\). But if player 2 chooses \(B\), player 1 strictly prefers \(L\), so \(r>0\) is impossible in equilibrium. Hence \(r=0\). Given \(r=0\), player 2 is indifferent between \(A\) and \(B\), while player 1 prefers \(L\) iff \(2\ge 3q\), i.e. \(q\le 2/3\). Therefore the Nash equilibria are exactly: player 1 chooses \(L\), and player 2 chooses \(A\) with any probability \(q\in[0,2/3]\).

Subgame perfection requires optimal play in the proper subgame after \(R\). In that subgame, player 2 strictly prefers \(B\) to \(A\), because \(1>0\). Therefore the unique subgame perfect equilibrium is the Nash equilibrium with \(q=0\): player 1 chooses \(L\) and player 2 chooses \(B\) after \(R\). The other Nash equilibria rely on nonoptimal behavior in a subgame that is not reached on the equilibrium path.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Original small game focused on unreached-subgame logic.

validation.notes: Accepted: mixed Nash set and unreached-subgame SPE restriction are correct.

title: Weak Dominance Elimination Can Conflict with Backward Induction
learning_objective: Construct an example where an order of weakly dominated strategy elimination removes the subgame-perfect outcome.
preliminaries: Consider the strategic form with player 1 rows \(AE,AF,BE,BF\) and player 2 columns \(C,D\). Payoffs are \((AE,C)=(2,0)\), \((AE,D)=(1,1)\), \((AF,C)=(0,2)\), \((AF,D)=(1,1)\), \((BE,C)=(3,3)\), \((BE,D)=(3,3)\), \((BF,C)=(3,3)\), and \((BF,D)=(3,3)\). This is the reduced strategic form of a finite perfect-information game whose unique backward-induction outcome is represented by \((BE,D)\).
question: Show that there is an order of iterated elimination of weakly dominated strategies that eliminates the strategy profile \((BE,D)\), even though it corresponds to the backward-induction outcome.
solution: First compare player 1's rows \(AE\) and \(BE\). Against \(C\), \(BE\) gives \(3>2\); against \(D\), \(BE\) gives \(3>1\). Thus \(AE\) is strictly, hence weakly, dominated by \(BE\), so eliminate \(AE\). In the remaining game, compare player 2's columns. Against \(AF\), \(C\) gives player 2 payoff \(2>1\) from \(D\); against \(BE\) and \(BF\), both columns give \(3\). Thus \(D\) is weakly dominated by \(C\) in the reduced game, so eliminate \(D\). Once \(D\) is eliminated, the profile \((BE,D)\) is no longer available. This demonstrates that weak dominance is order-dependent and can delete a strategy profile associated with the subgame-perfect outcome. The conflict does not arise for the particular backward-induction-guided elimination order, but arbitrary weak-dominance elimination is not a reliable substitute for subgame perfection.

validation.notes: Accepted: weak-dominance deletion order correctly removes the specified BI profile.

title: Subgame-Perfect Implementation in Solomon's Predicament
learning_objective: Verify that a simple extensive game can implement truthful allocation with fines.
preliminaries: An object belongs either to player 1 or player 2. The legitimate owner values it at \(v_H\), the nonowner at \(v_L\), with \(v_H>M>v_L>0\). First player 1 says \(Mine\) or \(His\). If \(His\), player 2 gets the object and no fines. If \(Mine\), player 2 says \(Mine\) or \(Hers\). If \(Hers\), player 1 gets the object and no fines. If \(Mine\), player 2 gets the object, player 2 pays fine \(M\), and player 1 pays a small fine \(\varepsilon>0\).
question: Show that this game form gives the object to the legitimate owner in the unique subgame perfect equilibrium for each ownership state, assuming \(\varepsilon\) is small.
solution:
If player 1 is the owner, consider player 2's decision after player 1 says \(Mine\). If player 2 says \(Hers\), player 2 gets \(0\). If he says \(Mine\), he gets the object as nonowner and pays \(M\), for payoff \(v_L-M<0\). Thus he says \(Hers\). Anticipating this, player 1 gets \(v_H\) by saying \(Mine\), and gets \(0\) by saying \(His\), so she says \(Mine\). The object goes to player 1.

If player 2 is the owner, then after \(Mine\), player 2 compares saying \(Hers\), which gives \(0\), with saying \(Mine\), which gives \(v_H-M>0\). Thus he says \(Mine\). Anticipating this, player 1, who is not the owner, gets \(-\varepsilon\) by saying \(Mine\) and \(0\) by saying \(His\), so she says \(His\). The object goes to player 2. Because the continuation choices are strict and player 1's initial choices are strict when \(\varepsilon>0\), the SPE is unique in each ownership state.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Simplified SPE-implementation verification based on the Solomon example.

validation.notes: Accepted: backward-induction implementation argument is correct.

title: Monotonicity as a Necessary Condition for Nash Implementation
learning_objective: Prove the standard monotonicity necessity argument for Nash implementation.
preliminaries: A choice rule \(f\) assigns a nonempty set of outcomes to each preference profile. A game form \(g:S\to C\) Nash-implements \(f\) if, at every preference profile \(R\), the set of Nash equilibrium outcomes equals \(f(R)\). A choice rule is monotonic if whenever \(x\in f(R)\) and no agent lowers \(x\) relative to any alternative when preferences change from \(R\) to \(R'\), then \(x\in f(R')\). Formally, for all agents \(i\) and alternatives \(y\), \(x R_i y\Rightarrow x R'_i y\).
question: Show that any Nash-implementable choice rule is monotonic.
solution: Suppose \(g\) Nash-implements \(f\). Let \(x\in f(R)\), and let \(s^*\) be a Nash equilibrium of the game form under profile \(R\) with \(g(s^*)=x\). Consider another profile \(R'\) such that for every agent \(i\) and outcome \(y\), \(x R_i y\) implies \(x R'_i y\). We claim \(s^*\) remains a Nash equilibrium under \(R'\). For any unilateral deviation \(s_i\), equilibrium under \(R\) implies \(x R_i g(s_i,s^*_{-i})\). By the no-lowering assumption, \(x R'_i g(s_i,s^*_{-i})\). Thus no agent has a profitable deviation from \(s^*\) under \(R'\). Therefore \(s^*\) is a Nash equilibrium at \(R'\), so its outcome \(x\) must belong to the implemented equilibrium outcome set, which equals \(f(R')\). Hence \(f\) is monotonic.

source.notes: Standalone necessity proof for monotonicity in Nash implementation.

title: Best Replies in an n-Firm Cournot Market
learning_objective: Derive each firm's best response under linear inverse demand with a shutdown boundary.
preliminaries: There are \(n\) firms. Firm \(i\) chooses \(q_i\ge 0\). Let \(Q_{-i}=\sum_{j\ne i}q_j\). Price is \(p=\max\{1-q_i-Q_{-i},0\}\), and marginal cost is \(c\in(0,1)\). Firm \(i\)'s profit is \((p-c)q_i\).
question: Characterize firm \(i\)'s best reply function.
solution: If \(Q_{-i}\ge 1-c\), then for every \(q_i>0\) the margin is weakly negative: when price is positive, \(1-q_i-Q_{-i}-c<0\), and when price is zero, the margin is \(-c<0\). Thus the best response is \(q_i=0\). If \(Q_{-i}<1-c\), the relevant positive-price profit for quantities below the choke point is \[\pi_i(q_i,Q_{-i})=(1-q_i-Q_{-i}-c)q_i.\] This is strictly concave in \(q_i\), and the first-order condition is \[1-Q_{-i}-c-2q_i=0.\] Hence \[BR_i(Q_{-i})=\max\left\{0,\frac{1-c-Q_{-i}}{2}\right\}.\]

source.original_question: In an n-firm Cournot market, firms choose quantities, price is max{1-sum q_i,0}, marginal cost is c in (0,1), and profit is (p-c)q_i. Characterize best replies.
source.notes: Converted from the source solution and independently checked at the boundary where rivals' output makes positive production unprofitable.

validation.notes: The best reply combines the concave interior solution with the correct zero-output boundary.

title: Uniqueness of Pure Cournot Equilibrium
learning_objective: Prove that the pure-strategy Cournot equilibrium is unique for two or more firms.
preliminaries: In the Cournot game, \(BR_i(Q_{-i})=\max\{0,(1-c-Q_{-i})/2\}\), with \(c\in(0,1)\).
question: Is the pure-strategy Nash equilibrium unique for \(n=2\)? Is it unique for \(n\ge 3\)?
solution: The pure-strategy equilibrium is unique for every \(n\ge 2\). First, no firm can produce zero in equilibrium. If firm \(i\) produced zero while some active set of \(m\le n-1\) other firms produced total output \(Q_{-i}<1-c\), then firm \(i\)'s best response would be positive. If instead \(Q_{-i}\ge 1-c\), active firms would earn nonpositive profit and could weakly improve by reducing output; at least one active firm facing large rival output would not satisfy the positive best-response condition. More directly, for every active firm \(i\), the first-order condition is \[q_i=1-c-Q,\] where \(Q=\sum_j q_j\). Hence all active firms have the same output. If \(m\) firms are active, each active output is \((1-c)/(m+1)\), and total active output is \(m(1-c)/(m+1)<1-c\). Any inactive firm would then have positive best response \((1-c-Q)/2>0\), a contradiction unless \(m=n\). Therefore all firms are active and symmetric, so \[q_i=\frac{1-c}{n+1}\quad\text{for every }i.\] This is the equilibrium already verified, so no other pure-strategy Nash equilibrium exists for \(n=2\) or for \(n\ge 3\).

source.original_question: In the same Cournot game, ask whether pure-strategy Nash equilibrium is unique for n=2 and for n>=3.
source.notes: Converted from the source solution and strengthened with an active-set proof of uniqueness.

validation.notes: The active-set argument rules out asymmetric and inactive-firm equilibria.

title: Symmetric Cournot Nash Equilibrium
learning_objective: Use best replies to compute the symmetric pure-strategy Cournot equilibrium.
preliminaries: Use the \(n\)-firm Cournot game with \(BR_i(Q_{-i})=\max\{0,(1-c-Q_{-i})/2\}\), where \(c\in(0,1)\).
question: Find a pure-strategy Nash equilibrium of the Cournot game.
solution: Look for a symmetric equilibrium with \(q_i=q\) for all \(i\). Since \(c\in(0,1)\), the candidate will have positive output. The best-response equation is \[q=\frac{1-c-(n-1)q}{2}.\] Therefore \[(n+1)q=1-c,\qquad q^*=\frac{1-c}{n+1}.\] This quantity is positive and gives total output \(n(1-c)/(n+1)<1-c<1\), so the positive-price branch is valid. Substituting \(Q_{-i}=(n-1)q^*\) into the best reply gives \[BR_i(Q_{-i})=\frac{1-c-(n-1)(1-c)/(n+1)}{2}=\frac{1-c}{n+1}=q^*.\] Thus \((q^*,\ldots,q^*)\) is a pure-strategy Nash equilibrium.

source.original_question: For the same n-firm Cournot market with linear demand and constant marginal cost, find a pure-strategy Nash equilibrium.
source.notes: Converted from the source solution and independently checked by substitution into the best-response function.

validation.notes: The symmetric quantity is positive and is exactly each firm's best response to rivals' quantities.

title: Direct Revelation from a Dominant-Strategy Mechanism
learning_objective: Prove the dominant-strategy revelation principle in a finite environment.
preliminaries: There are finite type sets \(\Theta_i\). An indirect mechanism has message sets \(M_i\), outcome function \(g:M\to X\), and dominant-strategy equilibrium message functions \(m_i^*:\Theta_i\to M_i\). It implements social choice function \(f\) if \(g(m^*(\theta))=f(\theta)\) for every type profile \(\theta\).
question: Construct a direct mechanism in which truthful reporting is a dominant strategy and the outcome is \(f(\theta)\). Prove truthfulness.
solution:
Define the direct mechanism with report spaces \(\Theta_i\). Given reports \(\hat\theta\), it chooses \[\hat g(\hat\theta)=g(m_1^*(\hat\theta_1),\ldots,m_n^*(\hat\theta_n)).\] If all agents report truthfully, the outcome is \(g(m^*(\theta))=f(\theta)\), so the direct mechanism implements the same social choice function.

Fix agent \(i\), true type \(\theta_i\), other reports \(\hat\theta_{-i}\), and a possible misreport \(\hat\theta_i\). In the indirect mechanism, dominance of \(m_i^*(\theta_i)\) means agent \(i\) weakly prefers the outcome from message \(m_i^*(\theta_i)\) to the outcome from any other message, holding opponents' messages fixed. In particular, holding opponents' messages at \(m_{-i}^*(\hat\theta_{-i})\), he weakly prefers \[g(m_i^*(\theta_i),m_{-i}^*(\hat\theta_{-i}))\] to \[g(m_i^*(\hat\theta_i),m_{-i}^*(\hat\theta_{-i})).\] These are exactly the direct-mechanism outcomes from truthful reporting and from misreport \(\hat\theta_i\). Thus truthful reporting is dominant in the direct mechanism.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.locator: Chapter 10 implementation theory; truthful implementation and DSE implementation references
source.notes: General revelation-principle proof added as mechanism-design coverage.

validation.notes: Accepted after fixing escaped theta notation; revelation-principle proof is correct.

title: Groves Mechanism for a Public Project
learning_objective: Prove truthful reporting is dominant in a pivotal public-project mechanism.
preliminaries:
There are \(n\) agents. Agent \(i\)'s value for a public project is \(\theta_i\in[0,1]\). The project costs \(c>0\). If built, agent \(i\)'s gross payoff is \(\theta_i\); if not built, it is \(0\). A mechanism asks reports \(\hat\theta_i\), builds iff \(\sum_i\hat\theta_i\ge c\), and charges Clarke pivot tax \(t_i=\max\{0,c-\sum_{j
e i}\hat	heta_j\}\) if the project is built and \(t_i=\max\{0,\sum_{j
e i}\hat	heta_j-c\}\) if the project is not built. Utility is gross payoff minus tax.
question: Show that truthful reporting is a weakly dominant strategy for every agent.
solution:
Fix agent \(i\) and the other reports. Let \(S=\sum_{j
e i}\hat	heta_j\). Agent \(i\)'s report matters only if it changes the build decision. If the project is built, his utility is \(\theta_i-\max\{0,c-S\}\). If it is not built, his utility is \(-\max\{0,S-c\}\). When \(S\ge c\), the project is built regardless of \(i\)'s report, and the tax is \(0\), so all reports give the same payoff \(\theta_i\). When \(S+	heta_i\ge c>S\), truthful reporting builds and gives \(\theta_i-(c-S)=S+	heta_i-c\ge 0\); any report that prevents building gives \(0\), so truth is weakly better. When \(S+	heta_i<c\), truthful reporting does not build and gives \(0\); any report that causes building gives \(\theta_i-(c-S)<0\), so truth is better. Thus truthful reporting weakly dominates every misreport for every profile of other reports. This is the Groves logic: the agent faces exactly the social opportunity cost of changing the decision.

source.locator: Groves mechanism references around Chapter 10; OCR lines on dominant-strategy demand revelation
source.notes: Public-project VCG variant inspired by Groves mechanism exercises.
