title: Pure and Mixed Equilibria in Battle of the Sexes
learning_objective: Compute all equilibria in a two-by-two coordination game with conflicting preferences.
preliminaries: Two players choose \(B\) or \(S\). Payoffs are \((2,1)\) at \((B,B)\), \((1,2)\) at \((S,S)\), and \((0,0)\) at mismatched profiles.
question: Find all pure and mixed Nash equilibria.
solution:
The pure equilibria are \((B,B)\) and \((S,S)\), since at either coordinated outcome neither player wants to deviate, while at a mismatch either player can switch to coordinate and improve.

Let player 1 choose \(B\) with probability \(p\) and player 2 choose \(B\) with probability \(q\). Player 1's expected payoff from \(B\) is \(2q\), and from \(S\) is \(1-q\). In a mixed equilibrium player 1 is indifferent, so \(2q=1-q\), giving \(q=1/3\). Player 2's expected payoff from \(B\) is \(p\), and from \(S\) is \(2(1-p)\). Indifference gives \(p=2(1-p)\), so \(p=2/3\). Therefore the unique fully mixed equilibrium is: player 1 chooses \(B\) with probability \(2/3\), and player 2 chooses \(B\) with probability \(1/3\). Together with the two pure equilibria, these are all equilibria because any nondegenerate mixed equilibrium in a two-action game requires both players' indifference conditions, which have the unique solution above.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.locator: Example 15.3/16.1 and Chapter 3 mixed strategy calculation around Example 34.1
source.notes: Canonical coordination-game mixed equilibrium inspired by the textbook BoS discussion.

validation.notes: Accepted: two pure equilibria and unique fully mixed equilibrium are correctly derived.

title: Inspection Game with Endogenous Audit Probability
learning_objective: Derive mixed equilibrium probabilities in an enforcement game.
preliminaries: A taxpayer chooses \(E\) (evade) or \(H\) (honest). An agency chooses \(A\) (audit) or \(N\) (not audit). If honest, the taxpayer gets \(0\) regardless of audit. If he evades and is not audited, he gets gain \(g>0\). If he evades and is audited, he pays penalty and gets \(-f\), where \(f>0\). The agency pays audit cost \(c>0\). It gets benefit \(r>0\) from catching evasion, and gets \(0\) from no audit. Assume \(r>c\).
question: Find the mixed-strategy Nash equilibrium in which both players mix.
solution: Let \(p\) be the probability of evasion and \(q\) the probability of audit. The taxpayer's payoff from evading is \(q(-f)+(1-q)g=g-q(g+f)\); from honesty it is \(0\). Indifference requires \(g-q(g+f)=0\), so \(q=g/(g+f)\). The agency's payoff from auditing is \(p(r-c)+(1-p)(-c)=pr-c\); from not auditing it is \(0\). Indifference requires \(pr-c=0\), so \(p=c/r\). Since \(r>c\), \(p\in(0,1)\), and \(q\in(0,1)\) automatically. Thus the unique fully mixed equilibrium is evasion with probability \(c/r\) and audit with probability \(g/(g+f)\). At these probabilities both players are indifferent between their two pure actions, so any pure action in support is a best response; actions outside the support do not exist. Hence the profile is a mixed Nash equilibrium.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.locator: Chapter 3 discussion of mixed strategy interpretations and support indifference Lemma 33.2
source.notes: Application variant inspired by the inspection/audit interpretation of mixed equilibrium.

validation.notes: Accepted: mixed audit and evasion probabilities follow from support indifference.

title: Mixed Equilibrium in Matching Pennies
learning_objective: Compute a unique mixed-strategy Nash equilibrium by support indifference.
preliminaries: Players 1 and 2 each choose \(H\) or \(T\). Player 1 gets \(1\) if the actions match and \(-1\) otherwise. Player 2's payoff is the negative of player 1's payoff.
question: Find all mixed-strategy Nash equilibria.
solution: There is no pure Nash equilibrium: at any matching profile player 2 wants to switch, and at any nonmatching profile player 1 wants to switch. Let \(p\) be the probability that player 1 chooses \(H\), and \(q\) the probability that player 2 chooses \(H\). Player 1's expected payoff from \(H\) is \(q-(1-q)=2q-1\); from \(T\) it is \((1-q)-q=1-2q\). Player 1 mixes only if \(2q-1=1-2q\), so \(q=1/2\). Player 2's expected payoff from \(H\) is \(-p+(1-p)=1-2p\); from \(T\) it is \(p-(1-p)=2p-1\). Player 2 mixes only if \(p=1/2\). If either player used a pure strategy, the opponent would have a strict best response and the pure player would then want to switch, so no other mixed equilibrium exists. The unique mixed equilibrium is \(p=q=1/2\), with value \(0\).

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.locator: Chapter 2 Matching Pennies example and Chapter 3 mixed strategy definitions
source.notes: Inspired by the canonical no-pure-equilibrium mixed-strategy example.

validation.notes: Accepted: support-indifference calculation and uniqueness are correct.

title: Eliminating Unsupported Actions in a Three-Action Mixed Equilibrium
learning_objective: Use support conditions to rule out candidate supports in a finite game.
preliminaries: Consider the symmetric two-player game with actions \(A,B,C\). Payoffs to the row player are given by \[\begin{array}{c|ccc} & A & B & C\\ \hline A&2&0&0\\ B&0&2&0\\ C&1&1&1\end{array}\] and the column player's payoff matrix is the transpose, so the game is symmetric.
question: Find all symmetric mixed-strategy Nash equilibria.
solution: Let the opponent use probabilities \((x,y,z)\) on \((A,B,C)\). The expected payoffs from \(A,B,C\) are \(2x\), \(2y\), and \(1\). In a symmetric equilibrium the support of \((x,y,z)\) must contain only actions with maximal payoff against \((x,y,z)\). If \(C\) is in support, then its payoff \(1\) must be maximal, and any other action in support must also yield \(1\). Thus \(A\) in support requires \(x=1/2\), and \(B\) in support requires \(y=1/2\). If all three are in support, then \(x=y=1/2\), leaving \(z=0\), contradiction. If support is \(\{A,C\}\), then \(x=1/2\), \(y=0\), \(z=1/2\), but \(B\)'s payoff is \(0<1\), so this works. Similarly support \(\{B,C\}\) gives \((0,1/2,1/2)\). If support is \(\{C\}\), then \(C\)'s payoff is \(1\), while \(A\) and \(B\) get \(0\), so pure \(C\) is an equilibrium. If support excludes \(C\), then in a symmetric equilibrium with support \(\{A,B\}\) we need \(2x=2y\), so \(x=y=1/2\), giving payoff \(1\), equal to \(C\)'s payoff; since \(C\) is also a best response, leaving it out is allowed. Pure \(A\) and pure \(B\) are also equilibria because the chosen action yields payoff \(2\), while the others yield \(0\) or \(1\). Therefore the symmetric equilibria are \((1,0,0)\), \((0,1,0)\), \((0,0,1)\), \((1/2,1/2,0)\), \((1/2,0,1/2)\), and \((0,1/2,1/2)\).

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Original finite-game support-enumeration variant anchored to the mixed-equilibrium support lemma.

validation.notes: Accepted: symmetric support enumeration is complete.

title: Why Continuity Matters for Pure Equilibrium Existence
learning_objective: Use a simple discontinuous payoff game to show why compact action sets alone do not guarantee a pure Nash equilibrium.
preliminaries: Two players choose numbers \(x_i\in[0,1]\). Payoffs are \(u_i(x_i,x_j)=1\{x_i>x_j\}-x_i\): a player gets prize value \(1\) for choosing a strictly higher number than the opponent and pays cost equal to his own number. Ties give no prize.
question: Show that this compact-action game has no pure Nash equilibrium. Identify which common existence assumption fails.
solution: Suppose \((x_1,x_2)\) is a pure Nash equilibrium. If \(x_i>x_j\), player \(i\) wins and pays \(x_i\). He can lower his choice slightly while still remaining above \(x_j\), preserving the prize and reducing the cost, so this cannot be an equilibrium. If \(x_i=x_j<1\), either player can choose a number slightly above the common value and obtain payoff close to \(1-x_i\), which is strictly higher than the tie payoff \(-x_i\). If \(x_1=x_2=1\), either player can deviate to \(0\) and improve from \(-1\) to \(0\). If \(x_i<x_j\), the losing player gets \(-x_i\). If \(x_j<1\), he can choose slightly above \(x_j\) and get positive payoff for a small enough increase when \(x_j<1\); if \(x_j=1\), then the winner is in the already-ruled-out case of choosing strictly above the opponent and can lower slightly. Hence no profile is a Nash equilibrium. The compactness of \([0,1]\) is not enough because payoff functions are discontinuous at ties and at the strict-inequality boundary \(x_i=x_j\). Standard pure-equilibrium existence theorems require continuity, together with convexity and quasi-concavity-type conditions.

source.locator: Opening discussion on mixed-strategy existence and discontinuous payoffs
source.notes: Counterexample variant inspired by the OCR discussion of discontinuities and existence.

validation.notes: Accepted: discontinuity counterexample has no pure Nash equilibrium.

title: Cournot Duopoly with Linear Demand
learning_objective: Derive and verify a unique Nash equilibrium in a continuous-action game.
preliminaries: Two firms choose quantities \(q_i\ge 0\). Inverse demand is \(P(Q)=a-Q\) when \(Q=q_1+q_2\le a\), and \(P(Q)=0\) otherwise. Each firm has constant marginal cost \(c\), where \(a>c>0\). Firm \(i\)'s profit is \(q_i(P(Q)-c)\).
question: Find the unique Nash equilibrium quantities and profits.
solution: For fixed \(q_j\), firm \(i\)'s profit on the relevant region is \(q_i(a-c-q_i-q_j)\), a strictly concave quadratic in \(q_i\). Its unconstrained maximizer is \((a-c-q_j)/2\). Accounting for the nonnegativity constraint, the best response is \[B_i(q_j)=\max\{0,(a-c-q_j)/2\}.\] In equilibrium both firms must produce less than \(a-c\); if one produced zero while the other produced less than \(a-c\), the zero producer would profitably enter, and if the other produced at least \(a-c\), it would prefer reducing output. Thus both best responses are interior. Solving \(q_1=(a-c-q_2)/2\) and \(q_2=(a-c-q_1)/2\) gives \(q_1=q_2=(a-c)/3\). Profit for each firm is \[(a-c)/3\cdot (a-c-2(a-c)/3)=(a-c)^2/9.\] Strict concavity of each payoff in own quantity and the unique intersection of best responses establish uniqueness.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Uses the Cournot topic as inspiration with a standard linear-demand specification.

validation.notes: Accepted after narrowing cost to c>0; best responses and unique Cournot equilibrium are correct.

title: Pure Equilibria of a Complete-Information First-Price Auction
learning_objective: Characterize pure Nash equilibria in a first-price auction with deterministic tie-breaking.
preliminaries: There are \(n\ge 2\) bidders with \(v_1>v_2>\cdots>v_n>0\). Bids are nonnegative. The highest bidder wins, ties are broken in favor of the lowest index, and the winner pays his own bid. A bidder who loses gets payoff \(0\).
question: Characterize all pure-strategy Nash equilibria. In particular, prove that bidder 1 wins in every equilibrium.
solution:
The pure equilibria are exactly the bid profiles \(b\) such that \(b_1\in [v_2,v_1]\), \(b_j\le b_1\) for all \(j
e 1\), and at least one bidder \(j
e 1\) bids \(b_j=b_1\). In such a profile bidder 1 wins by tie-breaking and obtains \(v_1-b_1\ge 0\). He cannot profitably raise his bid. He cannot lower it and still win unless he remains weakly above all other bids; the existence of another bidder bidding \(b_1\) prevents any strict lowering that still wins. Any losing bidder \(j\) would have to bid above \(b_1\) to win, giving payoff \(v_j-b'>v_j-b_1\) only if \(b'<v_j\). But \(b_1\ge v_2\ge v_j\), so no such profitable winning deviation exists.

Conversely, in any equilibrium bidder 1 must win. If bidder \(k
e 1\) wins at price \(p\), then either \(p>v_k\), in which case bidder \(k\) prefers losing, or \(p\le v_k<v_1\), in which case bidder 1 can bid slightly above \(p\) and win with positive payoff. Let \(p=b_1\) be the winning bid. We need \(p\le v_1\), otherwise bidder 1 prefers losing. We need \(p\ge v_2\), otherwise bidder 2 can bid between \(p\) and \(v_2\) and win profitably. Finally some other bidder must bid \(p\); otherwise bidder 1 could lower his bid slightly and still win. These conditions are therefore necessary and sufficient.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Inspired by the complete-information first-price auction equilibrium characterization.

validation.notes: Accepted: pure-equilibrium characterization correctly handles tie-breaking and deviations.

title: Immediate Concession in a Pure War of Attrition
learning_objective: Analyze pure equilibria in a timing game and handle simultaneous concession carefully.
preliminaries: Two players choose concession times \(t_i\in [0,\infty)\). If \(t_i<t_j\), player \(i\) concedes at \(t_i\), gets payoff \(-t_i\), and player \(j\) gets \(v_j-t_i\). If \(t_i=t_j=t\), each gets \(v_i/2-t\). Values satisfy \(v_i>0\).
question: Find all pure Nash equilibria of this game.
solution:
First, no equilibrium can have \(t_1=t_2=t>0\), because either player can deviate to \(t+\varepsilon\) for small \(\varepsilon>0\) and win the object at time \(t\), increasing payoff from \(v_i/2-t\) to \(v_i-t\). No equilibrium can have \(0<t_i<t_j\), because player \(i\), who concedes first, gets \(-t_i<0\) and can deviate to \(0\) to get \(0\). No equilibrium can have \(0<t_j<t_i\) by the same argument.

Thus any equilibrium must have at least one player choosing \(0\). If \((0,t_j)\) with \(t_j>0\), player 1 gets \(0\) and cannot improve by conceding later, because any later concession wins only if player 2 concedes at \(t_j\), giving at most \(v_1-t_j\) if he waits past \(t_j\), which may be positive; hence we need to check. If player 1 deviates to \(s>t_j\), he obtains \(v_1-t_j\), which is profitable whenever \(t_j<v_1\). Therefore \((0,t_j)\) is an equilibrium iff \(t_j\ge v_1\). Symmetrically, \((t_1,0)\) is an equilibrium iff \(t_1\ge v_2\). Finally, \((0,0)\) gives each \(v_i/2\); deviating to a positive time gives \(v_i\), so \((0,0)\) is not an equilibrium. The equilibria are exactly \((0,t_2)\) with \(t_2\ge v_1\) and \((t_1,0)\) with \(t_1\ge v_2\). In every equilibrium one player concedes immediately.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Inspired by the war-of-attrition strategic-form exercise; rewritten with complete equilibrium characterization.

validation.notes: Accepted: timing cases and immediate-concession equilibrium conditions are correct.

title: Removing a Strategy Can Improve Equilibrium Payoffs
learning_objective: Contrast single-agent menu deletion with strategic effects of removing an action in a game.
preliminaries: Consider the two-player normal-form game. Player 1 chooses row \(a_{11}\) or \(a_{12}\); player 2 chooses column \(a_{21}\) or \(a_{22}\). Payoffs are \((5,5)\) at \((a_{11},a_{21})\), \((0,4)\) at \((a_{11},a_{22})\), \((7,0)\) at \((a_{12},a_{21})\), and \((1,1)\) at \((a_{12},a_{22})\).
question: Find all Nash equilibria of the original game. Then eliminate \(a_{12}\) for player 1 and find all Nash equilibria of the reduced game. Compare this with the single-agent result that deleting an alternative cannot make the decision maker worse off.
solution:
In the original game, player 1's best response to \(a_{21}\) is \(a_{12}\), because \(7>5\). Player 1's best response to \(a_{22}\) is also \(a_{12}\), because \(1>0\). Thus \(a_{12}\) strictly dominates \(a_{11}\) for player 1. Player 2's best response to \(a_{11}\) is \(a_{21}\), because \(5>4\). Player 2's best response to \(a_{12}\) is \(a_{22}\), because \(1>0\). The only mutual best response is \((a_{12},a_{22})\), with payoffs \((1,1)\).

If \(a_{12}\) is removed, player 1 must play \(a_{11}\). Player 2's best response to \(a_{11}\) is \(a_{21}\), so the unique equilibrium of the reduced game is \((a_{11},a_{21})\), with payoffs \((5,5)\). Player 1 is better off in the reduced game equilibrium than in the original game equilibrium. This does not contradict the single-agent deletion result: in a game, removing one player's action can change the other player's equilibrium behavior. The improved outcome comes from strategic interaction, not from the removed action being directly preferred by player 1.

source.original_question: Find all Nash equilibria before and after eliminating a12 for player 1, and compare with the single-agent deletion result.
source.notes: Converted from the answer key's equilibrium comparison.

validation.notes: Best-response checks identify the unique equilibrium in each game and the strategic reason payoffs improve.

title: Nash Equilibrium by Strict Dominance
learning_objective: Find the Nash equilibrium of a two-action simultaneous game.
preliminaries: Player 1 chooses row \(S\) or \(C\); Player 2 chooses column \(S\) or \(C\). Payoffs are \((S,S)=(5,2)\), \((S,C)=(3,1)\), \((C,S)=(6,3)\), and \((C,C)=(4,4)\), with Player 1's payoff first.
question: Find all Nash equilibria of the simultaneous-move game.
solution: For Player 1, \(C\) strictly dominates \(S\): against Player 2's \(S\), \(6>5\), and against Player 2's \(C\), \(4>3\). Thus Player 1 must choose \(C\) in any Nash equilibrium. Given \(C\), Player 2 compares \(S\), which gives \(3\), with \(C\), which gives \(4\). Player 2 chooses \(C\). Therefore the unique Nash equilibrium is \((C,C)\).

source.original_question: May 2025 Question 4
source.notes: Converted from the payoff matrix without relying on the source figure.

validation.notes: Strict dominance and best response check verified.

title: Rationalizable Play in a \(p\)-Beauty Contest
learning_objective: Use iterated reasoning to solve a beauty-contest game.
preliminaries: There are \(n\ge 2\) players. Each chooses a number \(x_i\in[0,100]\). Let \(\bar x=(1/n)\sum_i x_i\). The winner is the player whose choice is closest to \(p\bar x\), where \(p\in(0,1)\); ties are split equally. For rationalizability, focus on actions that can be exact best responses to beliefs about the opponents' surviving choices.
question: Show that the only rationalizable action is \(0\).
solution: Fix a stage of the elimination argument in which every opponent's surviving choices lie in \([0,M]\). If player \(i\)'s opponents have total \(S\le (n-1)M\), choosing \(x_i\) makes the target \(p(x_i+S)/n\), so the distance to minimize is \[\left|x_i-\frac{p(x_i+S)}{n}\right|=\left|\left(1-\frac pn\right)x_i-\frac{pS}{n}\right|.\] The unique closest choice is \(x_i=pS/(n-p)\), which is at most \[\frac{p(n-1)}{n-p}M.\] Because \(p<1\), the factor \(\rho=p(n-1)/(n-p)\) satisfies \(0<\rho<1\). Starting from \(M_0=100\), iterated rationalizability therefore implies that surviving choices are contained in \([0,100\rho^k]\) after \(k\) rounds. Since \(100\rho^k\to 0\), every rationalizable action must be \(0\). The action \(0\) is rationalizable because it is a best response to the belief that all opponents choose \(0\), in which case the target is \(0\). Therefore the unique rationalizable action is \(0\).

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Inspired by the guess-the-average rationalizability exercise; stated with a complete standalone model.

validation.notes: Accepted after repairing best-response bound to account for own choice in the average.

title: Correlated Beliefs and Rationalizable Actions
learning_objective: Show how allowing correlated beliefs can enlarge the set of rationalizable actions in games with three or more players.
preliminaries: Three players share the same payoff. Player 1 chooses \(U\) or \(D\), player 2 chooses \(L\) or \(R\), and player 3 chooses \(M_1,M_2,M_3,M_4\). Payoffs are: under \(M_1\), \((U,L)=8\) and all other row-column pairs give \(0\); under \(M_2\), \((U,L)=4\), \((D,R)=4\), others \(0\); under \(M_3\), \((D,R)=8\), others \(0\); under \(M_4\), every row-column pair gives \(3\).
question: Show that \(M_2\) is a best response to a correlated belief over players 1 and 2's actions, but is not a best response to any independent mixed strategies of players 1 and 2.
solution:
If player 3 believes that \((U,L)\) and \((D,R)\) each occur with probability \(1/2\), then expected payoffs from \(M_1,M_2,M_3,M_4\) are \(4,4,4,3\). Thus \(M_2\) is a best response to this correlated belief.

Now suppose players 1 and 2 use independent mixed strategies, with \(p=\Pr(U)\) and \(q=\Pr(L)\). The expected payoff from \(M_2\) is \(4pq+4(1-p)(1-q)\). For \(M_2\) to be a best response it must beat \(M_1\), \(M_3\), and \(M_4\), so \[4pq+4(1-p)(1-q)\ge 8pq,\] \[4pq+4(1-p)(1-q)\ge 8(1-p)(1-q),\] and \[4pq+4(1-p)(1-q)\ge 3.\] The first two inequalities imply \((1-p)(1-q)\ge pq\) and \(pq\ge (1-p)(1-q)\), hence \(pq=(1-p)(1-q)\). Then the payoff from \(M_2\) is \(8pq\). The equality implies \(p+q=1\), so \(pq=p(1-p)\le 1/4\), and the payoff from \(M_2\) is at most \(2<3\). Thus \(M_4\) strictly beats \(M_2\). Therefore \(M_2\) cannot be a best response to independent beliefs, although it can be justified by a correlated belief.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.locator: Figure 58.1 and surrounding discussion on correlated versus independent rationalizability
source.notes: Recasts the source example as a standalone proof problem.

validation.notes: Accepted: correlated-belief construction and independent-belief impossibility are correct.

title: Rationalizability in a Bounded Cournot Game
learning_objective: Show how rationalizability can sharply select the Cournot equilibrium by iterating best-response bounds.
preliminaries: Two firms choose \(a_i\in[0,1]\). Payoffs are \(u_i(a_i,a_j)=a_i(1-a_i-a_j)\). An action is rationalizable if it is a best response to some belief supported on the opponent's rationalizable actions; in this two-player continuous game it is enough to track possible opponent quantities in intervals.
question: Prove that each player's only rationalizable action is \(1/3\).
solution: Firm \(i\)'s expected payoff against a belief over \(a_j\) depends on that belief only through \(m=E[a_j]\): \(a_i(1-a_i-m)\). The unique best response is \(B(m)=(1-m)/2\), since \(m\in[0,1]\). Starting from the initial possible set \([0,1]\), expected opponent quantities can have any mean in \([0,1]\), so first-round rationalizable actions lie in \([0,1/2]\). If the opponent's surviving set is an interval \([\ell_k,u_k]\), then possible means are exactly \([\ell_k,u_k]\), and the next surviving interval is \([(1-u_k)/2,(1-\ell_k)/2]\). Thus after the first round the intervals are nested and their lengths satisfy \(u_{k+1}-\ell_{k+1}=(u_k-\ell_k)/2\). Their endpoints therefore converge to a common limit \(x\), and the recurrence gives \(x=(1-x)/2\), so \(x=1/3\). The intersection of all surviving intervals is \(\{1/3\}\). Since \(1/3\) is a best response to the belief concentrated on \(1/3\), it is rationalizable. Hence it is the unique rationalizable action for each player.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Inspired by the rationalizability exercise; proof rewritten through interval best-response bounds.

validation.notes: Accepted after tightening interval-convergence proof; unique rationalizable action is correct.

title: Rationalizability versus Nash in a Pure Coordination Location Game
learning_objective: Distinguish the restrictions imposed by rationalizability from those imposed by Nash equilibrium.
preliminaries: Two players simultaneously choose locations \(a_i\in[0,1]\). Player \(i\)'s payoff is \(-|a_i-a_j|\). A mixed belief over the opponent's action is allowed.
question: Find the pure Nash equilibria and show that every action in \([0,1]\) is rationalizable for each player.
solution:
Given a pure opponent action \(a_j\), player \(i\)'s unique best response is \(a_i=a_j\). Therefore a pure profile is a Nash equilibrium iff \(a_1=a_2\). Hence the pure Nash equilibrium set is \(\{(x,x):x\in[0,1]\}\).

Every action \(x\in[0,1]\) is rationalizable. Let each player's candidate rationalizable set be the whole interval \([0,1]\). For any action \(x\) of player \(i\), the degenerate belief that the opponent chooses \(x\) is supported on \([0,1]\), and \(x\) is the unique best response to that belief. Thus each action in the candidate set is justified by a belief supported on the opponent's candidate set. By the fixed-point definition of rationalizability, all actions are rationalizable. Rationalizability therefore imposes no restriction here beyond feasibility, while pure Nash equilibrium requires the chosen locations to match.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Uses the location-game contrast between Nash equilibrium and rationalizability.

validation.notes: Accepted after narrowing Nash request to pure equilibria; rationalizability argument is correct.

title: Three-Period Punishments in a Repeated Prisoner's Dilemma
learning_objective: Derive an incentive constraint for cooperation under finite-length punishments.
preliminaries: Use the prisoner's dilemma payoffs \((C,C)=(3,3)\), \((D,C)=(5,0)\), \((C,D)=(0,5)\), and \((D,D)=(1,1)\). Consider the infinite discounted game with normalized payoffs. Strategy \(s\): play \(C\) in a normal state; after any unilateral \(D\) in a normal state, both players enter a public punishment state in which they play \(D\) for the next three periods, then return to the normal state if no new deviation occurs.
question: Find a sufficient condition on \(\delta\) under which no player wants to deviate from the normal cooperative state, assuming the prescribed punishment path is followed.
solution: From the normal state, complying forever gives payoff \(3\). A one-shot deviation to \(D\) gives current payoff \(5\). It then triggers three periods in which the deviator receives \(1\) each period if both play \(D\), followed by a return to cooperation with payoff \(3\) forever. The normalized deviation payoff is \[(1-\delta)5+(1-\delta)(\delta+\delta^2+\delta^3)1+\delta^4\cdot 3.\] Cooperation in the normal state is optimal if this is at most \(3\). Simplifying, \[3\ge 5(1-\delta)+(1-\delta)(\delta+\delta^2+\delta^3)+3\delta^4.\] Since \((1-\delta)(\delta+\delta^2+\delta^3)=\delta-\delta^4\), this becomes \(3\ge 5-4\delta-\delta^4+3\delta^4=5-4\delta+2\delta^4\), or \[2\delta^4-4\delta+2\le 0,\quad\text{equivalently}\quad \delta^4-2\delta+1\le 0.\] This is a sufficient no-deviation condition for the cooperative state. A full SPE verification would also check deviations during punishment states; the question asks only for the normal-state constraint under the prescribed punishment path.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.locator: Example 141.2 finite punishment machine and repeated-game trigger strategy discussion
source.notes: Finite-punishment variant inspired by the machine example.

validation.notes: Accepted after clarifying public punishment state; normal-state incentive constraint is correct.

title: Unused States Cannot Survive Costly Machine Equilibrium
learning_objective: Prove that equilibrium machines cannot contain states that are never reached when complexity is costly.
preliminaries: In a two-player machine game, each player chooses a finite automaton that induces an infinite discounted payoff \(U_i\). Player \(i\)'s preference is increasing in \(U_i\) and decreasing in the number of states of his own machine, holding \(U_i\) fixed.
question: Prove that if \((M_1,M_2)\) is a Nash equilibrium of the machine game, then every state of each \(M_i\) is reached along the path generated by \((M_1,M_2)\).
solution: Suppose player \(i\)'s machine \(M_i\) has a state \(q\) that is never reached when \((M_1,M_2)\) operate from their initial states. Construct a new machine \(M_i'\) by deleting \(q\) and all transitions from \(q\). Any transition from another state into \(q\) is irrelevant along the realized path, because \(q\) is never reached; define such off-path transitions arbitrarily to some remaining state. Against \(M_j\), the sequence of states actually visited before deletion is unchanged, so the sequence of actions and the repeated-game payoff \(U_i\) are unchanged. But \(M_i'\) has one fewer state, hence lower complexity. Since player \(i\)'s preferences strictly favor lower complexity when repeated-game payoff is the same, \(M_i'\) is a profitable deviation. Therefore the original pair could not have been a Nash equilibrium. Every state of every equilibrium machine must be reached.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Proof exercise based on the unused-state lemma for machine games.

validation.notes: Accepted: deletion of unreached state is a valid profitable deviation under costly complexity.

title: Finite Repetition of a Prisoner's Dilemma
learning_objective: Use backward induction to show why finite repetition with a unique stage equilibrium does not sustain cooperation.
preliminaries: Use the prisoner's dilemma stage game with payoffs \((C,C)=(3,3)\), \((D,C)=(5,0)\), \((C,D)=(0,5)\), and \((D,D)=(1,1)\). The game is repeated for \(T<\infty\) periods, with observed actions after each period and total payoff equal to the sum of stage payoffs.
question: Show that the unique subgame perfect equilibrium has both players choose \(D\) in every period after every history.
solution: In the final period \(T\), the continuation game is just the one-shot prisoner's dilemma. The action \(D\) strictly dominates \(C\), so both players must choose \(D\) in every subgame beginning in period \(T\). Now consider period \(T-1\). Since period \(T\) play will be \((D,D)\) regardless of what happens in period \(T-1\), future payoffs are fixed from the perspective of current deviations. Thus the period \(T-1\) choice again reduces to the one-shot prisoner's dilemma, and both players choose \(D\). Repeating this backward-induction argument period by period implies that both players choose \(D\) in every period after every history. Uniqueness follows because strict dominance in each terminal continuation pins down the action at every step.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.locator: Chapter 8 finite versus infinite repeated Prisoner's Dilemma discussion
source.notes: Assumption-change variant from infinite to finite horizon.

validation.notes: Accepted: backward induction gives unique all-D SPE.

title: Grim Trigger in the Infinitely Repeated Prisoner's Dilemma
learning_objective: Derive the discount-factor condition under which grim trigger sustains cooperation.
preliminaries: The stage game has actions \(C,D\) and payoffs \((C,C)=(3,3)\), \((D,C)=(5,0)\), \((C,D)=(0,5)\), and \((D,D)=(1,1)\). The game is repeated infinitely with common discount factor \(\delta\in(0,1)\), and payoffs are normalized as \((1-\delta)\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\delta^t u_t\). Grim trigger plays \(C\) until any deviation is observed and then plays \(D\) forever.
question: For which \(\delta\) is the grim-trigger strategy profile a subgame perfect equilibrium?
solution:
After any history in which a deviation has occurred, both players are prescribed \(D\) forever. Since \(D\) is a stage-game best response to \(D\), no player wants to deviate in a punishment subgame.

On the cooperative path, following the strategy gives normalized payoff \(3\). A one-shot deviation to \(D\) gives current payoff \(5\), followed by punishment payoff \(1\) forever, for normalized payoff \((1-\delta)5+\delta\cdot 1=5-4\delta\). Cooperation is optimal iff \(3\ge 5-4\delta\), i.e. \(\delta\ge 1/2\). By the one-shot deviation principle for discounted repeated games with finite action sets, checking these one-period deviations after every history is sufficient. Hence grim trigger is a subgame perfect equilibrium exactly when \(\delta\ge 1/2\).

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.locator: Figure 134.1 and Example 141.1; trigger strategies in Section 8.5
source.notes: Classic repeated PD grim-trigger calculation anchored to the source discussion.

validation.notes: Accepted: one-shot deviation threshold delta >= 1/2 is correct.

title: Pooling Equilibrium in a Two-Type Spence Signaling Game
learning_objective: Verify pooling equilibria and derive the education bound imposed by deviations.
preliminaries: Use the Spence environment with types \(0<\theta_L<\theta_H\). The prior probability of high type is \(p\in(0,1)\). If both types choose the same education \(e^*\), the competitive pooling wage is \(\bar\theta=p\theta_H+(1-p)\theta_L\). Worker payoff is \(w-e/\theta\).
question: Under off-path beliefs that any education \(e\ne e^*\) comes from the low type, for which \(e^*\) is pooling at \(e^*\) a perfect Bayesian equilibrium?
solution: If both types choose \(e^*\), Bayes' rule implies wage \(\bar\theta\) at \(e^*\). Off path, the employer pays \(\theta_L\). A type \(\theta\) worker's equilibrium payoff is \(\bar\theta-e^*/\theta\). The most attractive off-path deviation under the specified beliefs is \(e=0\), yielding \(	heta_L\), since any positive off-path education has the same wage and a higher education cost. Thus pooling is sequentially rational for type \(\theta\) iff \(\bar\theta-e^*/\theta\ge	heta_L\), or \(e^*\le 	heta( ar	heta-	heta_L)\). The tighter constraint is for the low type because \(\theta_L<\theta_H\). Therefore pooling at \(e^*\) is a PBE under these beliefs iff \[0\le e^*\le 	heta_L( ar	heta-	heta_L)=	heta_L p(\theta_H-	heta_L).\] The employer's wage choices are optimal given beliefs, and both worker types are optimal by the inequality.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Pooling variant of the Spence signaling calculation.

validation.notes: Accepted: pooling wage, off-path deviations, and low-type binding constraint are correct.

title: Separating Equilibrium in a Two-Type Spence Signaling Game
learning_objective: Verify separating perfect Bayesian equilibria using incentive constraints and beliefs.
preliminaries: A worker has type \(\theta\in\{\theta_L,\theta_H\}\), with \(0<\theta_L<\theta_H\). The worker chooses education \(e\ge 0\). A competitive employer observes \(e\) and pays wage equal to expected productivity. Productivity equals \(\theta\). Worker payoff is \(w-e/\theta\).
question: Find the range of education levels \(e_H\) that can be used in a separating perfect Bayesian equilibrium with \(e_L=0\), wage \(w(0)=\theta_L\), and wage \(w(e_H)=\theta_H\).
solution: In a separating equilibrium, beliefs put probability one on type \(L\) after \(e=0\) and probability one on type \(H\) after \(e=e_H\), so competitive wages are \(\theta_L\) and \(\theta_H\). The low type must not want to mimic the high type: \[\theta_L\ge \theta_H-e_H/\theta_L,\] which is equivalent to \(e_H\ge \theta_L(\theta_H-\theta_L)\). The high type must not want to mimic the low type: \[\theta_H-e_H/\theta_H\ge \theta_L,\] which is equivalent to \(e_H\le \theta_H(\theta_H-\theta_L)\). Since \(\theta_H>\theta_L\), the interval is nonempty. Off-path beliefs can assign probability one to the low type after education levels other than \(0\) and \(e_H\), with corresponding wage \(\theta_L\); under these beliefs no type obtains a better deviation than the constraints already rule out. Therefore separating PBE of this form exist exactly for \[e_H\in[\theta_L(\theta_H-\theta_L),\theta_H(\theta_H-\theta_L)].\]

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Standalone version of the separating-equilibrium calculation.

validation.notes: Accepted: separating IC bounds and off-path belief verification are correct.

title: Equilibrium and Maxmin in a Strictly Competitive Game
learning_objective: Connect Nash equilibrium payoffs to maxmin values in a two-player strictly competitive game.
preliminaries: A finite two-player game is zero-sum if player 2's payoff is \(-u_1(a_1,a_2)\). Mixed strategies are allowed. Define \(\underline v=\max_{\alpha_1}\min_{\alpha_2} U_1(\alpha_1,\alpha_2)\) and \(\overline v=\min_{\alpha_2}\max_{\alpha_1} U_1(\alpha_1,\alpha_2)\).
question: Prove that if \((\alpha_1^*,\alpha_2^*)\) is a mixed Nash equilibrium, then \(U_1(\alpha_1^*,\alpha_2^*)=\underline v=\overline v\), \(\alpha_1^*\) is maxmin, and \(\alpha_2^*\) is minmax.
solution: At a Nash equilibrium, \(\alpha_1^*\) is a best response to \(\alpha_2^*\), so \(U_1(\alpha_1^*,\alpha_2^*)=\max_{\alpha_1}U_1(\alpha_1,\alpha_2^*)\). Also \(\alpha_2^*\) is a best response for player 2, which in a zero-sum game means it minimizes player 1's payoff against \(\alpha_1^*\): \(U_1(\alpha_1^*,\alpha_2^*)=\min_{\alpha_2}U_1(\alpha_1^*,\alpha_2)\). Therefore \[\underline v=\max_{\alpha_1}\min_{\alpha_2}U_1(\alpha_1,\alpha_2)\ge \min_{\alpha_2}U_1(\alpha_1^*,\alpha_2)=U_1(\alpha_1^*,\alpha_2^*),\] while by definition \(\underline v\le \overline v\). Also \[\overline v=\min_{\alpha_2}\max_{\alpha_1}U_1(\alpha_1,\alpha_2)\le \max_{\alpha_1}U_1(\alpha_1,\alpha_2^*)=U_1(\alpha_1^*,\alpha_2^*).\] Combining with \(\underline v\le\overline v\) gives equality throughout. The equalities show that \(\alpha_1^*\) attains the maxmin value and \(\alpha_2^*\) attains the minmax value.

source.family: Osborne and Rubinstein, A Course in Game Theory
source.notes: Rewritten as a finite zero-sum mixed-strategy proof exercise.

validation.notes: Accepted: equilibrium payoff, maxmin, and minmax equalities are proved correctly.

title: Local Maxima of Concave Functions are Global
learning_objective: Use concavity to turn local optimality into global optimality.
preliminaries: Let \(X\subset\mathbb R^d\) be convex. A function \(f:X\to\mathbb R\) is concave if \(f(\lambda x+(1-\lambda)y)\ge\lambda f(x)+(1-\lambda)f(y)\) for all \(x,y\in X\), \(\lambda\in[0,1]\).
question: Suppose \(f:X\to\mathbb R\) is concave and \(x^*\in X\) is a local maximum: there exists \(r>0\) such that \(f(x^*)\ge f(x)\) for all \(x\in X\cap B_r(x^*)\). Prove that \(x^*\) is a global maximum on \(X\).
solution:
Take any \(y\in X\). If \(y=x^*\), there is nothing to prove. If \(y\ne x^*\), choose \(\lambda\in(0,1)\) close enough to \(1\) that \(z=\lambda x^*+(1-\lambda)y\) lies in \(B_r(x^*)\). This is possible because \(\|z-x^*\|=(1-\lambda)\|y-x^*\|\). By convexity of \(X\), \(z\in X\), so local maximality gives \(f(x^*)\ge f(z)\). Concavity gives
\[f(z)\ge \lambda f(x^*)+(1-\lambda)f(y).\]
Combining, \(f(x^*)\ge \lambda f(x^*)+(1-\lambda)f(y)\). Since \(1-\lambda>0\), rearrange to get \(f(x^*)\ge f(y)\). Because \(y\in X\) was arbitrary, \(x^*\) is a global maximum.

source.locator: Proposition 9, local and global maxima under concavity
source.notes: Standalone proof of the local-to-global property.

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: Caratheodory Reduction in the Plane
learning_objective: Apply the reduction idea behind Caratheodory's theorem in \(\mathbb R^2\).
preliminaries: In \(\mathbb R^2\), any three vectors are linearly dependent. A convex combination has nonnegative coefficients summing to one.
question: Suppose \(y\in\mathbb R^2\) can be written as a convex combination of four points \(x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4\in\mathbb R^2\): \(y=\sum_{i=1}^4\alpha_i x_i\), with \(\alpha_i>0\) and \(\sum_i\alpha_i=1\). Prove that \(y\) can be written as a convex combination of at most three of the four points.
solution:
The three vectors \(x_1-x_4,x_2-x_4,x_3-x_4\) in \(\mathbb R^2\) are linearly dependent. Hence there are numbers \(\beta_1,\beta_2,\beta_3\), not all zero, with \(\sum_{i=1}^3\beta_i(x_i-x_4)=0\). Define \(\beta_4=-\sum_{i=1}^3\beta_i\). Then \(\sum_{i=1}^4\beta_i=0\) and \(\sum_{i=1}^4\beta_i x_i=0\). At least one \(\beta_i>0\); otherwise all nonzero \(\beta_i\le0\) and their sum could not be zero.

Let \(t=\min\{\alpha_i/\beta_i:\beta_i>0\}\). Define \(\lambda_i=\alpha_i-t\beta_i\). If \(\beta_i>0\), then \(\lambda_i\ge0\) by the definition of \(t\). If \(\beta_i\le0\), then \(\lambda_i=\alpha_i+t|\beta_i|>0\). Also \(\sum_i\lambda_i=\sum_i\alpha_i-t\sum_i\beta_i=1\), and \(\sum_i\lambda_i x_i=\sum_i\alpha_i x_i-t\sum_i\beta_i x_i=y\). For an index attaining the minimum, \(\lambda_i=0\). Dropping that term gives a convex combination using at most three points.

source.notes: Specializes the proof of Caratheodory's theorem to \(\mathbb R^2\).

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: A Closed Set with Nonclosed Convex Hull
learning_objective: Show that closedness is not preserved by taking convex hulls without compactness.
preliminaries: For a set \(S\subset\mathbb R^2\), \(\operatorname{co}(S)\) denotes the set of finite convex combinations of points of \(S\). A subset of \(\mathbb R^2\) is closed if it contains the limit of every convergent sequence from the set.
question: Let \(S=\{(0,0)\}\cup\{(x,y)\in\mathbb R^2:x>0,\ xy=1\}\). Prove that \(S\) is closed but \(\operatorname{co}(S)\) is not closed by showing that \((1,0)\in\overline{\operatorname{co}(S)}\) while \((1,0)\notin\operatorname{co}(S)\).
solution:
First prove closedness. Let \(z_n\in S\) and \(z_n\to z=(x,y)\). If infinitely many \(z_n\) equal \((0,0)\), then that subsequence converges to \((0,0)\), so uniqueness of limits forces \(z=(0,0)\) unless all but finitely many remaining terms are absent. If infinitely many terms lie on the hyperbola, pass to that subsequence. Along it, write \(z_n=(x_n,y_n)\) with \(x_n>0\) and \(x_ny_n=1\). Continuity of multiplication gives \(xy=1\). Also \(x\ge0\) because \(x_n>0\), and \(xy=1\) implies \(x>0\). Thus \(z\) lies on the hyperbola. Hence every convergent sequence in \(S\) has its limit in \(S\), so \(S\) is closed.

For each \(n\ge1\), the point \(g_n=(n,1/n)\) lies in \(S\). The convex combination
\[z_n=\frac1n g_n+\left(1-\frac1n\right)(0,0)=(1,1/n^2)\]
belongs to \(\operatorname{co}(S)\), and \(z_n\to(1,0)\). Therefore \((1,0)\in\overline{\operatorname{co}(S)}\).

It remains to show \((1,0)\notin\operatorname{co}(S)\). Any finite convex combination of points of \(S\) has the form
\[\alpha_0(0,0)+\sum_{i=1}^m\alpha_i(x_i,1/x_i),\]
where \(x_i>0\), \(\alpha_i\ge0\), and \(\alpha_0+\sum_i\alpha_i=1\). If its first coordinate equals \(1\), then at least one \(\alpha_i\) with \(i\ge1\) is positive. Its second coordinate is then \(\sum_i\alpha_i/x_i>0\), so it cannot equal \(0\). Thus \((1,0)\notin\operatorname{co}(S)\), and the convex hull is not closed.

source.notes: Corrected hyperbola-plus-origin counterexample showing a closed set can have nonclosed convex hull.

validation.notes: Accepted after replacing the false hyperbola-only closure claim with a correct closed-set example including the origin; closure and nonmembership proofs checked.

title: Compactness of the Convex Hull of a Compact Set
learning_objective: Use Caratheodory's theorem to prove compactness of a convex hull.
preliminaries: You may use Caratheodory's theorem: if \(S\subset\mathbb R^d\), every point in \(\operatorname{co}(S)\) is a convex combination of at most \(d+1\) points of \(S\).
question: Let \(S\subset\mathbb R^d\) be compact. Prove that \(\operatorname{co}(S)\) is compact.
solution:
By Caratheodory's theorem, every \(y\in\operatorname{co}(S)\) can be represented as \(y=\sum_{i=1}^{d+1}\alpha_i s_i\), where \(s_i\in S\), \(\alpha_i\ge0\), and \(\sum_i\alpha_i=1\). Allowing zero coefficients, define the simplex \(\Delta_d=\{\alpha\in\mathbb R^{d+1}_+:\sum_i\alpha_i=1\}\), which is closed and bounded, hence compact. The product \(\Delta_d\times S^{d+1}\) is compact.

Define \(F:\Delta_d\times S^{d+1}\to\mathbb R^d\) by \(F(\alpha,s_1,\ldots,s_{d+1})=\sum_{i=1}^{d+1}\alpha_i s_i\). This map is continuous. Its image is exactly \(\operatorname{co}(S)\) by Caratheodory and the definition of convex combinations. A continuous image of a compact set is compact. Therefore \(\operatorname{co}(S)\) is compact.

source.notes: Uses the notes' suggested Caratheodory proof.

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: Convex Hull as Finite Convex Combinations
learning_objective: Prove the finite-combination representation of a convex hull.
preliminaries: For \(S\subset\mathbb R^d\), the convex hull \(\operatorname{co}(S)\) is the smallest convex set containing \(S\), equivalently the intersection of all convex sets containing \(S\).
question:
Prove that \(\operatorname{co}(S)\) equals the set of all finite convex combinations of points in \(S\):
\[C=\left\{\sum_{i=1}^n\alpha_i s_i:n\in\mathbb N,\ s_i\in S,\ \alpha_i\ge0,\ \sum_{i=1}^n\alpha_i=1\right\}.\]
solution:
First, \(C\) contains \(S\), because any \(s\in S\) equals \(1\cdot s\). We show \(C\) is convex. Take \(x=\sum_{i=1}^n\alpha_i s_i\in C\), \(y=\sum_{j=1}^m\beta_j t_j\in C\), and \(\lambda\in[0,1]\). Then
\[\lambda x+(1-\lambda)y=\sum_{i=1}^n(\lambda\alpha_i)s_i+\sum_{j=1}^m((1-\lambda)\beta_j)t_j,\]
where all coefficients are nonnegative and sum to \(\lambda+(1-\lambda)=1\). Hence \(\lambda x+(1-\lambda)y\in C\), so \(C\) is convex. Since \(\operatorname{co}(S)\) is the smallest convex set containing \(S\), \(\operatorname{co}(S)\subset C\).

Conversely, every convex set \(T\) that contains \(S\) contains all finite convex combinations of points in \(S\), by induction on the number of points. Therefore \(C\subset T\) for every such \(T\), and hence \(C\subset\bigcap_T T=\operatorname{co}(S)\). Thus \(C=\operatorname{co}(S)\).

source.notes: Standalone proof of the finite convex-combination representation.

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: Strongly Separating a Compact Convex Set and a Point
learning_objective: Derive a separating hyperplane between a compact convex set and an exterior point.
preliminaries: A hyperplane \(H(p,a)=\{x:p\cdot x=a\}\) strongly separates sets \(A\) and \(B\) if there is \(\eta>0\) such that \(p\cdot a'\le a-\eta<a+\eta\le p\cdot b'\) for all relevant points after possibly reversing signs.
question: Let \(C\subset\mathbb R^d\) be nonempty, compact, and convex, and let \(y\notin C\). Prove that there exist \(p\ne0\), \(a\in\mathbb R\), and \(\eta>0\) such that \(p\cdot x\le a-\eta<a+\eta\le p\cdot y\) for all \(x\in C\).
solution:
Because \(C\) is compact and the function \(x\mapsto\|y-x\|\) is continuous, there is \(x^*\in C\) minimizing distance to \(y\). Since \(y\notin C\), \(p=y-x^*\ne0\). We claim \(p\cdot(x-x^*)\le0\) for all \(x\in C\). If for some \(x\in C\), \(p\cdot(x-x^*)>0\), then for small \(t\in(0,1)\), \(x_t=x^*+t(x-x^*)\in C\) by convexity, and
\[\|y-x_t\|^2=\|p-t(x-x^*)\|^2=\|p\|^2-2t p\cdot(x-x^*)+t^2\|x-x^*\|^2<\|p\|^2,\]
contradicting minimality of \(x^*\). Thus \(p\cdot x\le p\cdot x^*\) for all \(x\in C\), while \(p\cdot y=p\cdot x^*+\|p\|^2\). Let \(a=p\cdot x^*+\|p\|^2/2\) and \(\eta=\|p\|^2/4\). Then \(p\cdot x\le p\cdot x^*=a-\|p\|^2/2\le a-\eta<a+\eta\le a+\|p\|^2/2=p\cdot y\).

source.notes: Adapts the compact-convex separation proof to a point outside the set.

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: Supporting Prices for a Production Set
learning_objective: Interpret a supporting hyperplane as a price vector supporting an efficient boundary point.
preliminaries: A hyperplane \(H(p,a)\) supports a convex set \(Y\) at \(y^*\in Y\) if \(p\cdot y^*=a\) and either \(p\cdot y\le a\) for all \(y\in Y\) or \(p\cdot y\ge a\) for all \(y\in Y\).
question: Let \(Y\subset\mathbb R^d\) be nonempty and convex, and suppose \(y^*\in Y\) is a boundary point at which a supporting hyperplane exists. Show that there is a nonzero vector \(p\) such that \(p\cdot y\le p\cdot y^*\) for all \(y\in Y\). Explain why \(y^*\) solves a profit maximization problem at price vector \(p\).
solution:
By the supporting hyperplane assumption, there are \(p\ne0\) and \(a\) with \(p\cdot y^*=a\) and all of \(Y\) lying in one of the two closed half-spaces. If the inequality is \(p\cdot y\le a\) for all \(y\in Y\), we are done. If it is \(p\cdot y\ge a\), replace \(p\) by \(-p\); then \((-p)\cdot y\le (-p)\cdot y^*\) for all \(y\in Y\). Thus after choosing orientation, \(p\cdot y\le p\cdot y^*\) for all \(y\in Y\).

The profit maximization problem at price vector \(p\) is \(\max_{y\in Y}p\cdot y\). The displayed inequality says every feasible production vector \(y\in Y\) yields profit no larger than \(p\cdot y^*\), while \(y^*\) is feasible. Therefore \(y^*\in\arg\max_{y\in Y}p\cdot y\). The nonzero condition rules out the vacuous price vector.

source.notes: Economics-flavored application of supporting hyperplanes.

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: Continuity of the Budget Correspondence
learning_objective: Verify continuity of a central economic correspondence.
preliminaries: Fix \(d\in\mathbb N\). For \((p,w)\in\mathbb R^d_{++}\times\mathbb R_{++}\), define \(B(p,w)=\{x\in\mathbb R^d_+:p\cdot x\le w\}\).
question: Prove that the budget correspondence \(B:\mathbb R^d_{++}\times\mathbb R_{++}\rightrightarrows\mathbb R^d\) is nonempty, compact-valued, convex-valued, and continuous.
solution:
Nonemptiness holds because \(0\in B(p,w)\). Convexity follows because \(\mathbb R^d_+\) is convex and \(p\cdot x\le w\) is a linear inequality. Closedness follows from closedness of \(\mathbb R^d_+\) and continuity of \(x\mapsto p\cdot x\). Boundedness follows from \(0\le x_i\le w/p_i\) for each coordinate, so values are compact.

For upper hemicontinuity, take \((p_n,w_n)\to(p,w)\) and \(x_n\in B(p_n,w_n)\). Since \(p_n\to p\in\mathbb R^d_{++}\) and \(w_n\to w\), the sequence \((x_n)\) is eventually bounded coordinatewise by, say, \(4w/p_i\), because \(w_n\le2w\) and \(p_{n,i}\ge p_i/2\) for all large \(n\). Any convergent subsequence \(x_{n_k}\to x\) satisfies \(x\ge0\) and \(p\cdot x=\lim p_{n_k}\cdot x_{n_k}\le\lim w_{n_k}=w\), so \(x\in B(p,w)\). With compact values, this sequential closedness criterion gives upper hemicontinuity.

For lower hemicontinuity, fix \(x\in B(p,w)\) and \((p_n,w_n)\to(p,w)\). If \(p\cdot x<w\), then \(x\in B(p_n,w_n)\) for all large \(n\), so choose \(x_n=x\). If \(p\cdot x=w\), then \(x\ne0\). For all large \(n\), \(p_n\cdot x>0\); set \(\lambda_n=\min\{1,w_n/(p_n\cdot x)\}\). Then \(\lambda_n\to1\), \(p_n\cdot(\lambda_n x)\le w_n\), and \(x_n=\lambda_nx\in B(p_n,w_n)\) with \(x_n\to x\). Hence \(B\) is lower hemicontinuous and therefore continuous.

source.notes: Self-contained proof of budget correspondence continuity.

validation.notes: Accepted after tightening the eventual boundedness constant and boundary lower-hemicontinuity construction.

title: Upper and Lower Hemicontinuity of an Interval Correspondence
learning_objective: Check hemicontinuity directly for a simple set-valued map.
preliminaries: For \(F:X\rightrightarrows Y\), upper hemicontinuity at \(x\) means that for every open \(O\supset F(x)\), nearby \(x'\) satisfy \(F(x')\subset O\). Lower hemicontinuity at \(x\) means that for every open \(O\) with \(F(x)\cap O\ne\emptyset\), nearby \(x'\) satisfy \(F(x')\cap O\ne\emptyset\).
question: Let \(F:[0,1]\rightrightarrows\mathbb R\) be defined by \(F(t)=[0,t]\). Prove that \(F\) is both upper and lower hemicontinuous on \([0,1]\).
solution:
Upper hemicontinuity: fix \(t\in[0,1]\) and open \(O\supset[0,t]\). Since \([0,t]\) is compact and contained in open \(O\), there is \(\eta>0\) such that every point within distance \(\eta\) of \([0,t]\) lies in \(O\). If \(|t'-t|<\eta\), then \([0,t']\subset[0,t]+(-\eta,\eta)\subset O\). Thus \(F(t')\subset O\).

Lower hemicontinuity: fix open \(O\) with \([0,t]\cap O\ne\emptyset\). Choose \(y\in[0,t]\cap O\). If \(y<t\), then for all \(t'\) sufficiently close to \(t\), \(y\le t'\), so \(y\in[0,t']\cap O\). If \(y=t\), because \(O\) is open there is \(\eta>0\) with \((t-\eta,t+\eta)\cap[0,1]\subset O\). For \(t'\) close to \(t\), the point \(\min\{t',t\}\) lies in \([0,t']\cap O\). Hence lower hemicontinuity holds at every \(t\).

source.notes: Simple interval correspondence for direct verification practice.

title: Upper Hemicontinuity without Lower Hemicontinuity
learning_objective: Distinguish upper from lower hemicontinuity using a jump correspondence.
preliminaries: Use the open-set definitions of upper and lower hemicontinuity for correspondences from \(\mathbb R\) to \(\mathbb R\).
question: Define \(F:\mathbb R\rightrightarrows\mathbb R\) by \(F(0)=\{0,1\}\) and \(F(t)=\{0\}\) for \(t\ne0\). Determine whether \(F\) is upper hemicontinuous and lower hemicontinuous at \(0\).
solution:
Upper hemicontinuity at \(0\) holds. Let \(O\) be any open set with \(F(0)=\{0,1\}\subset O\). For every \(t\), \(F(t)\subset\{0,1\}\subset O\) if \(t=0\), and \(F(t)=\{0\}\subset O\) if \(t
e0\). Thus any neighborhood of \(0\) works.

Lower hemicontinuity at \(0\) fails. Take the open set \(O=(1/2,3/2)\). Then \(F(0)\cap O=\{1\}
e\emptyset\). But for every \(t
e0\), \(F(t)=\{0\}\), so \(F(t)\cap O=\emptyset\). Therefore no neighborhood of \(0\) satisfies the lower hemicontinuity requirement. This example shows that upper hemicontinuity does not imply lower hemicontinuity.

title: Berge Maximum Theorem for a Quadratic Choice Problem
learning_objective: Apply the maximum theorem to continuity of value and argmax correspondences.
preliminaries: You may use Berge's maximum theorem: if \(\Gamma:\Theta\rightrightarrows X\) is nonempty, compact-valued, and continuous, and \(f:X\times\Theta\to\mathbb R\) is continuous, then the value \(v(\theta)=\max_{x\in\Gamma(\theta)}f(x,\theta)\) is continuous and the argmax correspondence is nonempty, compact-valued, and upper hemicontinuous.
question: Let \(\Theta=[0,1]\), \(X=\mathbb R\), \(\Gamma(\theta)=[0,1+\theta]\), and \(f(x,\theta)=-(x-2\theta)^2\). Find the value function and argmax correspondence. Verify that the conclusions of Berge's theorem hold directly.
solution:
For fixed \(\theta\), maximizing \(-(x-2\theta)^2\) over \([0,1+\theta]\) is equivalent to choosing the feasible \(x\) closest to \(2\theta\). Since \(2\theta\ge0\), the only possible upper truncation matters. The maximizer is \(x^*(\theta)=2\theta\) if \(2\theta\le1+\theta\), i.e. \(\theta\le1\), which holds for all \(\theta\in[0,1]\). Therefore \(X^*(\theta)=\{2\theta\}\). The value is \(v(\theta)=0\) for every \(\theta\).

The value function is constant, hence continuous. The argmax correspondence is singleton-valued with selection \(x^*(\theta)=2\theta\), a continuous function, so it is both upper and lower hemicontinuous. It is nonempty and compact-valued because each value is a singleton. The constraint correspondence \([0,1+\theta]\) is continuous with compact values, and the objective is continuous, so this direct computation matches Berge's theorem.

source.notes: Concrete maximum theorem application with an explicitly computed solution.

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: Berge Maximum Theorem with a Binding Upper Constraint
learning_objective: Compute an argmax correspondence when the unconstrained maximizer may be infeasible.
preliminaries: For \(a\in\mathbb R\), the point in \([0,b]\) closest to \(a\) is \(\min\{\max\{a,0\},b\}\).
question: Let \(\Theta=[0,2]\), \(\Gamma(\theta)=[0,1]\), and \(f(x,\theta)=-(x-\theta)^2\). Compute \(v(\theta)=\max_{x\in\Gamma(\theta)}f(x,\theta)\) and \(X^*(\theta)=\arg\max_{x\in\Gamma(\theta)}f(x,\theta)\). Is \(X^*\) upper hemicontinuous?
solution:
The objective is maximized by choosing the feasible point closest to \(\theta\). Since \(\theta\in[0,2]\), the lower bound never binds. The upper bound binds when \(\theta>1\). Thus
\[X^*(\theta)=\begin{cases}\{\theta\},&0\le\theta\le1,\\ \{1\},&1<\theta\le2,\end{cases}\]
and equivalently \(X^*(\theta)=\{\min\{\theta,1\}\}\). The value is
\[v(\theta)=\begin{cases}0,&0\le\theta\le1,\\-(1-\theta)^2,&1<\theta\le2.\end{cases}\]
This value is continuous at \(1\) because both pieces equal \(0\) there. The argmax correspondence is singleton-valued and generated by the continuous function \(x^*(\theta)=\min\{\theta,1\}\). Hence it is continuous as a correspondence, and in particular upper hemicontinuous. This also follows from Berge's theorem, since \(\Gamma\) is constant, compact-valued, and continuous, and \(f\) is continuous.

source.notes: Parameter variant emphasizing a binding constraint.

title: Concavity of Cobb-Douglas Utility
learning_objective: Verify concavity of a common economic objective using the Hessian.
preliminaries: A twice continuously differentiable function on a convex open set is concave if its Hessian is negative semidefinite everywhere.
question: Let \(u(x,y)=x^\alpha y^\beta\) on \(\mathbb R^2_{++}\), where \(\alpha>0\), \(\beta>0\), and \(\alpha+\beta\le1\). Prove that \(u\) is concave.
solution:
Compute derivatives: \(u_{xx}=\alpha(\alpha-1)x^{\alpha-2}y^\beta\), \(u_{yy}=\beta(\beta-1)x^\alpha y^{\beta-2}\), and \(u_{xy}=\alpha\beta x^{\alpha-1}y^{\beta-1}\). Since \(\alpha\le1\) and \(\beta\le1\), the diagonal entries are nonpositive. The determinant is
\[u_{xx}u_{yy}-u_{xy}^2=\alpha\beta x^{2\alpha-2}y^{2\beta-2}\left((\alpha-1)(\beta-1)-\alpha\beta\right).\]
The bracket equals \(\alpha\beta-\alpha-\beta+1-\alpha\beta=1-\alpha-\beta\ge0\). Thus the Hessian has nonpositive first diagonal entry and nonnegative determinant. For a symmetric \(2\times2\) matrix, these conditions imply negative semidefiniteness: its quadratic form has eigenvalues that are both nonpositive. Therefore \(H u(x,y)\) is negative semidefinite for all \((x,y)\in\mathbb R^2_{++}\), and \(u\) is concave on the convex domain \(\mathbb R^2_{++}\).

source.notes: Inspired by the Cobb-Douglas Hessian exercise in the differentiation notes.

title: Using Farkas' Lemma to Certify Infeasibility
learning_objective: Use a nonnegative multiplier certificate to prove a linear system has no solution.
preliminaries: One form of Farkas' lemma says exactly one of the following holds: (i) there exists \(x\in\mathbb R^n\) such that \(Ax\le b\); or (ii) there exists \(\lambda\ge0\) such that \(\lambda^\top A=0\) and \(\lambda^\top b<0\).
question: Show that the system \(x_1+x_2\le1\), \(-x_1\le0\), \(-x_2\le0\), and \(-x_1-x_2\le-2\) is infeasible. Provide a Farkas certificate.
solution:
The inequalities say \(x_1+x_2\le1\), \(x_1\ge0\), \(x_2\ge0\), and \(x_1+x_2\ge2\), so infeasibility is clear. A Farkas certificate makes this algebraic. Write \(Ax\le b\) with rows \((1,1)\), \((-1,0)\), \((0,-1)\), \((-1,-1)\), and \(b=(1,0,0,-2)\). Choose \(\lambda=(1,0,0,1)\ge0\). Then
\[\lambda^\top A=(1,1)+(-1,-1)=(0,0),\]
and
\[\lambda^\top b=1+(-2)=-1<0.\]
If a feasible \(x\) existed, multiplying inequalities by \(\lambda\) and summing would give \(0=\lambda^\top A x\le\lambda^\top b=-1\), impossible. Therefore no feasible \(x\) exists.

source.notes: Small infeasibility certificate inspired by the duality notes.

title: Blackwell's Test for a Bellman Operator
learning_objective: Verify contraction of a dynamic programming operator using monotonicity and discounting.
preliminaries: Let \(X\) be compact and let \(C(X)\) be continuous real-valued functions on \(X\) with the sup norm. Blackwell's test says an operator \(T:C(X)\to C(X)\) is a contraction with modulus \(\beta\in(0,1)\) if it is monotone and satisfies discounting: \(T(v+c)\le T(v)+\beta c\) for all constants \(c\ge0\).
question:
Let \(\Gamma(x)\subset X\) be nonempty and compact for each \(x\), and let
\[(Tv)(x)=\max_{y\in\Gamma(x)}\{u(x,y)+\beta v(y)\},\]
where \(\beta\in(0,1)\). Assuming \(T\) maps \(C(X)\) into \(C(X)\), prove that \(T\) is a contraction with modulus \(\beta\).
solution:
First show monotonicity. If \(v\ge w\) pointwise, then for every feasible \(y\), \(u(x,y)+\beta v(y)\ge u(x,y)+\beta w(y)\). Taking maxima over the same feasible set gives \((Tv)(x)\ge(Tw)(x)\) for all \(x\).

Next show discounting. For any constant \(c\ge0\),
\[(T(v+c))(x)=\max_{y\in\Gamma(x)}\{u(x,y)+\beta v(y)+\beta c\}=(Tv)(x)+\beta c.\]
Thus Blackwell's test applies and \(T\) is a contraction with modulus \(\beta\). Equivalently, one can prove the sup-norm inequality directly. Let \(d=\|v-w\|_\infty\). Since \(v\le w+d\), monotonicity and discounting give \(Tv\le T(w+d)=Tw+\beta d\). Since \(w\le v+d\), the same argument gives \(Tw\le Tv+\beta d\). Therefore \(\|Tv-Tw\|_\infty\le\beta\|v-w\|_\infty\).

source.notes: Bellman-operator application of Blackwell's sufficient conditions.

validation.notes: Accepted after clarifying the direct sup-norm contraction argument; monotonicity and discounting proof is correct.

title: Fixed Point on an Interval
learning_objective: Prove the one-dimensional Brouwer fixed point theorem.
preliminaries: A fixed point of \(f:X\to X\) is a point \(x^*\in X\) such that \(f(x^*)=x^*\).
question: Let \(f:[0,1]\to[0,1]\) be continuous. Prove that \(f\) has a fixed point.
solution: Define \(g(x)=f(x)-x\). Since \(f\) is continuous, so is \(g\). Because \(f(0)\in[0,1]\), \(g(0)=f(0)-0\ge0\). Because \(f(1)\in[0,1]\), \(g(1)=f(1)-1\le0\). If \(g(0)=0\), then \(0\) is a fixed point. If \(g(1)=0\), then \(1\) is a fixed point. Otherwise \(g(0)>0>g(1)\), and the intermediate value theorem gives \(x^*\in(0,1)\) with \(g(x^*)=0\). Thus \(f(x^*)=x^*\).

source.notes: One-dimensional fixed point proposition from the notes.

title: Contraction Mapping for an Affine Map
learning_objective: Compute the fixed point of a contraction and prove convergence of iterates.
preliminaries: A self-map \(T\) on a metric space is a contraction with modulus \(r<1\) if \(\rho(Tx,Ty)\le r\rho(x,y)\) for all \(x,y\).
question: Let \(T:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) be \(T(x)=a+\beta x\), where \(|\beta|<1\). Prove that \(T\) is a contraction, find its unique fixed point, and show that the iteration \(x_{n+1}=T(x_n)\) converges to it from any \(x_0\in\mathbb R\).
solution:
For any \(x,y\in\mathbb R\), \(|T(x)-T(y)|=|\beta||x-y|\). Since \(|\beta|<1\), \(T\) is a contraction on the complete metric space \(\mathbb R\). A fixed point satisfies \(x=a+\beta x\), so \(x^*=a/(1-\beta)\). It is unique either by the contraction mapping theorem or directly from this equation.

For convergence, subtract the fixed point equation from the recursion: \(x_{n+1}-x^*=\beta(x_n-x^*)\). By induction, \(x_n-x^*=\beta^n(x_0-x^*)\). Since \(|\beta|<1\), \(\beta^n\to0\), so \(x_n\to x^*\). This explicit calculation matches the constructive content of the contraction mapping theorem.

source.notes: Elementary affine contraction application.

title: Kakutani Fixed Point for an Interval Correspondence
learning_objective: Apply Kakutani's theorem to a simple correspondence and identify fixed points.
preliminaries: Kakutani's theorem: if \(X\subset\mathbb R^d\) is nonempty, compact, and convex, and \(F:X\rightrightarrows X\) is nonempty-valued, closed-valued, convex-valued, and upper hemicontinuous, then some \(x^*\in X\) satisfies \(x^*\in F(x^*)\).
question: Let \(F:[0,1]\rightrightarrows[0,1]\) be \(F(x)=[0,x]\). Verify Kakutani's hypotheses and find all fixed points.
solution:
The domain \([0,1]\) is nonempty, compact, and convex. For each \(x\), \(F(x)=[0,x]\) is nonempty, closed, and convex. Upper hemicontinuity follows as in the interval correspondence argument: if an open set \(O\) contains \([0,x]\), compact containment gives a margin \(\eta>0\) so that every point close enough to \([0,x]\) lies in \(O\); then \([0,x']\subset O\) for \(x'\) sufficiently close to \(x\). Therefore Kakutani guarantees a fixed point.

A fixed point satisfies \(x\in[0,x]\). This holds for every \(x\in[0,1]\), since \(x\le x\) and \(x\ge0\). Hence every point of \([0,1]\) is a fixed point. This illustrates that Kakutani gives existence but not uniqueness.

source.notes: Simple correspondence application of Kakutani's theorem.

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: Why Convex Values Matter in Kakutani's Theorem
learning_objective: Show that dropping convex-valuedness can destroy fixed point existence.
preliminaries: Kakutani's theorem requires the correspondence to have convex values. A subset of \(\mathbb R\) with two separated points is not convex.
question: Define \(F:[0,1]\rightrightarrows[0,1]\) by \(F(x)=\{0\}\) if \(x>1/2\), \(F(x)=\{1\}\) if \(x<1/2\), and \(F(1/2)=\{0,1\}\). Show that \(F\) has nonempty compact values and a closed graph, but no fixed point. Which Kakutani hypothesis fails?
solution:
Each value is finite and nonempty, hence compact. The graph consists of \((x,1)\) for \(x<1/2\), \((1/2,0)\), \((1/2,1)\), and \((x,0)\) for \(x>1/2\). Any convergent sequence in the graph approaching \(x=1/2\) from the left has limit \((1/2,1)\), included; from the right has limit \((1/2,0)\), included. Limits away from \(1/2\) remain on the same horizontal branch. Thus the graph is closed, and for compact-valued correspondences into a compact codomain this is enough for upper hemicontinuity.

There is no fixed point. If \(x<1/2\), then \(F(x)=\{1\}\), so \(x\notin F(x)\). If \(x>1/2\), then \(F(x)=\{0\}\), so \(x\notin F(x)\). If \(x=1/2\), then \(F(x)=\{0,1\}\), which does not contain \(1/2\). The failed Kakutani hypothesis is convex-valuedness at \(x=1/2\), since \(\{0,1\}\) is not convex.

source.notes: Counterexample demonstrating the role of convex-valuedness.

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: Comparative Statics from the Implicit Function Theorem
learning_objective: Differentiate a first-order condition to obtain the response of an optimizer to a parameter.
preliminaries: Suppose \(f\) is \(C^2\), the maximizer \(x^*(\theta)\) is interior and unique near \(\theta_0\), and \(f_{xx}(x^*(\theta_0),\theta_0)\ne0\).
question: Let \(f(x,\theta)=-(x-\theta)^2-a x\), where \(a\in\mathbb R\) is fixed and \(x,\theta\in\mathbb R\). Find \(x^*(\theta)\), compute \(dx^*/d\theta\) directly, and verify the implicit-function formula \(dx^*/d\theta=-f_{x\theta}/f_{xx}\).
solution:
The first-order condition is \(f_x(x,\theta)=-2(x-\theta)-a=0\). Hence \(x^*(\theta)=\theta-a/2\). Directly, \(dx^*/d\theta=1\). The second derivative \(f_{xx}=-2<0\), so the objective is strictly concave in \(x\) and this critical point is the unique global maximizer.

The cross derivative is \(f_{x\theta}=2\). The implicit-function formula gives
\[\frac{dx^*}{d\theta}=-\frac{f_{x\theta}}{f_{xx}}=-\frac{2}{-2}=1,\]
matching the direct derivative. The sign is positive because increasing \(\theta\) shifts the peak of the quadratic objective to the right one-for-one.

source.notes: Clean quadratic application of the IFT comparative-statics formula.

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: First-Order Conditions for a Concave Unconstrained Problem
learning_objective: Use the supporting-gradient inequality for concave functions to prove global optimality.
preliminaries: If \(f\) is differentiable and concave on a convex open set \(X\), then \(f(y)\le f(x)+\nabla f(x)\cdot(y-x)\) for all \(x,y\in X\).
question: Let \(X\subset\mathbb R^d\) be convex and open, and let \(f:X\to\mathbb R\) be differentiable and concave. Prove that \(x^*\in X\) maximizes \(f\) on \(X\) if and only if \(\nabla f(x^*)=0\).
solution:
If \(x^*\) maximizes \(f\) and lies in the open set \(X\), then the usual first-order necessary condition for an interior local maximum gives \(\nabla f(x^*)=0\).

Conversely, suppose \(\nabla f(x^*)=0\). By concavity and differentiability, for every \(y\in X\),
\[f(y)\le f(x^*)+\nabla f(x^*)\cdot(y-x^*)=f(x^*).\]
Thus no feasible \(y\) gives a larger value, so \(x^*\) is a global maximizer. The openness of \(X\) is used for the necessity direction; the sufficiency direction only needs the concavity inequality.

source.notes: Standalone version of the concave first-order characterization.

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: Envelope Theorem in an Interior Problem
learning_objective: Verify that the derivative of the value function equals the partial derivative with respect to the parameter at the optimizer.
preliminaries: For an unconstrained interior problem \(v(\theta)=\max_x f(x,\theta)\), if \(x^*(\theta)\) is differentiable and \(f_x(x^*(\theta),\theta)=0\), then \(v'(\theta)=f_\theta(x^*(\theta),\theta)\).
question: Let \(f(x,\theta)=\theta x-x^2\) with \(x\in\mathbb R\). Compute \(x^*(\theta)\) and \(v(\theta)\). Then verify the envelope formula.
solution:
The first-order condition is \(f_x=\theta-2x=0\), so \(x^*(\theta)=\theta/2\). Since \(f_{xx}=-2<0\), this is the unique global maximizer. The value is
\[v(\theta)=\theta(\theta/2)-(\theta/2)^2=\theta^2/4.\]
Thus \(v'(\theta)=\theta/2\). On the other hand, the partial derivative of the objective with respect to \(\theta\) is \(f_\theta(x,\theta)=x\). Evaluated at the optimizer, this gives \(f_\theta(x^*(\theta),\theta)=\theta/2\), exactly equal to \(v'(\theta)\). The indirect effect through \(x^*\) vanishes because \(f_x(x^*(\theta),\theta)=0\).

source.notes: Direct verification of the lecture notes' envelope theorem formula.

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: KKT with Nonnegativity on a Quarter Circle
learning_objective: Handle boundary constraints with KKT conditions.
preliminaries: Use the maximization Lagrangian \(L=f+\mu h+\lambda_1 x+\lambda_2 y\) for constraints \(h=0\), \(x\ge0\), and \(y\ge0\), with \(\lambda_1,\lambda_2\ge0\) and complementary slackness \(\lambda_1x=\lambda_2y=0\).
question: Solve \(\max x-y^2\) subject to \(x^2+y^2=4\), \(x\ge0\), and \(y\ge0\). Verify the KKT multipliers at the solution.
solution:
The feasible set is the quarter circle, compact, so a solution exists. Since \(x\in[0,2]\) and \(-y^2\le0\), the objective satisfies \(x-y^2\le x\le2\). The point \((2,0)\) is feasible and attains value \(2\), so it is the global maximizer.

Verify KKT. With \(h=x^2+y^2-4\), the Lagrangian is \(L=x-y^2+\mu(x^2+y^2-4)+\lambda_1x+\lambda_2y\). Stationarity requires
\[1+2\mu x+\lambda_1=0,\qquad -2y+2\mu y+\lambda_2=0.\]
At \((2,0)\), complementary slackness gives \(\lambda_1\cdot2=0\), so \(\lambda_1=0\). The first stationarity equation becomes \(1+4\mu=0\), so \(\mu=-1/4\). The second stationarity equation becomes \(\lambda_2=0\). Nonnegativity \(\lambda_1,\lambda_2\ge0\) holds, feasibility holds, and complementary slackness holds. Thus \((x,y,\mu,\lambda_1,\lambda_2)=(2,0,-1/4,0,0)\) satisfies KKT and is the global solution.

source.notes: Uses the quarter-circle KKT example from the lecture notes.

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: KKT Sufficiency in a Concave Program
learning_objective: Prove that KKT conditions are sufficient for global optimality in a concave maximization problem.
preliminaries: Consider \(\max_{x\in X} f(x)\) subject to \(g_j(x)\ge0\) for \(j=1,\ldots,J\), where \(X\) is convex, \(f\) and all \(g_j\) are concave and differentiable. Let \(L(x,\lambda)=f(x)+\sum_j\lambda_j g_j(x)\), with \(\lambda_j\ge0\).
question: Suppose a feasible \(x^*\in X\) and multipliers \(\lambda^*\ge0\) satisfy stationarity \(\nabla_x L(x^*,\lambda^*)=0\) and complementary slackness \(\lambda_j^*g_j(x^*)=0\) for all \(j\). Prove that \(x^*\) is a global maximizer.
solution:
Because \(f\) and each \(g_j\) are concave and \(\lambda_j^*\ge0\), the Lagrangian \(x\mapsto L(x,\lambda^*)\) is concave. Stationarity on the convex domain gives global maximization of the Lagrangian: for every \(x\in X\),
\[L(x,\lambda^*)\le L(x^*,\lambda^*).\]
At \(x^*\), complementary slackness implies \(L(x^*,\lambda^*)=f(x^*)+\sum_j\lambda_j^*g_j(x^*)=f(x^*)\). If \(x\) is feasible, then \(g_j(x)\ge0\) and \(\lambda_j^*\ge0\), so \(L(x,\lambda^*)=f(x)+\sum_j\lambda_j^*g_j(x)\ge f(x)\). Combining, \(f(x)\le L(x,\lambda^*)\le L(x^*,\lambda^*)=f(x^*)\). Thus every feasible \(x\) has objective no larger than \(f(x^*)\), so \(x^*\) is globally optimal. Slater-type conditions are used for necessity, not for this sufficiency proof.

source.notes: Abstract sufficiency proof connected to the notes' KKT conditions.

title: Lagrange Multipliers on a Circle
learning_objective: Solve a smooth equality-constrained problem and check candidates.
preliminaries:
For an equality constraint \(h(x,y)=0\), a regular local optimum with \(
abla h
e0\) satisfies \(
abla f+\mu
abla h=0\).
question: Solve \(\max_{x,y\in\mathbb R} x-y^2\) subject to \(x^2+y^2=4\). Identify all Lagrange candidates and the global maximizer.
solution:
The feasible set is the circle of radius \(2\), compact, so a maximizer exists. Let \(f(x,y)=x-y^2\) and \(h(x,y)=x^2+y^2-4\). The Lagrangian condition is
\[1+2\mu x=0,\qquad -2y+2\mu y=0,\qquad x^2+y^2=4.\]
The second equation gives \(2y(\mu-1)=0\). If \(y=0\), then \(x=\pm2\). The first equation gives \(\mu=-1/(2x)\), so candidates \((2,0)\) and \((-2,0)\). If \(\mu=1\), then the first equation gives \(1+2x=0\), so \(x=-1/2\). The constraint gives \(y^2=4-1/4=15/4\), so \(y=\pm\sqrt{15}/2\).

Evaluate the objective: \(f(2,0)=2\), \(f(-2,0)=-2\), and \(f(-1/2,\pm\sqrt{15}/2)=-1/2-15/4=-17/4\). Therefore the global maximizer is \((2,0)\), with maximum value \(2\).

source.notes: Equality-constrained variant of the static optimization example.
