title: The Cauchy Criterion in \(\mathbb R\)
learning_objective: Prove that Cauchy sequences converge in the real line.
preliminaries: A sequence \((x_n)\) is Cauchy if for every \(\varepsilon>0\), there is \(N\) such that \(|x_n-x_m|<\varepsilon\) whenever \(m,n\ge N\).
question: Prove that every Cauchy sequence in \(\mathbb R\) converges. Your proof may use the Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem.
solution:
Let \((x_n)\) be Cauchy. First show it is bounded. Take \(\varepsilon=1\). There exists \(N\) such that \(|x_n-x_N|<1\) for all \(n\ge N\). The finitely many terms \(x_1,\ldots,x_{N-1}\) are bounded, and all later terms lie in \((x_N-1,x_N+1)\), so the whole sequence is bounded.

By Bolzano-Weierstrass, \((x_n)\) has a convergent subsequence \((x_{n_k})\) with limit \(x\in\mathbb R\). We show the whole sequence converges to \(x\). Let \(\varepsilon>0\). Since \((x_n)\) is Cauchy, choose \(N_1\) such that \(|x_n-x_m|<\varepsilon/2\) whenever \(m,n\ge N_1\). Since \(x_{n_k}\to x\), choose \(k\) with \(n_k\ge N_1\) and \(|x_{n_k}-x|<\varepsilon/2\). Then for every \(n\ge N_1\), \(|x_n-x|\le |x_n-x_{n_k}|+|x_{n_k}-x|<\varepsilon\). Thus \(x_n\to x\).

source.notes: Follows the local notes' proof strategy for the Cauchy criterion.

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: A Cauchy Sequence in \(\mathbb Q\) with No Rational Limit
learning_objective: Understand why completeness of the ambient space matters for the Cauchy criterion.
preliminaries: A metric subspace can have Cauchy sequences that do not converge inside the subspace. Use the usual absolute-value metric on \(\mathbb Q\).
question: Construct a sequence of rational numbers that is Cauchy as a sequence in \((\mathbb Q,|\cdot|)\) but does not converge to any rational number.
solution:
For each \(n\), use density of \(\mathbb Q\) to choose \(q_n\in\mathbb Q\) with \(|q_n-\sqrt 2|<1/n\). Then \((q_n)\) converges to \(\sqrt 2\) in \(\mathbb R\), hence it is Cauchy in \(\mathbb R\): for large \(m,n\), \(|q_n-q_m|\le |q_n-\sqrt 2|+|q_m-\sqrt 2|\) is small. Since the metric on \(\mathbb Q\) is the restriction of the same absolute value metric, \((q_n)\) is also Cauchy in \(\mathbb Q\).

Suppose it converged in \(\mathbb Q\) to some \(q\in\mathbb Q\). Limits in \(\mathbb R\) are unique, and the same sequence already converges to \(\sqrt 2\) in \(\mathbb R\). Therefore \(q=\sqrt 2\), impossible because \(\sqrt 2\notin\mathbb Q\). Thus \((q_n)\) is Cauchy in \(\mathbb Q\) but has no rational limit.

source.notes: Uses the notes' contrast between \(\mathbb R\) and \(\mathbb Q\).

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: Convergence in \(\mathbb R^d\) and Coordinate Limits
learning_objective: Relate Euclidean convergence to convergence of each coordinate.
preliminaries: For \(x=(x_1,\ldots,x_d)\in\mathbb R^d\), use the Euclidean norm \(\|x\|=(\sum_{i=1}^d x_i^2)^{1/2}\).
question: Let \((x_n)\) be a sequence in \(\mathbb R^d\), with \(x_n=(x_{n,1},\ldots,x_{n,d})\), and let \(x=(x_1,\ldots,x_d)\). Prove that \(x_n\to x\) in Euclidean norm if and only if \(x_{n,i}\to x_i\) in \(\mathbb R\) for every coordinate \(i\).
solution:
If \(x_n\to x\), then for each coordinate \(i\), \(|x_{n,i}-x_i|\le \|x_n-x\|\). Hence \(|x_{n,i}-x_i|\to 0\), so \(x_{n,i}\to x_i\).

Conversely, suppose \(x_{n,i}\to x_i\) for each \(i\). Let \(\varepsilon>0\). For each \(i\), choose \(N_i\) such that \(|x_{n,i}-x_i|<\varepsilon/\sqrt d\) whenever \(n\ge N_i\). Let \(N=\max_i N_i\). Then for \(n\ge N\),
\[\|x_n-x\|^2=\sum_{i=1}^d |x_{n,i}-x_i|^2<d\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{\sqrt d}\right)^2=\varepsilon^2.\]
Thus \(\|x_n-x\|<\varepsilon\), so \(x_n\to x\).

source.notes: Based on the coordinate characterization of Euclidean sequence convergence.

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: Convergence from Equal Lim Sup and Lim Inf
learning_objective: Show that equality of limit superior and limit inferior characterizes convergence.
preliminaries: For a bounded real sequence \((x_n)\), define \(a_n=\sup\{x_k:k\ge n\}\) and \(b_n=\inf\{x_k:k\ge n\}\). Then \(\limsup x_n=\lim a_n\) and \(\liminf x_n=\lim b_n\).
question: Let \((x_n)\) be a bounded real sequence. Prove that if \(\limsup x_n=\liminf x_n=L\), then \(x_n\to L\). Give an example of a bounded sequence for which the two values differ.
solution:
For every \(n\), the tail infimum and supremum satisfy \(b_n\le x_n\le a_n\). Since \(a_n\to L\) and \(b_n\to L\), the squeeze theorem implies \(x_n\to L\): for any \(\varepsilon>0\), choose \(N_1\) such that \(a_n<L+\varepsilon\) for \(n\ge N_1\), and choose \(N_2\) such that \(b_n>L-\varepsilon\) for \(n\ge N_2\). If \(n\ge \max\{N_1,N_2\}\), then \(L-\varepsilon<x_n<L+\varepsilon\).

For an example with different values, let \(x_n=(-1)^n\). Every tail contains both \(-1\) and \(1\), so \(a_n=1\) and \(b_n=-1\) for all \(n\). Thus \(\limsup x_n=1\) and \(\liminf x_n=-1\), and the sequence does not converge.

source.notes: Inspired by the proposition equating convergence with equality of lim sup and lim inf.

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: Monotone Convergence from Completeness
learning_objective: Use completeness to prove convergence of a bounded monotone sequence.
preliminaries: A sequence \((x_n)\) is nondecreasing if \(x_n\le x_{n+1}\) for all \(n\). It is bounded above if there exists \(M\) such that \(x_n\le M\) for all \(n\).
question: Let \((x_n)\) be a nondecreasing real sequence that is bounded above. Prove that \((x_n)\) converges and identify its limit. Then prove the analogous statement for nonincreasing sequences bounded below.
solution:
Let \(S=\{x_n:n\in\mathbb N\}\). The set \(S\) is nonempty and bounded above, so completeness gives \(s=\sup S\). We prove \(x_n\to s\). Let \(\varepsilon>0\). Since \(s\) is the least upper bound, \(s-\varepsilon\) is not an upper bound of \(S\), so there exists \(N\) with \(x_N>s-\varepsilon\). Because the sequence is nondecreasing, for every \(n\ge N\), \(x_n\ge x_N>s-\varepsilon\). Since \(s\) is an upper bound, \(x_n\le s<s+\varepsilon\). Therefore \(|x_n-s|<\varepsilon\) for all \(n\ge N\), and \(x_n\to s=\sup\{x_n:n\in\mathbb N\}\).

If \((y_n)\) is nonincreasing and bounded below, let \(t=\inf\{y_n:n\in\mathbb N\}\). Equivalently, \((-y_n)\) is nondecreasing and bounded above, so \(-y_n\to \sup\{-y_n:n\in\mathbb N\}=-t\). Hence \(y_n\to t\).

source.notes: Based on the monotone convergence result for real sequences.

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: Supremum and the Epsilon Characterization
learning_objective: Prove and use the epsilon characterization of the least upper bound.
preliminaries: Let \(S\subset \mathbb R\) be nonempty and bounded above. A number \(b\in\mathbb R\) is \(\sup S\) if \(b\) is an upper bound for \(S\) and no smaller number is an upper bound.
question: Prove that \(b=\sup S\) if and only if both conditions hold: (i) \(s\le b\) for every \(s\in S\); and (ii) for every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists \(s_\varepsilon\in S\) such that \(b-\varepsilon<s_\varepsilon\le b\). Then apply the result to compute \(\sup\{x\in\mathbb Q:x^2<2\}\) as a subset of \(\mathbb R\).
solution:
First suppose \(b=\sup S\). Then \(b\) is an upper bound, so (i) holds. If (ii) failed, there would be some \(\varepsilon>0\) such that \(s\le b-\varepsilon\) for all \(s\in S\). Then \(b-\varepsilon\) would be an upper bound smaller than \(b\), contradicting leastness. Conversely, suppose (i) and (ii) hold. Condition (i) says \(b\) is an upper bound. If \(c<b\), let \(\varepsilon=b-c>0\). By (ii), some \(s_\varepsilon\in S\) satisfies \(c=b-\varepsilon<s_\varepsilon\). Thus \(c\) is not an upper bound. Hence no number smaller than \(b\) is an upper bound, so \(b=\sup S\).

For \(A=\{x\in\mathbb Q:x^2<2\}\), the upper bound should be \(\sqrt 2\). If \(x\in A\) and \(x\ge 0\), then \(x^2<2\) gives \(x<\sqrt 2\); if \(x<0\), then also \(x<\sqrt 2\). Thus \(\sqrt 2\) is an upper bound. For any \(\varepsilon>0\), density of \(\mathbb Q\) in \(\mathbb R\) gives rational \(q\) with \(\max\{0,\sqrt 2-\varepsilon\}<q<\sqrt 2\). Then \(q^2<2\), so \(q\in A\), and \(q>\sqrt 2-\varepsilon\). Therefore \(\sup A=\sqrt 2\) in \(\mathbb R\).

title: Closed Sets and Limits of Sequences
learning_objective: Prove the sequential characterization of closed subsets of Euclidean space.
preliminaries: A set \(S\subset\mathbb R^d\) is closed if its complement is open. A sequence \((x_n)\) in \(S\) converges to \(x\) if \(\|x_n-x\|\to 0\).
question: Prove that \(S\subset\mathbb R^d\) is closed if and only if every sequence \((x_n)\) in \(S\) that converges in \(\mathbb R^d\) has its limit in \(S\).
solution:
Assume \(S\) is closed and let \(x_n\in S\) with \(x_n\to x\). If \(x\notin S\), then \(x\in S^c\). Since \(S^c\) is open, there is \(\varepsilon>0\) with \(B_\varepsilon(x)\subset S^c\). For large \(n\), \(x_n\in B_\varepsilon(x)\), contradicting \(x_n\in S\). Hence \(x\in S\).

Conversely, assume every convergent sequence in \(S\) has limit in \(S\). If \(S\) were not closed, then \(S^c\) would not be open. Hence there exists \(x\in S^c\) such that every ball \(B_{1/n}(x)\) intersects \(S\). Choose \(x_n\in S\cap B_{1/n}(x)\). Then \(\|x_n-x\|<1/n\), so \(x_n\to x\). By the assumed sequential property, \(x\in S\), contradicting \(x\in S^c\). Therefore \(S\) is closed.

source.notes: Rewrites the sequential closedness proposition as a standalone proof question.

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: Open Covers Showing Noncompactness
learning_objective: Construct open covers with no finite subcover for sets that fail closedness or boundedness.
preliminaries: A set is compact if every open cover has a finite subcover. In \(\mathbb R^d\), compactness is equivalent to closedness and boundedness.
question: For each set, give an explicit open cover with no finite subcover: (a) \((0,1]\subset\mathbb R\); (b) \([0,\infty)\subset\mathbb R\). Prove your covers have no finite subcover.
solution:
For \((0,1]\), use the cover \(\mathcal U=\{(1/n,2):n\in\mathbb N, n\ge 2\}\). Every \(x\in(0,1]\) is covered: choose \(n>1/x\), then \(1/n<x<2\). A finite subcollection has indices \(n_1,\ldots,n_k\). Let \(N=\max_i n_i\). The union is \((1/N,2)\) because the interval with largest \(n\) starts furthest left. Any \(x\in(0,1/N]\) is in \((0,1]\) but not in the finite union. Hence no finite subcover exists.

For \([0,\infty)\), use \(\mathcal V=\{(-1,n):n\in\mathbb N\}\). This covers \([0,\infty)\), because every \(x\ge 0\) lies in \((-1,n)\) for some integer \(n>x\). A finite subcollection has largest endpoint \(N\), so its union is \((-1,N)\), which misses every \(x\ge N\). Thus no finite subcover exists.

source.notes: Uses the requested noncompactness examples from the topology notes.

title: Sequential and Epsilon-Delta Continuity
learning_objective: Prove equivalence between sequential and epsilon-delta continuity in \(\mathbb R\).
preliminaries: Let \(S\subset\mathbb R\), \(x_0\in S\), and \(f:S\to\mathbb R\). Epsilon-delta continuity at \(x_0\) means: for every \(\varepsilon>0\), there is \(\delta>0\) such that \(x\in S\) and \(|x-x_0|<\delta\) imply \(|f(x)-f(x_0)|<\varepsilon\).
question: Prove that \(f\) is epsilon-delta continuous at \(x_0\) if and only if for every sequence \((x_n)\) in \(S\) with \(x_n\to x_0\), we have \(f(x_n)\to f(x_0)\).
solution:
Assume epsilon-delta continuity. Let \(x_n\to x_0\) in \(S\). Given \(\varepsilon>0\), choose \(\delta>0\) from continuity. Since \(x_n\to x_0\), there is \(N\) such that \(|x_n-x_0|<\delta\) for \(n\ge N\). Then \(|f(x_n)-f(x_0)|<\varepsilon\) for \(n\ge N\), so \(f(x_n)\to f(x_0)\).

Conversely, suppose the sequential condition holds but epsilon-delta continuity fails. Then there exists \(\varepsilon_0>0\) such that for every \(\delta>0\), some \(x\in S\) satisfies \(|x-x_0|<\delta\) and \(|f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\). For each \(n\), choose \(x_n\in S\) with \(|x_n-x_0|<1/n\) and \(|f(x_n)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\). Then \(x_n\to x_0\), but \(f(x_n)\) does not converge to \(f(x_0)\), contradiction. Hence epsilon-delta continuity holds.

source.notes: Standalone version of the notes' equivalence proof.

validation.notes: Accepted: independently checked standalone assumptions, LaTeX-displayable formatting, and correctness of the full solution.

title: Continuity at an Isolated Point
learning_objective: Recognize that every function is continuous at an isolated point of its domain.
preliminaries: A point \(x_0\in S\subset\mathbb R\) is isolated in \(S\) if there is \(r>0\) such that \(B_r(x_0)\cap S=\{x_0\}\). Continuity is understood relative to the domain \(S\).
question: Let \(S=\{1\}\cup[2,3]\). Prove that every function \(f:S\to\mathbb R\) is continuous at \(1\). Does the same claim necessarily hold at \(2\)?
solution:
Since \(1\) is isolated in \(S\), take \(r=1/2\). Then \((1-r,1+r)\cap S=\{1\}\). Let \(\varepsilon>0\). Choose \(\delta=r\). If \(x\in S\) and \(|x-1|<\delta\), then \(x=1\), so \(|f(x)-f(1)|=0<\varepsilon\). Thus \(f\) is continuous at \(1\), regardless of how it is defined elsewhere.

The same claim does not necessarily hold at \(2\), because \(2\) is not isolated in \(S\). For example, define \(f(2)=1\) and \(f(x)=0\) for \(x\in S\setminus\{2\}\). Take \(x_n=2+1/n\in S\). Then \(x_n\to2\), but \(f(x_n)=0\not\to1=f(2)\). Therefore this particular \(f\) is not continuous at \(2\).

title: Extreme Value Theorem via Compactness
learning_objective: Use compactness to prove existence of maximizers for continuous functions.
preliminaries: You may use that every sequence in a compact subset of \(\mathbb R^d\) has a convergent subsequence with limit in the set.
question: Let \(K\subset\mathbb R^d\) be nonempty and compact, and let \(f:K\to\mathbb R\) be continuous. Prove that \(f\) attains a maximum and a minimum on \(K\).
solution:
We prove existence of a maximum; the minimum follows by applying the same argument to \(-f\). Let \(M=\sup f(K)\) in the extended real line. First, \(f(K)\) is bounded above. If not, for each \(n\) choose \(x_n\in K\) with \(f(x_n)>n\). Compactness gives a subsequence \(x_{n_k}\to x\in K\). Continuity implies \(f(x_{n_k})\to f(x)\), which is finite, contradicting \(f(x_{n_k})>n_k\to\infty\).

Since \(M\) is finite, for each \(n\) choose \(x_n\in K\) with \(f(x_n)>M-1/n\). Compactness gives a subsequence \(x_{n_k}\to x^*\in K\). By continuity, \(f(x_{n_k})\to f(x^*)\). Since \(M-1/n_k<f(x_{n_k})\le M\), the squeeze theorem gives \(f(x_{n_k})\to M\). Hence \(f(x^*)=M\), so \(x^*\) maximizes \(f\). Applying this to \(-f\) gives a minimizer.

source.notes: Builds the Weierstrass result used repeatedly in the optimization notes.

title: Existence of a Best Alternative on a Finite Set
learning_objective: Prove that complete and transitive preferences have a maximal element on any finite nonempty set.
preliminaries: Let \(A=\{a_1,\ldots,a_N\}\) be finite and nonempty. A rational preference relation \(\succeq\) is complete and transitive. An alternative \(a^*\in A\) is best in \(A\) if \(a^*\succeq a_i\) for every \(a_i\in A\).
question: Show that a best alternative exists for this decision maker and finite set \(A\).
solution: Proceed by induction on the number of alternatives. For one alternative, that alternative is best. Suppose every set with \(N-1\) alternatives has a best element. Let \(A=\{a_1,\ldots,a_N\}\) and let \(B=\{a_1,\ldots,a_{N-1}\}\). By the induction hypothesis, there is \(b^*\in B\) such that \(b^*\succeq b\) for every \(b\in B\). By completeness, either \(b^*\succeq a_N\) or \(a_N\succeq b^*\), or both. In the first case, \(b^*\) is best in \(A\). In the second case, transitivity gives \(a_N\succeq b^*\succeq b\) for every \(b\in B\), so \(a_N\) is best in \(A\). Thus a best alternative exists for every finite nonempty set.

source.original_question: Show that a best alternative exists for a rational decision maker on a finite set A.

title: Deleting an Alternative Cannot Hurt a Single Decision Maker
learning_objective: Show that removing an alternative from a feasible set cannot make a rational single-agent chooser worse off relative to the original best alternative.
preliminaries: Let \(A=\{a_1,…,a_N\}\) and \(A'=\{a_1,…,a_{N-1}\}\). Let \(a^*\) be a best alternative in \(A\), and let \(a'\) be a best alternative in \(A'\), under the same complete and transitive preference relation \(\succeq\).
question: Show that \(a^*\succeq a'\), and interpret the result.
solution: Because \(A'\subseteq A\), the alternative \(a'\in A'\) is also an element of \(A\). Since \(a^*\) is best in \(A\), it is weakly preferred to every element of \(A\). In particular, \[a^*\succeq a'.\] Thus the best attainable alternative from the original larger set is at least as good as the best attainable alternative after deleting \(a_N\). For an individual decision maker with fixed rational preferences, reducing the feasible set cannot improve the best element relative to what was already available in the larger set; the original best could always have been chosen before the deletion.

source.original_question: Let a' be best in A' after deleting a_N. Show that a* is weakly preferred to a'.

title: Choice from a Union of Menus
learning_objective: Prove a menu-union identity for choices generated by a rational preference relation.
preliminaries: Let \(X\) be a nonempty finite set and let \(\succeq\) be complete and transitive on \(X\). For a nonempty menu \(A\subseteq X\), define \(C(A,\succeq)=\{x\in A:x\succeq a\text{ for all }a\in A\}\).
question: Is the claim \(C(A\cup B,\succeq)=C(C(A,\succeq)\cup C(B,\succeq),\succeq)\) correct for all nonempty \(A,B\subseteq X\)? Prove or give a counterexample.
solution:
The claim is correct. First, take \(x\in C(A\cup B,\succeq)\). Then \(x\succeq y\) for every \(y\in A\cup B\), so in particular \(x\) is at least as good as every element of \(C(A,\succeq)\cup C(B,\succeq)\). Since \(x\in A\cup B\), if \(x\in A\) then \(x\in C(A,\succeq)\), and if \(x\in B\) then \(x\in C(B,\succeq)\). Hence \(x\) belongs to the right-hand choice set.

Conversely, take \(x\in C(C(A,\succeq)\cup C(B,\succeq),\succeq)\). Then \(x\) is a best element of either \(A\) or \(B\), so \(x\in A\cup B\). For any \(y\in A\cup B\), if \(y\in A\), choose some \(a^*\in C(A,\succeq)\). Since \(a^*\succeq y\) and \(x\succeq a^*\), transitivity gives \(x\succeq y\). The same argument applies if \(y\in B\), using an element of \(C(B,\succeq)\). Thus \(x\succeq y\) for all \(y\in A\cup B\), so \(x\in C(A\cup B,\succeq)\).

source.original_question: For rational preferences on finite X, is C(A union B)=C(C(A) union C(B)) correct? Provide a proof or counterexample.
source.notes: Converted from the answer key; reverse inclusion proof expanded to use transitivity correctly.

validation.notes: Both inclusions verified; finite nonempty menus guarantee nonempty choice sets.

title: Independence Axiom and a Common Consequence Test
learning_objective: Use the independence axiom to identify an expected-utility restriction on lottery choices.
preliminaries: The independence axiom says that for lotteries \(A,B,C\) and \(\alpha\in(0,1)\), \(A\succeq B\) if and only if \(\alpha A+(1-\alpha)C\succeq \alpha B+(1-\alpha)C\).
question: Let prizes be dollars. Define \(A=(1\text{ with prob. }1)\), \(B=(5\text{ with prob. }0.1;1\text{ with prob. }0.89;0\text{ with prob. }0.01)\), \(C=(1\text{ with prob. }0.11;0\text{ with prob. }0.89)\), and \(D=(5\text{ with prob. }0.1;0\text{ with prob. }0.9)\). Show that preferences \(A\succ B\) and \(D\succ C\) violate expected utility independence.
solution: Write the first pair as mixtures with a common consequence. Let \(E\) be the sure prize \(1\), \(F\) the lottery paying \(5\) with probability \(10/11\) and \(0\) with probability \(1/11\), and \(G\) the sure prize \(0\). Then \(A=0.11E+0.89E\), while \(B=0.11F+0.89E\). Thus \(A\succ B\) implies, by independence after removing the common \(0.89E\), that \(E\succ F\). The second pair is \(C=0.11E+0.89G\) and \(D=0.11F+0.89G\). If \(E\succ F\), independence implies \(C\succ D\), not \(D\succ C\). Therefore the pattern \(A\succ B\) and \(D\succ C\) cannot be represented by expected utility preferences satisfying independence.

title: Arrow-Pratt Approximation to the Risk Premium
learning_objective: Use local absolute risk aversion to approximate the risk premium of a small zero-mean gamble.
preliminaries: For Bernoulli utility \(u\), absolute risk aversion is \(A(w)=-u''(w)/u'(w)\). For a small zero-mean risk \(\varepsilon\) with variance \(\sigma^2\), the risk premium is approximately \(\rho\approx \frac{1}{2}A(w)\sigma^2\).
question: Let \(u(w)=\sqrt{w}\), initial wealth \(w=100\), and a zero-mean gamble \(\varepsilon\) that is \(+10\) or \(-10\) with equal probability. Compute the Arrow-Pratt approximation to the risk premium.
solution: For \(u(w)=w^{1/2}\), \(u'(w)=1/(2\sqrt{w})\) and \(u''(w)=-1/(4w^{3/2})\). Thus \(A(w)=-u''(w)/u'(w)=1/(2w)\). At \(w=100\), \(A(100)=1/200\). The gamble has mean zero and variance \(E[\varepsilon^2]=100\). The Arrow-Pratt approximation is \(\rho\approx\frac{1}{2}(1/200)(100)=1/4\). Thus the consumer would approximately give up \(0.25\) units of wealth to avoid this small risk. The approximation is local; it becomes more accurate as the gamble size shrinks relative to wealth.

title: Affine Transformations of Expected Utility
learning_objective: Show why expected utility representations are unique only up to positive affine transformations.
preliminaries: A finite lottery \(L\) gives prize \(x_i\) with probability \(q_i\). An expected utility representation evaluates \(L\) by \(EU_u(L)=\sum_i q_i u(x_i)\).
question: Suppose \(u\) represents preferences over lotteries by expected utility. Show that \(v(x)=a u(x)+b\), with \(a>0\), represents the same preferences by expected utility. Explain why an arbitrary increasing nonlinear transform need not preserve expected utility rankings.
solution: For any lottery \(L\), \(EU_v(L)=\sum_i q_i(au(x_i)+b)=a\sum_i q_i u(x_i)+b\sum_i q_i=a EU_u(L)+b\). Therefore \(EU_v(L)\ge EU_v(M)\) if and only if \(aEU_u(L)+b\ge aEU_u(M)+b\), which holds if and only if \(EU_u(L)\ge EU_u(M)\), since \(a>0\). Thus \(v\) gives the same ranking. A nonlinear increasing transform \(f(u)\) generally fails because \(\sum_i q_i f(u(x_i))\) is not an increasing transformation of \(\sum_i q_i u(x_i)\). Expectations and nonlinear functions do not commute, so the transformed index can change attitudes toward mixtures and hence lottery rankings.

title: Full Insurance at an Actuarially Fair Premium
learning_objective: Show that a risk-averse expected utility maximizer fully insures at actuarially fair odds.
preliminaries: Initial wealth is \(W\). A loss \(L>0\) occurs with probability \(q\in(0,1)\). The consumer buys coverage \(I\in[0,L]\) at premium \(qI\). Bernoulli utility \(u\) is strictly increasing and strictly concave.
question: Prove that the optimal insurance choice is \(I=L\).
solution: Final wealth if no loss occurs is \(W-qI\). Final wealth if loss occurs is \(W-L+I-qI=W-L+(1-q)I\). Expected wealth is \((1-q)(W-qI)+q(W-L+(1-q)I)=W-qL\), independent of \(I\), because the premium is actuarially fair. The choice of \(I\) only changes the spread of wealth across states. Full insurance \(I=L\) gives wealth \(W-qL\) in both states. For any other \(I\), final wealth has the same mean but is nonconstant. By strict concavity, Jensen's inequality gives \(E[u(W_s)]<u(E[W_s])=u(W-qL)\) for any nonconstant wealth distribution. Therefore full insurance uniquely maximizes expected utility.

title: Log Utility Portfolio Choice
learning_objective: Solve a simple risky-asset share problem under log utility.
preliminaries: An investor has wealth \(W>0\). A riskless asset has gross return \(1\). A risky asset has gross return \(R_H=2\) with probability \(1/2\) and \(R_L=1/2\) with probability \(1/2\). The investor puts share \(a\in[0,1]\) in the risky asset and maximizes expected \(\ln\) final wealth.
question: Find the optimal risky share \(a\).
solution: Final wealth is \(W(1+a(R-1))\). Since \(\ln W\) is constant, maximize \(f(a)=\frac{1}{2}\ln(1+a)+\frac{1}{2}\ln(1-a/2)\) over \([0,1]\). The derivative is \(f'(a)=\frac{1}{2}(1/(1+a))-\frac{1}{4}(1/(1-a/2))\). Setting \(f'(a)=0\) gives \(2/(1+a)=1/(1-a/2)\), so \(2-a=1+a\), hence \(a=1/2\). The second derivative \(f''(a)=-\frac{1}{2}(1+a)^{-2}-\frac{1}{8}(1-a/2)^{-2}<0\), so this is the unique maximizer. Boundary derivatives also confirm an interior solution: \(f'(0)=1/4>0\) and \(f'(1)<0\).

title: Risk Premium with Log Utility
learning_objective: Compute a certainty equivalent and risk premium for a binary wealth lottery.
preliminaries: A consumer with Bernoulli utility \(u(w)=\ln w\) evaluates lotteries by expected utility. The certainty equivalent \(CE\) solves \(u(CE)=EU\). The risk premium is expected wealth minus \(CE\).
question: Initial wealth is \(100\). A fair risk adds \(+50\) or \(-50\) with equal probability. Compute expected utility, certainty equivalent, and risk premium.
solution: Final wealth is \(150\) or \(50\), each with probability \(1/2\). Expected utility is \(EU=(1/2)\ln150+(1/2)\ln50=\ln\sqrt{7500}\). The certainty equivalent solves \(\ln CE=\ln\sqrt{7500}\), so \(CE=\sqrt{7500}=50\sqrt{3}\approx86.60\). Expected wealth is \((150+50)/2=100\). Hence the risk premium is \(100-50\sqrt{3}\approx13.40\). Since \(\ln\) is strictly concave, Jensen's inequality implies \(CE<E[w]=100\), so the positive risk premium is consistent with risk aversion.

title: Positive Expected Return Need Not Attract a Log-Utility Investor
learning_objective: Show that positive expected monetary return is not sufficient for project acceptance under log utility.
preliminaries: An expected-utility maximizer has initial wealth \(1\) and Bernoulli utility \(u(w)=\log w\). A project pays \(\alpha>0\) with probability \(1/2\) and \(\beta>0\) with probability \(1/2\). Not investing leaves wealth \(1\).
question: Is \((\alpha+\beta)/2>1\) sufficient to guarantee that the individual accepts the all-or-nothing project?
solution: No. Expected utility from not investing is \(\log 1=0\). Expected utility from investing is \[\frac{1}{2}\log\alpha+\frac{1}{2}\log\beta=\log\sqrt{\alpha\beta}.\] The investor accepts only if \(\alpha\beta>1\). Positive expected return requires \(\alpha+\beta>2\), which does not imply \(\alpha\beta>1\). For example, \(\alpha=4\) and \(\beta=1/8\) give \((\alpha+\beta)/2=17/8>1\), but \(\alpha\beta=1/2<1\), so expected utility from investing is negative. Thus positive expected return is not sufficient.

title: Necessary and Sufficient Acceptance Condition under Log Utility
learning_objective: Derive the exact acceptance condition for a binary risky project under log utility.
preliminaries: An investor has utility \(\log w\), initial wealth \(1\), and can either keep wealth \(1\) or invest in a project paying \(\alpha>0\) or \(\beta>0\), each with probability \(1/2\).
question: Give a necessary and sufficient condition on \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) for accepting the project strictly.
solution: The investor is indifferent to not investing at utility \(0\). Investing gives expected utility \[EU=\frac{1}{2}\log\alpha+\frac{1}{2}\log\beta=\log\sqrt{\alpha\beta}.\] Because \(\log\) is strictly increasing, \(EU>0\) if and only if \(\sqrt{\alpha\beta}>1\), equivalently \(\alpha\beta>1\). If \(\alpha\beta<1\), the project is rejected; if \(\alpha\beta=1\), the investor is indifferent. Thus strict acceptance is equivalent to \(\alpha\beta>1\).

title: Positive Fractional Investment under Log Utility
learning_objective: Show that positive expected return implies a positive optimal investment share from zero.
preliminaries: The investor has initial wealth \(1\), utility \(\log w\), and may invest fraction \(\delta\in[0,1]\). Wealth is \(1-\delta+\delta\alpha\) with probability \(1/2\) and \(1-\delta+\delta\beta\) with probability \(1/2\). Assume \((\alpha+\beta)/2>1\) and wealth remains positive for small \(\delta\).
question: Will the investor choose a positive fraction of wealth to invest?
solution: Expected utility is \[V(\delta)=\frac{1}{2}\log(1+\delta(\alpha-1))+\frac{1}{2}\log(1+\delta(\beta-1)).\] At \(\delta=0\), \(V(0)=0\). The right derivative at zero is \[V'(0)=\frac{1}{2}(\alpha-1)+\frac{1}{2}(\beta-1)=\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}-1>0.\] Therefore there is some sufficiently small \(\delta>0\) such that \(V(\delta)>V(0)\). Hence \(\delta=0\) is not optimal, and any optimum invests a strictly positive fraction.

title: CES Demand with Two Goods
learning_objective: Derive Marshallian demand for a constant elasticity of substitution utility function.
preliminaries: Let \(p_1,p_2,w>0\). For \(0<\rho<1\), define \(u(x_1,x_2)=(a x_1^{\rho}+(1-a)x_2^{\rho})^{1/\rho}\), where \(0<a<1\). Since the outer power is increasing, the same bundles maximize \(a x_1^{\rho}+(1-a)x_2^{\rho}\).
question: Derive the Marshallian demand for the CES utility in the preliminaries. Express demand in terms of prices, wealth, \(a\), and \(\rho\).
solution: The objective is strictly concave for \(0<\rho<1\), so the interior first-order conditions are sufficient. They are \(a\rho x_1^{\rho-1}=\lambda p_1\) and \((1-a)\rho x_2^{\rho-1}=\lambda p_2\). Dividing gives \((x_1/x_2)^{\rho-1}=((1-a)p_1)/(a p_2)\). Let \(\sigma=1/(1-\rho)>1\). Then \(x_1/x_2=(a p_2/((1-a)p_1))^{\sigma}\). Write \(r=(a p_2/((1-a)p_1))^{\sigma}\), so \(x_1=rx_2\). The binding budget implies \((p_1r+p_2)x_2=w\), hence \(x_2=w/(p_1r+p_2)\) and \(x_1=rw/(p_1r+p_2)\). Equivalently, after multiplying numerator and denominator by \(((1-a)p_1)^{\sigma}\), \(x_1=w a^{\sigma}p_1^{-\sigma}/(a^{\sigma}p_1^{1-\sigma}+(1-a)^{\sigma}p_2^{1-\sigma})\), and \(x_2=w(1-a)^{\sigma}p_2^{-\sigma}/(a^{\sigma}p_1^{1-\sigma}+(1-a)^{\sigma}p_2^{1-\sigma})\).

title: Cobb-Douglas Marshallian Demand and Indirect Utility
learning_objective: Derive Marshallian demand and indirect utility for an interior smooth consumer problem.
preliminaries: Let prices satisfy \(p_1,p_2>0\) and wealth \(w>0\). A Marshallian demand solves \(\max_{x_1,x_2\ge 0} u(x_1,x_2)\) subject to \(p_1x_1+p_2x_2\le w\).
question: A consumer has utility \(u(x_1,x_2)=x_1^{\alpha}x_2^{1-\alpha}\), where \(0<\alpha<1\). Derive the Marshallian demand \(x(p,w)\), verify Walras' law, and compute the indirect utility function \(v(p,w)\).
solution: Because utility is strictly increasing, the budget constraint binds. For an interior optimum, maximize \(\alpha\ln x_1+(1-\alpha)\ln x_2\) subject to the budget. The first-order conditions are \(\alpha/x_1=\lambda p_1\) and \((1-\alpha)/x_2=\lambda p_2\). Hence \(p_1x_1=\alpha/\lambda\) and \(p_2x_2=(1-\alpha)/\lambda\). Adding and using the binding budget gives \(1/\lambda=w\). Therefore \(x_1(p,w)=\alpha w/p_1\) and \(x_2(p,w)=(1-\alpha)w/p_2\). Walras' law holds since \(p_1x_1+p_2x_2=\alpha w+(1-\alpha)w=w\). Substitution into utility yields \(v(p,w)=(\alpha w/p_1)^{\alpha}((1-\alpha)w/p_2)^{1-\alpha}=w\alpha^{\alpha}(1-\alpha)^{1-\alpha}/(p_1^{\alpha}p_2^{1-\alpha})\). Strict concavity of \(\ln u\) gives uniqueness.

title: Cobb-Douglas Elasticities and Homogeneity
learning_objective: Compute price and wealth elasticities and verify homogeneity restrictions implied by demand theory.
preliminaries: For a differentiable demand \(x_i(p,w)\), the own-price elasticity is \(\varepsilon_{ii}=\partial x_i/\partial p_i\cdot p_i/x_i\), and the wealth elasticity is \(\eta_i=\partial x_i/\partial w\cdot w/x_i\).
question: Use the Cobb-Douglas demand from the canonical problem, \(x_1=\alpha w/p_1\) and \(x_2=(1-\alpha)w/p_2\). Compute all own-price, cross-price, and wealth elasticities. Then verify that the demand is homogeneous of degree zero in \((p,w)\).
solution: For good 1, \(\partial x_1/\partial p_1=-\alpha w/p_1^2\), so \(\varepsilon_{11}=(-\alpha w/p_1^2)(p_1/(\alpha w/p_1))=-1\). Since \(x_1\) does not depend on \(p_2\), \(\varepsilon_{12}=0\). Also \(\partial x_1/\partial w=\alpha/p_1\), so \(\eta_1=(\alpha/p_1)(w/(\alpha w/p_1))=1\). Similarly, \(\varepsilon_{22}=-1\), \(\varepsilon_{21}=0\), and \(\eta_2=1\). For any \(t>0\), \(x_1(tp,tw)=\alpha tw/(tp_1)=x_1(p,w)\) and \(x_2(tp,tw)=(1-\alpha)tw/(tp_2)=x_2(p,w)\). Thus demand is homogeneous of degree zero. The result also illustrates that Cobb-Douglas budget shares are constant and wealth elasticities are one.

title: Perfect Complements Demand and Indirect Utility
learning_objective: Solve a nonsmooth consumer problem by using the structure of perfect complements.
preliminaries: Let \(p_1,p_2,w>0\). A consumer has preferences represented by \(u(x_1,x_2)=\min\{x_1,2x_2\}\) over \(\mathbb{R}_+^2\).
question: Find the Marshallian demand and indirect utility. Explain why the optimal bundle lies on the kink unless wealth is zero.
solution: If \(x_1>2x_2\), reducing \(x_1\) while holding \(x_2\) fixed lowers expenditure without lowering utility, so such a bundle cannot be cost-effective at an optimum with positive wealth. If \(x_1<2x_2\), reducing \(x_2\) similarly saves money without lowering utility. Hence any positive optimum has \(x_1=2x_2\), and the budget binds. Substitute into \(p_1x_1+p_2x_2=w\): \(p_1(2x_2)+p_2x_2=w\), so \(x_2=w/(2p_1+p_2)\) and \(x_1=2w/(2p_1+p_2)\). The utility attained is \(u=x_1=2x_2=2w/(2p_1+p_2)\). Thus \(v(p,w)=2w/(2p_1+p_2)\). The argument does not rely on differentiability; it uses the fact that excess of either component beyond the fixed ratio is wasted.

title: Quasilinear Demand with a Boundary Case
learning_objective: Handle interior and boundary cases in a quasilinear utility maximization problem.
preliminaries: Let \(x\ge 0\) be consumption of a nonlinear good with price \(p>0\), and let \(m\ge 0\) be numeraire consumption with price one. Wealth is \(W>0\).
question: A consumer maximizes \(u(x,m)=2\sqrt{x}+m\) subject to \(px+m\le W\), \(x,m\ge 0\). Derive demand for \(x\) and \(m\) for all \(p,W>0\).
solution: Utility is increasing, so \(px+m=W\). Substitute \(m=W-px\), with \(0\le x\le W/p\), and maximize \(2\sqrt{x}+W-px\). For \(x>0\), the derivative is \(1/\sqrt{x}-p\). The unconstrained optimum is \(x=1/p^2\). This is feasible if \(1/p^2\le W/p\), equivalently \(W\ge 1/p\). In that case \(x^*=1/p^2\) and \(m^*=W-1/p\). If \(W<1/p\), then \(W/p<1/p^2\), so \(1/\sqrt{x}-p>0\) for every feasible \(0<x<W/p\). Therefore the objective is increasing on the feasible interval and the consumer spends all wealth on \(x\): \(x^*=W/p\) and \(m^*=0\). Thus \(x^*(p,W)=\min\{1/p^2,W/p\}\) and \(m^*(p,W)=W-px^*(p,W)\). The threshold is where the desired quasilinear demand just exhausts wealth.

title: Income Elasticity from a Multiplicatively Separable Expenditure Function
learning_objective: Derive Walrasian demand and income elasticity from an expenditure function of the form \(e(p,u)=f(u)g(p)\).
preliminaries: An individual has expenditure function \(e(p,u)=f(u)g(p)\), where \(p\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^n\), \(f\) is strictly increasing and twice continuously differentiable, and \(g\) is twice continuously differentiable. Assume differentiability conditions for Roy's identity and that good \(i\)'s demand is positive.
question: Compute the income elasticity of Walrasian demand for an arbitrary good \(i\).
solution: The indirect utility \(V(p,w)\) solves \(e(p,V(p,w))=w\), so \[f(V(p,w))g(p)=w,\qquad V(p,w)=f^{-1}\left(\frac{w}{g(p)}\right).\] Roy's identity gives \[x_i(p,w)=-\frac{\partial V(p,w)/\partial p_i}{\partial V(p,w)/\partial w}.\] Differentiating, the common factor \((f^{-1})'(w/g(p))\) cancels, yielding \[x_i(p,w)=\frac{w}{g(p)}\frac{\partial g(p)}{\partial p_i}\] up to the sign convention embedded in the expenditure derivative. For a valid expenditure function, Shephard's lemma implies the resulting demand is nonnegative; equivalently write \(x_i(p,w)=w\,a_i(p)\), where \(a_i(p)\) depends only on prices. Therefore \[\frac{\partial x_i}{\partial w}\frac{w}{x_i}=a_i(p)\frac{w}{w a_i(p)}=1.\] The income elasticity of every positively demanded good is \(1\).

source.original_question: An individual has expenditure function e(p,u)=f(u)g(p), with f strictly increasing and f,g twice continuously differentiable. Compute the income elasticity of Walrasian demand for an arbitrary good i.
source.notes: Converted from the source solution and independently checked by inverting expenditure and applying Roy's identity.

validation.notes: Demand is homogeneous of degree one in wealth, so income elasticity is one for positive demand.

title: Utility Representations Need Not All Be Homogeneous
learning_objective: Distinguish a property of preferences from a property of every utility representation.
preliminaries: Preferences are represented by some utility function. Any strictly increasing transformation of a utility representation represents the same preferences.
question: True or false: any utility function representing preferences whose expenditure function is \(e(p,u)=f(u)g(p)\) must be homogeneous of degree one. Explain.
solution: False. Homogeneity of a particular utility index is not ordinally invariant. If \(u(x)\) represents the preferences and is homogeneous of degree one, then \(\tilde u(x)=[u(x)]^2\) also represents the same preferences whenever \(u(x)>0\), because the square function is strictly increasing on positive utility levels. But \[\tilde u(\lambda x)=[u(\lambda x)]^2=[\lambda u(x)]^2=\lambda^2\tilde u(x),\] so \(\tilde u\) is homogeneous of degree two, not degree one. More generally, strictly increasing transformations preserve preferences while changing cardinal homogeneity properties. Thus it cannot be true that every utility representation must be homogeneous of degree one.

source.original_question: For preferences with expenditure function e(p,u)=f(u)g(p), decide whether any utility function representing the preferences must be homogeneous of degree one.
source.notes: Converted from the source answer and independently checked using monotone transformations.

validation.notes: A monotone transformation changes the degree of homogeneity while preserving preferences.

title: Concavity of Indirect Utility in Wealth
learning_objective: Show that a concave direct utility representation implies concavity of indirect utility in wealth.
preliminaries: Fix \(p\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^2\). Preferences are represented by a concave utility function \(u\), and \[V(p,w)=\max\{u(x):x\ge 0,\ p\cdot x\le w\}.\]
question: Show that \(V(p,w)\) is concave in \(w\) for fixed \(p\).
solution: Let \(w,w'>0\), \(\lambda\in[0,1]\), and \(\bar w=\lambda w+(1-\lambda)w'\). Let \(x\) solve the problem at wealth \(w\), and let \(x'\) solve it at wealth \(w'\). The convex combination \(\bar x=\lambda x+(1-\lambda)x'\) is affordable at wealth \(\bar w\), since \[p\cdot \bar x=\lambda p\cdot x+(1-\lambda)p\cdot x'\le \lambda w+(1-\lambda)w'=\bar w.\] Therefore \[V(p,\bar w)\ge u(\bar x).\] By concavity of \(u\), \[u(\bar x)\ge \lambda u(x)+(1-\lambda)u(x')=\lambda V(p,w)+(1-\lambda)V(p,w').\] Combining the inequalities yields \[V(p,\lambda w+(1-\lambda)w')\ge \lambda V(p,w)+(1-\lambda)V(p,w'),\] so \(V\) is concave in wealth.

source.original_question: Suppose the consumer's preferences are represented by a concave utility function. Show that indirect utility V(p,w) is concave in w for fixed p.
source.notes: Converted from the source answer and independently verified via affordable convex combinations.

validation.notes: The affordability and utility-concavity inequalities prove the result without differentiability.

title: Concavity and Quasiconvexity Are Compatible
learning_objective: Explain why indirect utility can be both concave and quasiconvex in wealth.
preliminaries: For fixed prices \(p\), suppose \(V(p,w)\) is concave in \(w\). From quasiconvexity in \((p,w)\), it also satisfies \[V(p,\lambda w+(1-\lambda)w')\le \max\{V(p,w),V(p,w')\}.\]
question: Is it a contradiction that \(V(p,w)\) is quasiconvex in \(w\) for fixed \(p\), while for a concave utility representation it is also concave in \(w\)?
solution: There is no contradiction. Concavity gives a lower bound, \[V(p,\lambda w+(1-\lambda)w')\ge \lambda V(p,w)+(1-\lambda)V(p,w'),\] while quasiconvexity gives an upper bound, \[V(p,\lambda w+(1-\lambda)w')\le \max\{V(p,w),V(p,w')\}.\] These inequalities are compatible because any weighted average of two numbers is weakly below their maximum: \[\lambda V(p,w)+(1-\lambda)V(p,w')\le \max\{V(p,w),V(p,w')\}.\] For example, an increasing concave function of one variable can also be quasiconvex because its value at an intermediate wealth lies between the endpoint values. Thus the two properties restrict \(V\) in different directions but do not conflict.

source.original_question: Since V(p,w) is quasiconvex in (p,w), it is quasiconvex in w for fixed p. If utility is concave, V(p,w) is concave in w for fixed p. Is this a contradiction?
source.notes: Converted from the source answer and independently checked against the definitions of concavity and quasiconvexity.

validation.notes: The weighted-average inequality shows the two bounds can hold simultaneously.

title: Quasiconvexity of Indirect Utility in Prices and Wealth
learning_objective: Prove quasiconvexity of indirect utility in the price-wealth vector by budget-set inclusion.
preliminaries: A consumer has continuous, convex, monotone preferences on \(\mathbb{R}_+^2\), represented by a utility function \(u\). For \(p\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^2\) and \(w>0\), define \[V(p,w)=\max\{u(x):x\in\mathbb{R}_+^2,\ p\cdot x\le w\}.\]
question: Show that \(V(p,w)\) is quasiconvex in \((p,w)\).
solution: Take two price-wealth pairs \((p,w)\) and \((p',w')\), and let \(\lambda\in[0,1]\). Write \((\bar p,\bar w)=(\lambda p+(1-\lambda)p',\lambda w+(1-\lambda)w')\). If \(x\) is affordable at \((\bar p,\bar w)\), then \[\lambda(p\cdot x-w)+(1-\lambda)(p'\cdot x-w')\le 0.\] It cannot be true that both \(p\cdot x>w\) and \(p'\cdot x>w'\), because then the left side would be positive. Hence \(x\) is affordable at \((p,w)\) or at \((p',w')\). Therefore \[B(\bar p,\bar w)\subseteq B(p,w)\cup B(p',w'),\] where \(B\) denotes the budget set. Maximizing utility over a subset of the union gives \[V(\bar p,\bar w)\le \max\{V(p,w),V(p',w')\}.\] This is exactly quasiconvexity of \(V\) in \((p,w)\).

source.original_question: A consumer has continuous, convex, monotonic preferences on R^2_+, wealth w>0, and prices p in R^2_{++}. Show that indirect utility V(p,w) is quasiconvex in (p,w).
source.notes: Converted from the source answer and independently checked through the budget-set inclusion proof.

validation.notes: The proof establishes the defining quasiconvex inequality directly.

title: First-Order Welfare Effect of Offset Price Changes
learning_objective: Use the indirect utility function to sign the first-order welfare effect of changing two prices in opposite directions.
preliminaries: A consumer has strictly increasing, strictly quasi-concave, twice continuously differentiable utility and interior Marshallian demand \(x^*(p,w)=(x_1^*,x_2^*)\). Initial prices are \(p=(p_1,p_2)\gg 0\), wealth is \(w>0\), and indirect utility is \(V(p,w)\). A policy changes prices to \(\tilde p_1=(1-\alpha)p_1\) and \(\tilde p_2=(1+\alpha)p_2\) for small \(\alpha>0\).
question: Relate the first-order effect of the policy on welfare to the consumer's initial spending on each good.
solution: By the envelope theorem for the utility maximization problem, \(\partial V/\partial p_i=-\lambda x_i^*\), where \(\lambda>0\) is the marginal utility of wealth at the initial optimum. Differentiating \(V((1-\alpha)p_1,(1+\alpha)p_2,w)\) at \(\alpha=0\) gives \[\left.\frac{dV}{d\alpha}\right|_{0}=\frac{\partial V}{\partial p_1}(-p_1)+\frac{\partial V}{\partial p_2}(p_2)=\lambda p_1x_1^*-\lambda p_2x_2^*.\] Therefore the first-order welfare effect is positive if \(p_1x_1^*>p_2x_2^*\), negative if \(p_1x_1^*<p_2x_2^*\), and zero to first order if \(p_1x_1^*=p_2x_2^*\). The policy is beneficial to first order when the consumer initially spends more on the subsidized good than on the good whose price rises.

source.original_question: Relate how the price program affects consumer welfare to the amount spent on each good before the program.

validation.notes: Envelope signs and first-order comparison by initial expenditure shares are verified.

title: Compensating Transfer and Revealed Preference
learning_objective: Use affordability of the old bundle to show that a compensated price change weakly raises welfare.
preliminaries: Let \(x^*=(x_1^*,x_2^*)\) solve the original consumer problem at prices \(p=(p_1,p_2)\) and wealth \(w\). New prices are \(\tilde p_1=(1-\alpha)p_1\) and \(\tilde p_2=(1+\alpha)p_2\). The transfer is \(T=\alpha p_2x_2^* - \alpha p_1x_1^*\), so new wealth is \(w+T\).
question: With the new effective prices and transfer, does consumer welfare increase, decrease, or remain unchanged? Explain carefully.
solution: Evaluate the cost of the original bundle under the new prices: \[\tilde p_1x_1^*+\tilde p_2x_2^*=(1-\alpha)p_1x_1^*+(1+\alpha)p_2x_2^*=p_1x_1^*+p_2x_2^*+\alpha p_2x_2^* - \alpha p_1x_1^*.\] Since the original bundle exhausts the original budget under monotonicity, \(p_1x_1^*+p_2x_2^*=w\). Thus \[\tilde p\cdot x^*=w+T.\] The original bundle is exactly affordable after the price change and transfer. Therefore the consumer can attain at least the original utility, so welfare weakly increases. It may strictly increase because the compensated budget line pivots through the original bundle, and another newly affordable bundle may be strictly preferred.

source.original_question: With the price changes and transfer T, does consumer welfare increase, decrease, or remain unchanged?

validation.notes: The old bundle is exactly affordable under the compensated budget, giving weak welfare improvement.

title: Incompleteness of a Two-Inequality Preference Relation
learning_objective: Disprove completeness by finding two bundles that the preference relation cannot rank.
preliminaries: Bundles are \(x=(x_1,x_2)\in\mathbb{R}_+^2\). Define \(x\succeq y\) if and only if both \(2x_1+x_2\ge 2y_1+y_2\) and \(x_1+2x_2\ge y_1+2y_2\). Completeness means that for every pair \(x,y\), either \(x\succeq y\), \(y\succeq x\), or both.
question: Are these preferences complete? Explain.
solution: They are not complete. Take \(x=(1,0)\) and \(y=(0,1)\). To have \(x\succeq y\), both defining inequalities must hold. The first gives \(2\ge 1\), but the second gives \(1\ge 2\), which is false. Hence \(x\not\succeq y\). To have \(y\succeq x\), the first inequality gives \(1\ge 2\), which is false, even though the second gives \(2\ge 1\). Hence \(y\not\succeq x\). Since this pair is not ranked in either direction, the relation is not complete.

title: Transitivity of a Two-Inequality Preference Relation
learning_objective: Prove transitivity for a preference relation defined as the intersection of two complete preorder comparisons.
preliminaries: Bundles are \(x=(x_1,x_2)\in\mathbb{R}_+^2\). Define \(x\succeq y\) if and only if both \(2x_1+x_2\ge 2y_1+y_2\) and \(x_1+2x_2\ge y_1+2y_2\). Transitivity means that \(x\succeq y\) and \(y\succeq z\) imply \(x\succeq z\).
question: Do these preferences satisfy transitivity? Explain.
solution: Yes. Suppose \(x\succeq y\) and \(y\succeq z\). From \(x\succeq y\), \[2x_1+x_2\ge 2y_1+y_2,\qquad x_1+2x_2\ge y_1+2y_2.\] From \(y\succeq z\), \[2y_1+y_2\ge 2z_1+z_2,\qquad y_1+2y_2\ge z_1+2z_2.\] Chaining the first pair of inequalities gives \(2x_1+x_2\ge 2z_1+z_2\). Chaining the second pair gives \(x_1+2x_2\ge z_1+2z_2\). Both inequalities required for \(x\succeq z\) hold, so the relation is transitive.

title: Writing the Consumer Problem with Possible Nonattainment
learning_objective: State the utility maximization problem carefully when an optimizer may not exist.
preliminaries: A consumer has wealth \(w>0\), price vector \(p\in\mathbb{R}_+^n\), and utility \(u:\mathbb{R}_+^n\to\mathbb{R}\). The budget set is \(B(p,w)=\{x\in\mathbb{R}_+^n:p\cdot x\le w\}\).
question: Write the consumer's utility-maximization problem while accounting for the possibility that a maximum might not exist.
solution: The robust formulation uses a supremum: \[V(p,w)=\sup\{u(x):x\in\mathbb{R}_+^n,\ p\cdot x\le w\}.\] The demand correspondence is then \[X(p,w)=\arg\max\{u(x):x\in\mathbb{R}_+^n,\ p\cdot x\le w\}\] when the supremum is attained. If the supremum is not attained, \(X(p,w)\) is empty even though \(V(p,w)\) may still be well-defined as an extended real number.

title: Existence of Demand under Positive Prices
learning_objective: Prove existence of a utility maximizer when prices are strictly positive.
preliminaries: A consumer has continuous utility \(u:\mathbb{R}_+^n\to\mathbb{R}\), wealth \(w>0\), and price vector \(p\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^n\). The budget set is \(B(p,w)=\{x\ge 0:p\cdot x\le w\}\).
question: Prove that the consumer's problem has a solution. What can fail if prices are only nonnegative?
solution: If \(p_i>0\) for every \(i\), feasibility implies \(0\le x_i\le w/p_i\). Hence \(B(p,w)\) is bounded. It is closed as the intersection of \(\mathbb{R}_+^n\) and the closed half-space \(\{x:p\cdot x\le w\}\). Therefore \(B(p,w)\) is compact. By the Weierstrass extreme value theorem, continuous \(u\) attains a maximum on \(B(p,w)\). If some price is zero, the budget set can be unbounded. For example, if \(p_1=0\) and \(u(x)=x_1\), utility is unbounded above on the budget set, so no solution exists.

title: Budget Exhaustion under Monotonicity
learning_objective: Prove that a monotone consumer spends all wealth at strictly positive prices.
preliminaries: Prices satisfy \(p\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^n\), wealth is \(w>0\), and \(x^*\) is an optimal bundle. Strong monotonicity means \(x\ge y\) and \(x\ne y\) imply \(u(x)>u(y)\). Strict monotonicity means \(x\gg y\) implies \(u(x)>u(y)\).
question: Show that the budget constraint binds under strong monotonicity. Does the proof change if utility is only strictly increasing?
solution: Suppose \(p\cdot x^*<w\). Let \(\varepsilon=w-p\cdot x^*>0\), and define \[x'=x^*+\frac{\varepsilon}{2\sum_i p_i}(1,\ldots,1).\] Then \(x'\gg x^*\) and \[p\cdot x'=p\cdot x^*+\frac{\varepsilon}{2\sum_i p_i}\sum_i p_i=p\cdot x^*+\varepsilon/2<w.\] Thus \(x'\) is feasible and strictly preferred under strong monotonicity, contradicting optimality. The same construction also works under strict monotonicity, because \(x'\gg x^*\). Hence the budget binds under either condition.

title: Strict Quasiconcavity Implies Unique Demand
learning_objective: Prove single-valued demand from strict quasiconcavity on a convex budget set.
preliminaries: Let \(B(p,w)=\{x\ge 0:p\cdot x\le w\}\), with \(p\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^n\). Utility is continuous and strictly quasiconcave: for distinct \(x,y\) and \(\lambda\in(0,1)\), \(u(\lambda x+(1-\lambda)y)>\min\{u(x),u(y)\}\).
question: Assuming a maximizer exists, prove that the demand correspondence is single-valued. What changes if utility is not strongly increasing?
solution: Suppose two distinct bundles \(x\) and \(y\) both maximize utility on \(B(p,w)\), and let the maximum value be \(V\). Since the budget set is convex, \(z=\lambda x+(1-\lambda)y\) is feasible for \(\lambda\in(0,1)\). Strict quasiconcavity gives \(u(z)>\min\{u(x),u(y)\}=V\), a contradiction. Thus the maximizer is unique. Strong monotonicity is not needed for this uniqueness result. Without strong monotonicity, the unique bundle may fail to exhaust the budget, but strict quasiconcavity still rules out two distinct maximizers.

title: Why Demand Has No General Monotone Price Response
learning_objective: Explain why the standard consumer problem does not directly satisfy Milgrom-Shannon hypotheses.
preliminaries: Milgrom-Shannon monotone comparative statics requires ordered feasible sets with lattice structure and suitable single-crossing or increasing-differences conditions. A Walrasian budget set is \(B(p,w)=\{x\in\mathbb{R}_+^n:p\cdot x\le w\}\).
question: Can we generally say how demand changes with prices under continuous, strongly increasing, strictly quasiconcave utility? Can Milgrom-Shannon's theorem be directly applied?
solution: There is no general coordinatewise monotonicity conclusion for Marshallian demand. A price increase combines substitution and wealth effects; own demand can even rise for a Giffen good, and cross-price effects can have either sign. The listed assumptions give existence, budget exhaustion, and uniqueness, but not monotone demand in prices. Milgrom-Shannon does not directly apply because the budget set is generally not a lattice. In two goods, two feasible bundles can have a componentwise join that violates the budget constraint. Therefore \(B(p,w)\) is not closed under joins and does not satisfy the required feasible-set structure. Demand comparative statics require other tools or stronger assumptions.

title: The Work-Minimization Problem
learning_objective: Formulate a dual work-minimization problem with labor entering preferences.
preliminaries: A consumer chooses \(x\in\mathbb{R}_+^L\) and work hours \(h\ge 0\). Utility \(u(x,h)\) is continuous, strictly increasing in each component of \(x\), and weakly decreasing in \(h\). The wage is normalized to one, so the budget constraint is \(p\cdot x\le h\).
question: For target utility \(\bar u\), write the work-minimization problem and define the minimal-work function.
solution: The work-minimization problem is \[m(p,\bar u)=\inf_{x\ge 0,h\ge 0}\{h:u(x,h)\ge \bar u,\ p\cdot x\le h\}.\] The value \(m(p,\bar u)\) is the minimal number of work hours needed to afford a bundle that attains utility at least \(\bar u\). Since utility is weakly decreasing in \(h\), any feasible pair with \(h>p\cdot x\) can reduce \(h\) down to \(p\cdot x\) without hurting feasibility or lowering utility. Thus an equivalent reduced problem is \[m(p,\bar u)=\inf_{x\ge 0}\{p\cdot x:u(x,p\cdot x)\ge \bar u\}.\]

title: Minimal Work Is Nondecreasing in Prices
learning_objective: Prove price monotonicity of the minimal-work function by set inclusion.
preliminaries: The minimal-work function is \(m(p,\bar u)=\inf\{h:u(x,h)\ge \bar u,\ p\cdot x\le h,\ x\ge 0,h\ge 0\}\). Utility is weakly decreasing in \(h\) and strictly increasing in goods.
question: Prove that \(m(p,\bar u)\) is nondecreasing in each component of \(p\).
solution: Take price vectors \(p'\ge p\) componentwise. If \((x,h)\) is feasible at prices \(p'\), then \(u(x,h)\ge \bar u\) and \(p'\cdot x\le h\). Since \(p\le p'\), we also have \(p\cdot x\le p'\cdot x\le h\). Thus every pair feasible at \(p'\) is feasible at \(p\). The feasible set at lower prices contains the feasible set at higher prices. Minimizing the same objective \(h\) over the larger feasible set cannot yield a larger value, so \(m(p,\bar u)\le m(p',\bar u)\). Therefore raising any goods price weakly increases minimal work.

title: A Counterexample to Homogeneity of Minimal Work
learning_objective: Show that minimal work need not be homogeneous of degree one when work enters utility.
preliminaries: Consider one consumption good with price \(p>0\). Work hours are \(h\ge 0\), the budget constraint is \(px\le h\), and the target utility is \(\bar u=1\). Utility is \(u(x,h)=x^2/h\) for \(h>0\).
question: Use this example to show that the minimal-work function need not be homogeneous of degree one in \(p\).
solution: At an optimum, \(h=px\); otherwise the consumer could reduce \(h\), which raises \(x^2/h\) and lowers the objective. The utility constraint becomes \[\frac{x^2}{px}=\frac{x}{p}\ge 1,\] so \(x\ge p\). Minimal work is therefore \[m(p,1)=\min_{x\ge p}px=p^2.\] If prices double, \(m(2p,1)=4p^2\), while degree-one homogeneity would require \(m(2p,1)=2m(p,1)=2p^2\). Thus the minimal-work function is not generally homogeneous of degree one in prices.

title: Homogeneity When Utility Is Independent of Work
learning_objective: Recover expenditure-function homogeneity when work hours do not affect utility.
preliminaries: Suppose utility is independent of work hours, so \(u(x,h)=v(x)\). The minimal-work function is \(m(p,\bar u)=\inf\{h:v(x)\ge \bar u,\ p\cdot x\le h,\ x\ge 0,h\ge 0\}\).
question: Show that \(m(p,\bar u)\) is homogeneous of degree one in \(p\) when utility is constant in \(h\).
solution: If \(u(x,h)=v(x)\), the minimal-work problem reduces to \[m(p,\bar u)=\inf_{x\ge 0}\{p\cdot x:v(x)\ge \bar u\}.\] This is the standard expenditure problem, expressed in work-hours units because the wage is normalized to one. For any \(\lambda>0\), \[m(\lambda p,\bar u)=\inf_{x:v(x)\ge \bar u}\lambda p\cdot x=\lambda\inf_{x:v(x)\ge \bar u}p\cdot x=\lambda m(p,\bar u).\] Therefore the minimal-work function is homogeneous of degree one in prices under this additional assumption.

title: Project Choice with Uncertain Beliefs
learning_objective: Choose between risky projects when the probability distribution is itself uncertain.
preliminaries: There are two states, \(H\) and \(L\). Project I pays \(2\) in state \(H\) and \(5\) in state \(L\). Project II pays \(5\) in state \(H\) and \(3\) in state \(L\). Bernoulli utility is \(u(x)=\log x\). With probability \(1/2\), the state distribution is \(p=(1/3,2/3)\); with probability \(1/2\), it is \(q=(2/3,1/3)\).
question: Without additional information, which project should the individual choose?
solution: The unconditional probability of state \(H\) is \((1/2)(1/3)+(1/2)(2/3)=1/2\), and the unconditional probability of state \(L\) is also \(1/2\). Therefore expected utility from Project I is \[EU_I=\frac12\log 2+\frac12\log 5=\log\sqrt{10}.\] Expected utility from Project II is \[EU_{II}=\frac12\log 5+\frac12\log 3=\log\sqrt{15}.\] Since \(15>10\), \(\log\sqrt{15}>\log\sqrt{10}\). The individual should choose Project II.

source.notes: Converted from the source solution and independently checked by reducing the mixture to unconditional state probabilities.

validation.notes: The expected utilities simplify to \(\log\sqrt{10}\) and \(\log\sqrt{15}\), so Project II is optimal.

title: A Noisy Signal with Zero Decision Value
learning_objective: Use posterior predictive probabilities to determine whether a signal changes the optimal action.
preliminaries: Instead of learning whether the distribution is \(p\) or \(q\), the individual observes one draw from the true distribution before choosing a project. The payoff state is then determined by a second independent draw from the same true distribution.
question: How much would the individual pay to observe one draw from the true distribution before choosing a project?
solution: The ex ante probability of observing \(H\) is \(1/2\). By Bayes' rule, after signal \(H\), \[\Pr(p\mid H)=\frac{(1/2)(1/3)}{(1/2)(1/3)+(1/2)(2/3)}=\frac13,\qquad \Pr(q\mid H)=\frac23.\] The posterior predictive probability of \(H\) on the payoff draw is \((1/3)(1/3)+(2/3)(2/3)=5/9\). After signal \(L\), the posterior weights are \(\Pr(p\mid L)=2/3\) and \(\Pr(q\mid L)=1/3\), so the posterior predictive probability of \(H\) is \((2/3)(1/3)+(1/3)(2/3)=4/9\). For any predictive probability \(\pi\) of state \(H\), Project II is preferred to Project I when \[\pi\log5+(1-\pi)\log3\ge \pi\log2+(1-\pi)\log5.\] This is equivalent to \[\pi\log(5/2)+(1-\pi)\log(3/5)\ge 0,\] whose cutoff is below \(4/9\). Therefore Project II is optimal after both possible signals. Since the signal never changes the chosen project, it has zero value in expected utility terms. The individual would pay \(0\) for this signal.

source.original_question: A less precise information source gives one draw from the actual distribution before the project choice, and the payoff is determined by a second independent draw. How much would the individual pay to observe the draw?

validation.notes: Bayesian updating gives predictive probabilities \(5/9\) and \(4/9\); Project II remains optimal after both signals, so the value is zero.

title: Value of Learning the Probability Distribution
learning_objective: Compute the expected utility gain from observing which prior distribution governs payoffs before choosing a project.
preliminaries: Use the two-project problem with log utility. Before choosing the project, the individual can learn whether the true state distribution is \(p=(1/3,2/3)\) or \(q=(2/3,1/3)\). Each distribution is equally likely ex ante.
question: Compute the increase in expected utility from being told whether the state distribution is \(p\) or \(q\) before choosing a project.
solution: Under distribution \(p\), expected utilities are \[EU_I(p)=\frac13\log2+\frac23\log5=\frac13\log50,\] and \[EU_{II}(p)=\frac13\log5+\frac23\log3=\frac13\log45.\] Since \(50>45\), choose Project I under \(p\). Under distribution \(q\), \[EU_I(q)=\frac23\log2+\frac13\log5=\frac13\log20,\] and \[EU_{II}(q)=\frac23\log5+\frac13\log3=\frac13\log75.\] Since \(75>20\), choose Project II under \(q\). Expected utility with information is \[\frac12\cdot\frac13\log50+\frac12\cdot\frac13\log75=\frac16\log(3750).\] Without information, the individual chooses Project II and gets \(\log\sqrt{15}=\frac12\log15\). The gain is \[\frac16\log(3750)-\frac12\log15=\frac16\log\left(\frac{3750}{15^3}\right)=\frac16\log\left(\frac{10}{9}\right).\]

title: Two-Person Exchange Equilibrium
learning_objective: Compute a competitive equilibrium in a two-good pure exchange economy.
preliminaries: There are two consumers and two goods. Consumer \(A\) has \(u_A(x_1,x_2)=x_1^{1/2}x_2^{1/2}\) and endowment \(\omega_A=(4,0)\). Consumer \(B\) has \(u_B(x_1,x_2)=x_1^{1/3}x_2^{2/3}\) and endowment \(\omega_B=(0,6)\). Prices are positive and normalized by \(p_2=1\).
question: Find the Walrasian equilibrium price \(p_1\) and allocation.
solution: Cobb-Douglas demands spend fixed shares of wealth. Consumer \(A\)'s wealth is \(m_A=4p_1\), so \(x_{A1}=m_A/(2p_1)=2\) and \(x_{A2}=m_A/2=2p_1\). Consumer \(B\)'s wealth is \(m_B=6\), so \(x_{B1}=m_B/(3p_1)=2/p_1\) and \(x_{B2}=2m_B/3=4\). Market clearing for good 1 requires \(2+2/p_1=4\), so \(2/p_1=2\) and \(p_1=1\). Then good 2 demand is \(x_{A2}+x_{B2}=2+4=6\), so the second market clears by Walras' law and direct calculation. The equilibrium allocation is \(x_A=(2,2)\) and \(x_B=(2,4)\), with normalized prices \((1,1)\).

title: Robinson Crusoe Production Equilibrium
learning_objective: Compute equilibrium in a one-consumer one-firm economy and connect decentralized and planner outcomes.
preliminaries: There is one consumption good \(c\) and labor \(l\). The consumer has one unit of time, leisure \(r=1-l\), and utility \(u(c,r)=\ln c+\ln r\). A firm produces \(y=\sqrt{l}\) from labor. The consumer owns the firm. Normalize the consumption good price to one, and let the wage be \(w>0\).
question: Find the competitive equilibrium wage, labor, consumption, leisure, and profit.
solution: The firm maximizes \(\sqrt{l}-wl\). The first-order condition is \((1/(2\sqrt{l}))=w\), so labor demand is \(l_f=1/(4w^2)\), output is \(1/(2w)\), and profit is \(\pi=1/(2w)-w/(4w^2)=1/(4w)\). The consumer's wealth is \(w+\pi\), since selling all time would earn \(w\) and firm profit is rebated. Choosing \(c,r\) subject to \(c+wr=w+\pi\), Cobb-Douglas demands give \(r=(w+\pi)/(2w)\) and labor supply \(l_s=1-r\). Equilibrium requires \(l_s=l_f\). Substitute \(\pi=1/(4w)\): \[1-\frac{w+1/(4w)}{2w}=\frac{1}{4w^2}.\] The left side is \(1/2-1/(8w^2)\), so multiplying by \(8w^2\) gives \(4w^2-1=2\). Hence \(w^2=3/4\), and the positive wage is \(w=\sqrt{3}/2\). Then \(l=1/(4w^2)=1/3\), \(r=2/3\), \(y=c=\sqrt{1/3}=1/\sqrt{3}\), and \(\pi=1/(4w)=1/(2\sqrt{3})\).

title: Deadweight Loss from a Unit Tax
learning_objective: Compute equilibrium incidence, revenue, and deadweight loss in a linear market.
preliminaries: A competitive market has inverse demand \(P_D(Q)=10-Q\) and inverse supply \(P_S(Q)=2+Q\). A per-unit tax \(t=2\) is imposed on sellers, so buyers pay \(P_b\), sellers receive \(P_s\), and \(P_b=P_s+t\).
question: Find the pre-tax equilibrium, post-tax equilibrium, tax revenue, and deadweight loss.
solution: Without the tax, equilibrium solves \(10-Q=2+Q\), so \(Q^0=4\) and price is \(6\). With the tax, equilibrium solves \(P_D(Q)=P_S(Q)+2\), or \(10-Q=2+Q+2\). Thus \(Q^1=3\), buyers pay \(P_b=10-3=7\), and sellers receive \(P_s=2+3=5\). Revenue is \(tQ^1=2\cdot3=6\). The quantity reduction is \(1\). The deadweight loss triangle has height \(t=2\) and base \(Q^0-Q^1=1\), so \(DWL=\frac{1}{2}\cdot2\cdot1=1\). Incidence is split equally here because demand and supply slopes have equal absolute value: buyers pay \(1\) more and sellers receive \(1\) less than the original price.

title: First Welfare Theorem in an Exchange Economy
learning_objective: Prove the First Welfare Theorem using budget inequalities.
preliminaries: In a pure exchange economy, an allocation \((x_i)_i\) is feasible if \(\sum_i x_i\le \sum_i \omega_i\). A Walrasian equilibrium consists of prices \(p\gg 0\) and feasible allocation \((x_i)_i\) such that each \(x_i\) maximizes preferences over \(\{x:p\cdot x\le p\cdot\omega_i\}\). Preferences are locally nonsatiated.
question: Prove that every Walrasian equilibrium allocation is Pareto efficient.
solution: Suppose an equilibrium allocation \((x_i)_i\) is not Pareto efficient. Then there is a feasible allocation \((y_i)_i\) such that every consumer weakly prefers \(y_i\) to \(x_i\), and at least one consumer strictly prefers \(y_j\) to \(x_j\). Since \(x_i\) maximizes on the budget set, any bundle strictly preferred to \(x_i\) must be unaffordable: \(p\cdot y_j>p\cdot\omega_j\). For any consumer who weakly prefers \(y_i\) to \(x_i\), local nonsatiation and optimality imply \(p\cdot y_i\ge p\cdot\omega_i\); otherwise a slightly better nearby affordable bundle would contradict optimality of \(x_i\). Summing gives \(p\cdot\sum_i y_i>p\cdot\sum_i\omega_i\). But feasibility gives \(\sum_i y_i\le\sum_i\omega_i\), and with \(p\gg0\), \(p\cdot\sum_i y_i\le p\cdot\sum_i\omega_i\), a contradiction. Thus the equilibrium allocation is Pareto efficient.

title: Cobb-Douglas Expenditure and Hicksian Demand
learning_objective: Derive an expenditure function and Hicksian demand, and verify Shephard's lemma.
preliminaries: For prices \(p_1,p_2>0\) and target utility \(u>0\), the expenditure function is \(e(p,u)=\min_{x\ge 0} p_1x_1+p_2x_2\) subject to \(x_1^{\alpha}x_2^{1-\alpha}\ge u\), where \(0<\alpha<1\).
question: Derive \(e(p,u)\) and Hicksian demand \(h(p,u)\). Verify that \(\partial e/\partial p_i=h_i\).
solution: At the optimum the utility constraint binds and the solution is interior. The first-order conditions for minimizing cost are \(p_1=\lambda\alpha x_1^{\alpha-1}x_2^{1-\alpha}\) and \(p_2=\lambda(1-\alpha)x_1^{\alpha}x_2^{-\alpha}\). Dividing gives \(p_1/p_2=(\alpha/(1-\alpha))(x_2/x_1)\), so cost shares satisfy \(p_1x_1=\alpha C\) and \(p_2x_2=(1-\alpha)C\), where \(C=e(p,u)\). Hence \(x_1=\alpha C/p_1\) and \(x_2=(1-\alpha)C/p_2\). Plugging into utility gives \(u=(\alpha C/p_1)^{\alpha}((1-\alpha)C/p_2)^{1-\alpha}=C\alpha^{\alpha}(1-\alpha)^{1-\alpha}/(p_1^{\alpha}p_2^{1-\alpha})\). Thus \(e(p,u)=u p_1^{\alpha}p_2^{1-\alpha}/(\alpha^{\alpha}(1-\alpha)^{1-\alpha})\). Hicksian demands are \(h_1=\alpha e/p_1\) and \(h_2=(1-\alpha)e/p_2\). Differentiating \(e\) with respect to \(p_1\) and \(p_2\) gives exactly these expressions, verifying Shephard's lemma.

title: Slutsky Symmetry as an Integrability Test
learning_objective: Use the Slutsky matrix to reject a candidate demand system as utility-generated.
preliminaries: For two goods, define the Slutsky terms \(S_{ij}=\partial x_i/\partial p_j+x_j\partial x_i/\partial w\). A differentiable demand generated by smooth preferences must have a symmetric Slutsky matrix where interior regularity conditions apply.
question: Consider the demand system \(x_1(p,w)=w/(2p_1)+p_2\) and \(x_2(p,w)=w/(2p_2)-p_1p_2/p_2\), on a domain where both quantities are positive. Does it pass Slutsky symmetry?
solution: First simplify \(x_2=w/(2p_2)-p_1\). Compute \(S_{12}=\partial x_1/\partial p_2+x_2\partial x_1/\partial w=1+x_2(1/(2p_1))=1+(w/(2p_2)-p_1)/(2p_1)=1+w/(4p_1p_2)-1/2=1/2+w/(4p_1p_2)\). Next \(S_{21}=\partial x_2/\partial p_1+x_1\partial x_2/\partial w=-1+x_1(1/(2p_2))=-1+(w/(2p_1)+p_2)/(2p_2)=-1+w/(4p_1p_2)+1/2=-1/2+w/(4p_1p_2)\). Since \(S_{12}-S_{21}=1\), the Slutsky matrix is not symmetric at any point in the domain. Therefore this differentiable demand system cannot be generated by a standard smooth utility maximization problem satisfying the usual integrability conditions.

title: Roy's Identity for an Indirect Utility Function
learning_objective: Recover Marshallian demand from an indirect utility function using Roy's identity.
preliminaries: Roy's identity states that if indirect utility \(v(p,w)\) is differentiable and locally nonsatiated demand is single-valued, then \(x_i(p,w)=-v_{p_i}(p,w)/v_w(p,w)\).
question: Let \(v(p_1,p_2,w)=w/(p_1^{1/3}p_2^{2/3})\). Use Roy's identity to recover Marshallian demand and verify Walras' law.
solution: Compute derivatives: \(v_w=1/(p_1^{1/3}p_2^{2/3})\), \(v_{p_1}=-(1/3)w p_1^{-4/3}p_2^{-2/3}\), and \(v_{p_2}=-(2/3)w p_1^{-1/3}p_2^{-5/3}\). Roy's identity gives \(x_1=-v_{p_1}/v_w=(1/3)w/p_1\) and \(x_2=-v_{p_2}/v_w=(2/3)w/p_2\). Walras' law holds because \(p_1x_1+p_2x_2=w/3+2w/3=w\). The recovered demand corresponds to Cobb-Douglas budget shares \((1/3,2/3)\), which is consistent with the homogeneity of \(v\) of degree zero in \((p,w)\).

title: Equivalent and Compensating Variation from Expenditure
learning_objective: Compute welfare measures for a price change using the expenditure function.
preliminaries: For an initial price \(p^0\), new price \(p^1\), and wealth \(w\), let \(u^0=v(p^0,w)\) and \(u^1=v(p^1,w)\). Compensating variation for a price increase is \(CV=e(p^1,u^0)-w\). Equivalent variation is \(EV=w-e(p^0,u^1)\).
question: A consumer has \(u(x_1,x_2)=\sqrt{x_1x_2}\), initial prices \(p^0=(1,1)\), new prices \(p^1=(4,1)\), and wealth \(w=100\). Compute \(CV\) and \(EV\).
solution: For \(u=\sqrt{x_1x_2}=x_1^{1/2}x_2^{1/2}\), the expenditure function is \(e(p,u)=2u\sqrt{p_1p_2}\). At initial prices, indirect utility is \(u^0=w/(2\sqrt{p_1p_2})=100/2=50\). At new prices, \(u^1=100/(2\sqrt{4})=25\). Compensating variation is the extra wealth at new prices needed to reach old utility: \(CV=e(p^1,u^0)-100=2\cdot50\cdot\sqrt{4}-100=200-100=100\). Equivalent variation is the wealth at old prices the consumer would give up to be as well off as after the price increase: \(EV=100-e(p^0,u^1)=100-2\cdot25\cdot1=50\). The price increase hurts the consumer; both numbers are positive losses, with \(CV>EV\) for this case.

title: Competitive Equilibrium with Recursive Consumption Externalities
learning_objective: Compute a competitive equilibrium when agents care about others' consumption but choose only their own consumption.
preliminaries: Use the utility formulas from the three-person cycle with \(0\le \beta<1\). Agents 1 and 2 each supply \(e>1\) units of labor inelastically, and agent 3 supplies one unit. Apples are produced from labor by \(y=l\). Normalize the apple price to \(p=1\), and let \(w\) be the wage.
question: Find a competitive equilibrium for the apple economy with recursive social utility.
solution: Agent \(i\) takes other agents' consumptions and prices as given. In \(U_i\), the only term affected by agent \(i\)'s own apple choice is \(\log x_i/(1-\beta^3)\), so each agent spends all labor income on apples. Thus \[x_1=we,\qquad x_2=we,\qquad x_3=w.\] Total labor supply is \(2e+1\), and one unit of labor produces one apple, so aggregate apple supply is \(2e+1\). Apple-market clearing requires \[we+we+w=w(2e+1)=2e+1.\] Since \(2e+1>0\), \(w=1\). Therefore an equilibrium, with apples as numeraire, is \[p=1,\qquad w=1,\qquad (x_1,x_2,x_3)=(e,e,1),\qquad y=2e+1.\] Labor and apple markets clear, and each consumer maximizes because utility is strictly increasing in own consumption and labor is inelastically supplied.

title: Recursive Social Utility on a Three-Person Cycle
learning_objective: Solve a cyclic recursive utility system and express each utility as a weighted sum of log consumptions.
preliminaries: There are three individuals and one consumption good, apples. Consumptions satisfy \(x_i>0\). Let \(s(1)=2\), \(s(2)=3\), and \(s(3)=1\). Assume \(0\le \beta<1\), so the recursive utility system has a finite solution.
question: Each individual has utility \[U_i(x_1,x_2,x_3)=\log x_i+\beta U_{s(i)}(x_1,x_2,x_3).\] Find the functional form of \(U_1\), \(U_2\), and \(U_3\).
solution: The system is \[U_1=\log x_1+\beta U_2,\qquad U_2=\log x_2+\beta U_3,\qquad U_3=\log x_3+\beta U_1.\] Substituting forward, \[U_1=\log x_1+\beta\log x_2+\beta^2\log x_3+\beta^3U_1.\] Since \(1-\beta^3>0\), \[U_1=\frac{\log x_1+\beta\log x_2+\beta^2\log x_3}{1-\beta^3}.\] Cycling indices gives \[U_2=\frac{\log x_2+\beta\log x_3+\beta^2\log x_1}{1-\beta^3},\qquad U_3=\frac{\log x_3+\beta\log x_1+\beta^2\log x_2}{1-\beta^3}.\] Direct substitution of these three formulas into the original recursion verifies the solution.

title: Marginal Incentives Under Consumption Externalities
learning_objective: Explain why payoff externalities need not affect a price-taking consumer's private marginal choice.
preliminaries: Use the three-person utility functions \[U_i=(\log x_i+\beta\log x_{s(i)}+\beta^2\log x_{s(s(i))})/(1-\beta^3)\] with \(0\le \beta<1\). Each consumer chooses only his own apple consumption subject to his own budget and takes others' consumptions as fixed.
question: Does the consumption externality distort any marginal incentives in the competitive equilibrium?
solution: No private marginal incentive is distorted in the consumer's own choice problem. For agent \(i\), the derivative of utility with respect to the consumption he controls is \[\frac{\partial U_i}{\partial x_i}=\frac{1}{(1-\beta^3)x_i}.\] The terms involving other agents' consumptions are constants from agent \(i\)'s perspective, because he cannot choose \(x_{s(i)}\) or \(x_{s(s(i))}\). Therefore the consumer's optimality condition is the same as for a monotone transformation of \(\log x_i\): spend all available income on apples. The externality changes utility levels and social marginal effects, but it does not change the private marginal tradeoff faced by any price-taking individual in this one-good economy.

title: Monopoly Pricing with Shutdown Demand
learning_objective: Solve monopoly pricing with a boundary case where marginal cost exceeds choke price.
preliminaries: A monopolist has constant marginal cost \(c_1>0\) and faces demand \(D(p)=\max\{1-p,0\}\). Profit is \(\pi(p)=(p-c_1)D(p)\).
question: Find the monopoly price as \(c_1\) varies.
solution: If \(c_1\ge 1\), any price below one has negative margin and any price at least one yields zero demand and zero profit. Thus setting \(p=1\) is optimal. If \(0<c_1<1\), the firm maximizes \((p-c_1)(1-p)\) on \([c_1,1]\). The objective is strictly concave, and the FOC is \(1+c_1-2p=0\). Hence \(p^M=(1+c_1)/2\). Combining cases, \[p^M(c_1)=\begin{cases}(1+c_1)/2,&0<c_1\le 1,\\1,&c_1>1.\end{cases}\]

title: Equal Prices in Pure Bertrand Equilibrium
learning_objective: Prove that pure equilibria in this asymmetric-cost Bertrand game must have equal prices.
preliminaries: Two firms sell a homogeneous good. Costs satisfy \(0<c_1<c_2\). Demand is \(D(p)=\max\{1-p,0\}\). Consumers buy from the lower-price firm; if prices tie, firm 1 receives share \(\alpha_1\in[0,1]\). Firm 1's monopoly price is \(p_1^M\), and \(c_2<p_1^M\).
question: Show that any pure-strategy Nash equilibrium must have \(p_1=p_2\).
solution: Suppose first that \(p_1>p_2\). If \(p_2\ge c_2\), firm 1 gets no demand and can undercut to a price in \((c_1,p_2)\), earning positive profit. If \(p_2<c_2\), firm 2 earns negative profit when it sells and can raise to \(c_2\) to get nonnegative profit. Thus \(p_1>p_2\) is impossible. Suppose instead that \(p_1<p_2\). If \(p_1\ge c_2\), firm 2 can undercut firm 1 slightly and earn positive profit. If \(p_1<c_2\), firm 1 can raise price slightly while staying below \(\min\{p_2,c_2\}\), keeping all demand and increasing its margin. Thus \(p_1<p_2\) is impossible. Therefore any pure equilibrium must have equal prices.

title: Bertrand Equilibrium with Favorable Tie-Breaking
learning_objective: Verify the undominated pure equilibrium when ties favor the low-cost firm.
preliminaries: In the asymmetric-cost Bertrand game, \(0<c_1<c_2<p_1^M\), where \(p_1^M\) is firm 1's monopoly price. Demand is \(D(p)=\max\{1-p,0\}\). If prices tie and \(\alpha_1=1\), firm 1 serves the whole market.
question: Find the unique pure-strategy Nash equilibrium in weakly undominated strategies when \(\alpha_1=1\).
solution: The equilibrium is \((p_1,p_2)=(c_2,c_2)\). Firm 1 serves the whole market and earns \((c_2-c_1)(1-c_2)>0\). Because \(c_2<p_1^M\), firm 1's monopoly profit is increasing at \(c_2\), so lowering price reduces profit, while raising price loses demand. Firm 2 earns zero. It cannot profitably lower price below \(c_2\), because that gives negative margin, and raising price gives zero demand and the same zero profit. Thus \((c_2,c_2)\) is a Nash equilibrium. In weakly undominated strategies it is unique: prices below \(c_2\) are weakly dominated for firm 2, and any common price above \(c_2\) can be profitably undercut by firm 2. Therefore \((c_2,c_2)\) is the unique pure equilibrium in weakly undominated strategies.

title: No Pure Equilibrium with Unfavorable Tie-Breaking
learning_objective: Show why a pure equilibrium fails when the low-cost firm receives less than the full tie market.
preliminaries: Use the asymmetric-cost Bertrand model with \(0<c_1<c_2<p_1^M\). At equal prices, firm 1 receives market share \(\alpha_1\in[0,1)\). Any pure equilibrium must have equal prices.
question: Is there a pure-strategy Nash equilibrium when \(\alpha_1<1\)?
solution: No. Any pure equilibrium must have \(p_1=p_2=p\). If \(p<c_2\), firm 2 sells at negative margin or uses a weakly dominated below-cost price. If \(p>c_2\), firm 2 can slightly undercut and earn positive profit. If \(p=c_2\), firm 1 receives only share \(\alpha_1<1\) and earns \(\alpha_1(c_2-c_1)(1-c_2)\). By choosing \(c_2-\varepsilon\) for sufficiently small \(\varepsilon>0\), firm 1 captures the full market at almost the same margin and demand, earning strictly more because \(\alpha_1<1\). Thus no common price can be a Nash equilibrium, so no pure-strategy equilibrium exists.
