title: Nonattainment in a Discontinuous Undercutting Problem
learning_objective: Explain why a best response may fail to exist because the profitable undercutting set is open.
preliminaries: Firm 1 has cost \(c_1\), firm 2 posts a finite price \(p_2>c_2>c_1\), and ties give firm 1 share \(\alpha_1<1\). Demand is \(D(p)=1-p\) for \(p\le 1\), and assume the relevant prices are below the choke price.
question: Does firm 1's profit maximization problem always have a solution against fixed \(p_2>c_2\) when \(\alpha_1<1\)?
solution: Not always. If firm 1 sets \(p_1<p_2\), it captures the market and earns \((p_1-c_1)(1-p_1)\). If it sets \(p_1=p_2\), it receives only share \(\alpha_1<1\). If the monopoly profit function is increasing up to \(p_2\), then firm 1 wants the highest price strictly below \(p_2\). The relevant problem is \[\max_{p_1\in[c_1,p_2)}(p_1-c_1)(1-p_1).\] This interval is open at \(p_2\). The supremum is approached as \(p_1\uparrow p_2\), but it is not attained. Pricing exactly \(p_2\) causes a discontinuous drop in demand share because \(\alpha_1<1\). Hence the best-response problem may have no solution.

title: Separating Insurance Contracts with Two Risk Types
learning_objective: Check incentive compatibility and zero profit for a simple menu of insurance contracts.
preliminaries: There are two risk types. A type \(H\) has loss probability \(q_H=1/2\); type \(L\) has loss probability \(q_L=1/4\). Loss size is \(D=100\). Initial wealth is \(200\). A contract \((P,I)\) charges premium \(P\) and pays indemnity \(I\) if loss occurs. Insurers are risk neutral and break even type by type if \(P=q_t I\). Consumers have expected utility with \(u(w)=\sqrt{w}\).
question: Consider contracts \(C_H=(50,100)\) and \(C_L=(10,40)\). Do they break even type by type? Does the menu separate the two types?
solution: Break-even premiums are \(q_HI_H=(1/2)100=50\) and \(q_LI_L=(1/4)40=10\), so each contract breaks even for its intended type. Compute expected utilities. Type \(H\) choosing \(C_H\) gets wealth \(150\) in both states, so utility \(\sqrt{150}\). Type \(H\) choosing \(C_L\) gets no-loss wealth \(190\) and loss wealth \(130\), so expected utility \((1/2)\sqrt{190}+(1/2)\sqrt{130}\), which is approximately \(12.59\), above \(\sqrt{150}\approx12.25\). Thus \(H\) would deviate to \(C_L\). The menu does not separate, even though each contract breaks even for its intended type. The low-risk contract gives too much coverage at too low a premium from the high-risk type's perspective.

title: A Lemons Market Cutoff Equilibrium
learning_objective: Solve for market breakdown or trade in a simple adverse-selection model.
preliminaries: Car quality \(q\) is uniformly distributed on \([0,1]\). Sellers know \(q\) and value a car at \(q\). Buyers do not observe \(q\) and value a car at \(\frac{3}{2}q\). A competitive market price \(p\) is paid for every car offered. A seller sells if \(p\ge q\).
question: Find all competitive equilibrium prices with positive trade. Is there market breakdown?
solution: At price \(p\in[0,1]\), sellers with \(q\le p\) offer cars, so the conditional expected quality is \(E[q\mid q\le p]=p/2\). Buyers' expected value of an offered car is \((3/2)(p/2)=3p/4\). In a competitive equilibrium with positive trade and risk-neutral buyers, price must equal expected buyer value, so \(p=3p/4\), implying \(p=0\). This contradicts positive trade with \(p>0\). If \(p>1\), all cars are offered and buyers' expected value is \((3/2)(1/2)=3/4\), so such a price cannot be sustained. Thus the only equilibrium is market breakdown at \(p=0\), with no positive-quality sellers willing to sell. The adverse selection feedback lowers expected quality below any positive candidate price.

title: When a Lemons Market Has Positive Trade
learning_objective: Show how stronger buyer surplus can restore positive trade in a lemons market.
preliminaries: Quality \(q\sim U[0,1]\). Sellers value a car at \(q\). Buyers value it at \(kq\), where \(k>1\). At price \(p\), sellers with \(q\le p\) sell. With risk-neutral competitive buyers, a zero-profit competitive price equals the expected buyer value conditional on the qualities traded.
question: Assume \(k=3\). Find competitive equilibrium prices and traded qualities. Compare with the case \(k=3/2\).
solution: For \(p\in[0,1]\), the offered qualities are \([0,p]\), with conditional mean \(p/2\). Buyers' expected value is \(kp/2=3p/2\). A positive interior cutoff equilibrium would require zero expected buyer profit, \(p=3p/2\), which has no solution with \(p>0\). Thus no equilibrium has only a proper subset of qualities trading. If \(p\ge1\), all qualities \(q\in[0,1]\) are offered, the mean quality is \(1/2\), and the expected buyer value is \(3/2\). Zero profit for competitive buyers therefore gives the full-trade equilibrium price \(p=3/2\), with all sellers trading. For \(k=3/2\), the interior cutoff equation is \(p=3p/4\), so no positive cutoff price is sustainable and the market breaks down. The higher buyer valuation therefore restores full trade.

title: Moral Hazard and a Bonus Contract
learning_objective: Derive the minimum bonus that implements high effort in a binary-output agency problem.
preliminaries: An agent chooses effort \(e\in\{L,H\}\). High output \(Y=100\) occurs with probability \(0.8\) under \(H\) and \(0.4\) under \(L\). Low output is \(0\). Effort costs are \(c_H=10\), \(c_L=0\). The agent is risk neutral, has outside option \(0\), and wages must be nonnegative. A contract pays \(b\) after high output and \(s\) after low output.
question: Find the cheapest contract that induces high effort and satisfies participation. What is the principal's expected profit under that contract?
solution: The agent's expected utility from high effort is \(0.8b+0.2s-10\). From low effort it is \(0.4b+0.6s\). Incentive compatibility for high effort requires \(0.8b+0.2s-10\ge0.4b+0.6s\), or \(0.4(b-s)\ge10\), so \(b-s\ge25\). Participation requires \(0.8b+0.2s-10\ge0\). To minimize expected wage cost under high effort, set the low-output wage as low as possible: \(s=0\). Then incentive compatibility requires \(b\ge25\), and participation is \(0.8b-10\ge0\), requiring \(b\ge12.5\). Thus \(b=25,s=0\) is cheapest. Expected wage cost is \(0.8\cdot25=20\). Expected output under high effort is \(80\), so the principal's expected profit is \(80-20=60\).

title: A Separating Education Signaling Equilibrium
learning_objective: Verify separating equilibrium conditions in a two-type signaling model.
preliminaries: Workers are high productivity \(H\) or low productivity \(L\). Productivities are \(y_H=10\) and \(y_L=4\). A worker chooses education \(e\ge0\), which does not affect productivity. Education cost is \(c_H(e)=e\) for \(H\) and \(c_L(e)=2e\) for \(L\). Competitive firms pay expected productivity conditional on observed education.
question: Find values of \(e^*>0\) for which there is a separating equilibrium where \(H\) chooses \(e^*\), \(L\) chooses \(0\), firms pay \(10\) after \(e^*\), and firms pay \(4\) after \(0\).
solution: Given the proposed beliefs, type \(H\) gets payoff \(10-e^*\) from choosing \(e^*\) and payoff \(4\) from mimicking \(L\) at \(0\). Thus \(H\)'s incentive constraint is \(10-e^*\ge4\), or \(e^*\le6\). Type \(L\) gets payoff \(4\) from choosing \(0\) and payoff \(10-2e^*\) from mimicking \(H\). Thus \(L\)'s incentive constraint is \(4\ge10-2e^*\), or \(e^*\ge3\). Therefore any \(e^*\in[3,6]\) can support the separating allocation with the stated on-path beliefs and competitive wages. Off-path beliefs can assign any unexpected education level to type \(L\), inducing wage \(4\), which prevents profitable deviations to other education levels. The least-cost separating signal is \(e^*=3\).

title: Increasing Differences Imply Single Crossing
learning_objective: Prove that increasing differences is stronger than the single-crossing property.
preliminaries: Let \(X,T\subseteq\mathbb{R}\). A function \(f:X\times T\to\mathbb{R}\) has increasing differences if for \(x'>x\) and \(t'>t\), \(f(x',t')-f(x,t')\ge f(x',t)-f(x,t)\). It has the single-crossing property if \(f(x',t)\ge f(x,t)\) implies \(f(x',t')\ge f(x,t')\), with strict implication preserved for strict inequalities.
question: Prove that increasing differences imply the single-crossing property.
solution: Assume \(f\) has increasing differences. Fix \(x'>x\) and \(t'>t\). If \(f(x',t)\ge f(x,t)\), then \(f(x',t)-f(x,t)\ge 0\). Increasing differences gives \[f(x',t')-f(x,t')\ge f(x',t)-f(x,t)\ge 0.\] Hence \(f(x',t')\ge f(x,t')\). If the premise is strict, then \(f(x',t)-f(x,t)>0\), so the same inequality yields \(f(x',t')-f(x,t')>0\). Thus the weak and strict single-crossing implications both hold.

title: Single Crossing under Increasing Transformations
learning_objective: Show that the single-crossing property is ordinal under strictly increasing transformations.
preliminaries: Let \(f:X\times T\to\mathbb{R}\) satisfy the single-crossing property. Let \(\phi:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) be strictly increasing and define \(h(x,t)=\phi(f(x,t))\).
question: Prove that if \(f\) has the single-crossing property in \((x,t)\), then any strictly increasing transformation \(h=\phi\circ f\) also has the single-crossing property.
solution: Fix \(x'>x\) and \(t'>t\). Because \(\phi\) is strictly increasing, it preserves order: \(h(x',t)\ge h(x,t)\) if and only if \(f(x',t)\ge f(x,t)\), and strict inequalities are also preserved. Suppose \(h(x',t)\ge h(x,t)\). Then \(f(x',t)\ge f(x,t)\). Since \(f\) is single-crossing, \(f(x',t')\ge f(x,t')\). Applying the increasing transformation again gives \(h(x',t')\ge h(x,t')\). The same argument with strict inequalities proves the strict single-crossing implication. Therefore \(h\) has the single-crossing property.

source.original_question: Prove that if f has the single-crossing property, then any strictly increasing transformation of f has the single-crossing property.
source.notes: Converted from the order-preservation argument in the answer key.

validation.notes: Strict monotonicity preserves weak and strict order comparisons, which is exactly what single crossing uses.

title: Profit Maximization with Decreasing Returns
learning_objective: Derive factor demand, supply, and profit for a one-input decreasing returns technology.
preliminaries: A competitive firm produces output \(y=x^a\) from input \(x\ge 0\), where \(0<a<1\). Output price is \(p>0\), input price is \(w>0\), and profit is \(\pi(x)=px^a-wx\).
question: Find the profit-maximizing input demand, output supply, and profit function. Verify the second-order condition.
solution: For \(x>0\), the first-order condition is \(pa x^{a-1}=w\). Since \(a-1<0\), this gives \(x^*(p,w)=(ap/w)^{1/(1-a)}\). The second derivative is \(pa(a-1)x^{a-2}<0\), so the objective is strictly concave on \(x>0\) and the solution is unique. Output supply is \(y^*=(x^*)^a=(ap/w)^{a/(1-a)}\). Profit can be simplified using the first-order condition \(wx^*=ap(x^*)^a\). Hence \(\pi(p,w)=p(x^*)^a-wx^*=(1-a)p(x^*)^a=(1-a)p(ap/w)^{a/(1-a)}\). This is positive, homogeneous of degree one in \((p,w)\), increasing in \(p\), and decreasing in \(w\).

title: Cost Minimization for Cobb-Douglas Production
learning_objective: Derive conditional factor demands and the cost function for a Cobb-Douglas technology.
preliminaries: A firm produces \(y=x_1^{\alpha}x_2^{1-\alpha}\), with \(0<\alpha<1\). Input prices are \(w_1,w_2>0\). The cost function is \(c(w,y)=\min_{x\ge 0} w_1x_1+w_2x_2\) subject to \(x_1^{\alpha}x_2^{1-\alpha}\ge y\).
question: Find the conditional factor demands and the cost function.
solution: At a positive target \(y>0\), the constraint binds and the solution is interior. The first-order conditions imply \(w_1/w_2=(\alpha/(1-\alpha))(x_2/x_1)\). Thus cost shares are \(w_1x_1=\alpha C\) and \(w_2x_2=(1-\alpha)C\), where \(C=c(w,y)\). Therefore \(x_1=\alpha C/w_1\) and \(x_2=(1-\alpha)C/w_2\). Substitute into production: \(y=(\alpha C/w_1)^{\alpha}((1-\alpha)C/w_2)^{1-\alpha}=C\alpha^{\alpha}(1-\alpha)^{1-\alpha}/(w_1^{\alpha}w_2^{1-\alpha})\). Hence \(c(w,y)=y w_1^{\alpha}w_2^{1-\alpha}/(\alpha^{\alpha}(1-\alpha)^{1-\alpha})\). Conditional demands are \(x_1(w,y)=\alpha c(w,y)/w_1\) and \(x_2(w,y)=(1-\alpha)c(w,y)/w_2\). For \(y=0\), both demands and cost are zero.

title: Constant Returns and Competitive Profits
learning_objective: Explain why constant returns technologies have zero or unbounded competitive profits.
preliminaries: A production set \(Y\) has constant returns to scale if \(y\in Y\) and \(t\ge 0\) imply \(ty\in Y\). A competitive firm facing price vector \(p\) solves \(\sup_{y\in Y} p\cdot y\).
question: Prove that if \(Y\) has constant returns to scale and there exists \(\bar y\in Y\) with \(p\cdot \bar y>0\), then no finite profit maximum exists. What happens if \(p\cdot y\le 0\) for every \(y\in Y\) and \(0\in Y\)?
solution: If \(p\cdot\bar y>0\), constant returns imply \(t\bar y\in Y\) for every \(t\ge 0\). Profits along this ray are \(p\cdot(t\bar y)=t(p\cdot\bar y)\), which diverge to infinity as \(t\to\infty\). Thus the supremum profit is infinite and no finite maximizer exists. If instead \(p\cdot y\le 0\) for all \(y\in Y\) and \(0\in Y\), the firm can choose inactivity \(0\) and earn \(0\), while no feasible plan earns more. The maximum profit is therefore \(0\), and every feasible \(y\) with \(p\cdot y=0\) is profit-maximizing. Constant returns are compatible with competitive equilibrium only at prices that do not allow strictly positive profits.

title: Recovering Net Supply from a Profit Function
learning_objective: Use Hotelling's lemma to recover net supply and check homogeneity.
preliminaries: If a profit function \(\pi(p)\) is differentiable, Hotelling's lemma says that net supply is \(y(p)=\nabla_p\pi(p)\). A valid profit function should be homogeneous of degree one and convex in prices.
question: Let \(\pi(p_1,p_2)=\sqrt{p_1^2+p_2^2}\) for \(p_1,p_2>0\). Recover net supply, and verify that \(p\cdot y(p)=\pi(p)\).
solution: Differentiate: \(y_1(p)=\partial\pi/\partial p_1=p_1/\sqrt{p_1^2+p_2^2}\) and \(y_2(p)=p_2/\sqrt{p_1^2+p_2^2}\). Then \(p\cdot y=p_1^2/\sqrt{p_1^2+p_2^2}+p_2^2/\sqrt{p_1^2+p_2^2}=\sqrt{p_1^2+p_2^2}=\pi(p)\). This equality is Euler's theorem for a differentiable homogeneous function of degree one. The function is homogeneous of degree one because \(\pi(tp)=t\pi(p)\) for \(t>0\), and it is convex because it is the Euclidean norm. Thus it has the formal properties of a profit function, and the gradient is homogeneous of degree zero.

title: Shephard's Lemma for a CES Cost Function
learning_objective: Differentiate a cost function to obtain conditional factor demands and verify cost exhaustion.
preliminaries: Assume the cost function is \(c(w_1,w_2,y)=y(w_1^{1-\sigma}+w_2^{1-\sigma})^{1/(1-\sigma)}\), with \(\sigma>0\) and \(\sigma\ne 1\). Let \(A=w_1^{1-\sigma}+w_2^{1-\sigma}\).
question: Use Shephard's lemma to compute conditional input demands \(x_1(w,y)\) and \(x_2(w,y)\). Show that \(w_1x_1+w_2x_2=c(w,y)\).
solution: Differentiate \(c=yA^{1/(1-\sigma)}\). For input 1, \(\partial c/\partial w_1=y(1/(1-\sigma))A^{1/(1-\sigma)-1}(1-\sigma)w_1^{-\sigma}=yA^{\sigma/(1-\sigma)}w_1^{-\sigma}\). Similarly, \(x_2=yA^{\sigma/(1-\sigma)}w_2^{-\sigma}\). Cost exhaustion follows by multiplication: \(w_1x_1+w_2x_2=yA^{\sigma/(1-\sigma)}(w_1^{1-\sigma}+w_2^{1-\sigma})=yA^{\sigma/(1-\sigma)}A=yA^{1/(1-\sigma)}=c(w,y)\). The equality reflects homogeneity of degree one of the cost function in input prices.

title: Envelope Derivative of Profit with Respect to Productivity
learning_objective: Use the envelope theorem to sign the derivative of optimized profit with respect to a productivity parameter.
preliminaries: Use the profit function \(\pi(p,w,\delta)=\max_{x\ge 0}\{p g(\delta)f(x)-w\cdot x\}\). Assume the maximizer \(x^*(p,w,\delta)\gg 0\) exists, \(f(x)\ge 0\), \(p>0\), and \(g'(\delta)>0\) for \(\delta>0\).
question: Show that \(\partial \pi(p,w,\delta)/\partial \delta\ge 0\) for \(\delta>0\).
solution: By the envelope theorem, the derivative of the value function with respect to \(\delta\) is the partial derivative of the objective with respect to \(\delta\), evaluated at the optimizer, holding the optimizer fixed. Hence \[\frac{\partial \pi(p,w,\delta)}{\partial \delta}=p g'(\delta) f(x^*(p,w,\delta)).\] Since \(p>0\), \(g'(\delta)>0\), and the production function maps into \(\mathbb{R}_+\), this derivative is nonnegative. It is strictly positive whenever the optimal output of the base technology satisfies \(f(x^*(p,w,\delta))>0\).

title: Output Response to Input-Augmenting Productivity
learning_objective: Relate an increase in a productivity parameter to output supply through the convexity of the profit function in output price.
preliminaries: Let \(r=p g(\delta)\). For the base technology, define \(\varphi(r,w)=\max_{x\ge 0}\{r f(x)-w\cdot x\}\). Assume the maximizer is unique and interior, so the supplied base output \(y(r,w)=f(x^*(r,w))\) is well defined. Since \(g'(\delta)>0\), increasing \(\delta\) increases \(r\).
question: If input one becomes more productive, so \(\delta\) increases, what happens to the profit-maximizing output? Prove your answer.
solution: The profit problem can be rewritten as \(\varphi(r,w)\) with \(r=p g(\delta)\). For fixed \(w\), \(\varphi(r,w)\) is the pointwise supremum of affine functions of \(r\), so it is convex in \(r\). By Hotelling's lemma, where differentiable, \[\frac{\partial \varphi(r,w)}{\partial r}=f(x^*(r,w))=y(r,w).\] The derivative of a convex function is nondecreasing. Therefore \(y(r,w)\) is nondecreasing in \(r\). Because \(r=p g(\delta)\) and \(p g'(\delta)>0\), \(r\) is increasing in \(\delta\). Thus the profit-maximizing output is nondecreasing as \(\delta\) rises. If \(\varphi\) is not differentiable, the same conclusion holds for the output-supply correspondence in the monotone comparative statics sense: its selections are ordered by the increasing subdifferential of the convex profit function.

source.notes: Converted from the answer key idea that delta raises the effective output price; proof tightened using convexity of profit in price.

validation.notes: The conclusion follows from Hotelling's lemma and monotonicity of derivatives of convex value functions.

title: Profit Function with Input-Augmenting Productivity
learning_objective: Write the profit function when a productivity parameter scales effective input use and output.
preliminaries: A firm has output price \(p>0\), input prices \(w=(w_1,w_2)\gg 0\), and chooses inputs \(x=(x_1,x_2)\in\mathbb{R}_+^2\). Output is \(y=f(\delta x_1,x_2)\), where \(\delta\ge 0\). The technology satisfies \(f(\delta x_1,x_2)=g(\delta)f(x_1,x_2)\), with \(g(0)=0\), \(g(1)=1\), and \(g'(\delta)>0\) for \(\delta>0\).
question: Write the firm's profit function \(\pi(p,w,\delta)\).
solution: Profit is revenue less input cost, maximized over feasible input vectors. Therefore \[\pi(p,w,\delta)=\max_{x_1,x_2\ge 0}\left\{p f(\delta x_1,x_2)-w_1x_1-w_2x_2\right\}.\] Using the assumed scaling property of the technology, this can equivalently be written as \[\pi(p,w,\delta)=\max_{x_1,x_2\ge 0}\left\{p g(\delta)f(x_1,x_2)-w_1x_1-w_2x_2\right\}.\] This expression makes clear that \(\delta\) acts like a multiplicative increase in the effective output price \(p g(\delta)\) for the base technology \(f(x_1,x_2)\).

source.notes: Converted from the answer key and restated with the shared setup.

validation.notes: The objective is revenue minus input cost and the alternate expression follows directly from the stated scaling property.

title: Conditional Input Demand for a Two-Output Technology
learning_objective: Derive conditional input demand from a binding production requirement.
preliminaries: Input prices are \(w_1,w_2>0\). Outputs are \(q=(q_1,q_2)\in\mathbb{R}_+^2\). Producing \(q\) requires inputs \(z=(z_1,z_2)\in\mathbb{R}_+^2\) satisfying \(z_1^{1/3}z_2^{1/3}\ge (q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{1/\sigma}\), where \(\sigma>0\).
question: Derive the conditional input demand \(z(w,q)\).
solution: Let \(A(q)=(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{1/\sigma}\). The cost-minimization problem is \[\min_{z_1,z_2\ge 0} w_1z_1+w_2z_2 \quad \text{s.t.}\quad z_1^{1/3}z_2^{1/3}\ge A(q).\] For \(q\ne 0\), the constraint binds and the optimum is interior. Using logs, the constraint is \((1/3)\log z_1+(1/3)\log z_2\ge \log A(q)\). The FOCs are \(w_1=\lambda/(3z_1)\) and \(w_2=\lambda/(3z_2)\), so \(w_1z_1=w_2z_2\) and \(z_1=(w_2/w_1)z_2\). The binding constraint implies \(z_1z_2=A(q)^3=(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/\sigma}\). Therefore \[z_1(w,q)=\sqrt{\frac{w_2}{w_1}}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)},\qquad z_2(w,q)=\sqrt{\frac{w_1}{w_2}}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}.\] If \(q=(0,0)\), the same formula gives \(z=(0,0)\).

title: Cost Function for a Multi-Output Requirement
learning_objective: Compute the cost function by substituting conditional input demands.
preliminaries: Use the technology \(z_1^{1/3}z_2^{1/3}\ge (q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{1/\sigma}\) and input prices \(w_1,w_2>0\). The conditional input demands are \(z_1=\sqrt{w_2/w_1}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}\) and \(z_2=\sqrt{w_1/w_2}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}\).
question: Derive the cost function \(C(w,q)\).
solution: The cost function is the minimized value \(C(w,q)=w_1z_1(w,q)+w_2z_2(w,q)\). Substitution gives \[C(w,q)=w_1\sqrt{\frac{w_2}{w_1}}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}+w_2\sqrt{\frac{w_1}{w_2}}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}.\] Both terms equal \(\sqrt{w_1w_2}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}\), so \[C(w,q)=2\sqrt{w_1w_2}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}.\] This also gives zero cost at \(q=(0,0)\), as expected.

title: Output Supply with \(\sigma=3/2\)
learning_objective: Use a cost function to derive profit-maximizing outputs.
preliminaries: Let output prices be \(p_1,p_2>0\), input prices be \(w_1,w_2>0\), and set \(\sigma=3/2\). The cost function is \(C(w,q)=2\sqrt{w_1w_2}(q_1^{3/2}+q_2^{3/2})\).
question: Derive the profit-maximizing output vector \(q^*(p,w)\).
solution: The firm solves \[\max_{q_1,q_2\ge 0} p_1q_1+p_2q_2-2\sqrt{w_1w_2}(q_1^{3/2}+q_2^{3/2}).\] The objective is strictly concave in \((q_1,q_2)\) because \(q_i^{3/2}\) is strictly convex and enters with a negative coefficient. Since \(p_i>0\), the optimum is interior. The FOCs are \[p_i-3\sqrt{w_1w_2}q_i^{1/2}=0\quad i=1,2.\] Hence \[q_i^*(p,w)=\frac{p_i^2}{9w_1w_2}\quad i=1,2.\]

title: Unconditional Input Demand with \(\sigma=3/2\)
learning_objective: Obtain unconditional input demand by composing conditional demand with output supply.
preliminaries: For \(\sigma=3/2\), conditional input demands are \(z_1(w,q)=\sqrt{w_2/w_1}(q_1^{3/2}+q_2^{3/2})\) and \(z_2(w,q)=\sqrt{w_1/w_2}(q_1^{3/2}+q_2^{3/2})\). Profit-maximizing outputs are \(q_i^*(p,w)=p_i^2/(9w_1w_2)\).
question: Derive the unconditional input demands \(x_1^*(p,w)\) and \(x_2^*(p,w)\).
solution: Unconditional input demand is conditional demand evaluated at the profit-maximizing output: \(x^*(p,w)=z(w,q^*(p,w))\). Since \((q_i^*)^{3/2}=p_i^3/(27(w_1w_2)^{3/2})\), \[q_1^{*3/2}+q_2^{*3/2}=\frac{p_1^3+p_2^3}{27(w_1w_2)^{3/2}}.\] Therefore \[x_1^*(p,w)=\sqrt{\frac{w_2}{w_1}}\frac{p_1^3+p_2^3}{27(w_1w_2)^{3/2}}=\frac{p_1^3+p_2^3}{27w_1^2w_2},\] and \[x_2^*(p,w)=\sqrt{\frac{w_1}{w_2}}\frac{p_1^3+p_2^3}{27(w_1w_2)^{3/2}}=\frac{p_1^3+p_2^3}{27w_1w_2^2}.\]

title: Input Price Derivative of Profit
learning_objective: Apply Hotelling's lemma to an input price derivative.
preliminaries: For a differentiable profit function \(\pi(p,w)=\max_q\{p\cdot q-C(w,q)\}\), Hotelling's lemma gives \(\partial \pi/\partial w_i=-x_i^*(p,w)\), where \(x_i^*\) is unconditional demand for input \(i\). In this problem \(x_1^*(p,w)=(p_1^3+p_2^3)/(27w_1^2w_2)\).
question: Give \(\partial \pi(p,w)/\partial w_1\) without first deriving and differentiating a closed-form profit function.
solution: By Hotelling's lemma, the derivative of maximum profit with respect to an input price equals minus the profit-maximizing use of that input. Therefore \[\frac{\partial \pi(p,w)}{\partial w_1}=-x_1^*(p,w)=-\frac{p_1^3+p_2^3}{27w_1^2w_2}.\] The sign is negative because increasing an input price lowers profit at the rate at which the firm uses that input, holding the optimized choice fixed by the envelope theorem.

title: WARP Implies the Compensated Law of Demand
learning_objective: Prove the compensated law of demand from revealed preference reasoning.
preliminaries: Let \(x(p,w)\) be a single-valued demand satisfying Walras' law. Starting from \((p,w)\) with \(x=x(p,w)\), define a Slutsky-compensated change to \(p'\) by setting \(w'=p'\cdot x\), and let \(x'=x(p',w')\).
question: Assume demand satisfies WARP and Walras' law. Prove that \((p'-p)\cdot(x'-x)\le 0\) for every Slutsky-compensated price change.
solution: By construction, \(p'\cdot x=w'\), so the old bundle \(x\) is affordable at the new budget. Since \(x'\) is chosen at \((p',w')\), \(x'\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x\). Suppose, toward contradiction, that \((p'-p)\cdot(x'-x)>0\). Since Walras' law gives \(p'\cdot x'=w'=p'\cdot x\), we have \(p'\cdot(x'-x)=0\). The strict inequality then implies \(-p\cdot(x'-x)>0\), or \(p\cdot x'<p\cdot x=w\). Thus \(x'\) was strictly affordable at the original budget when \(x\) was chosen, so \(x\) is strictly directly revealed preferred to \(x'\). We now have \(x'\) revealed preferred to \(x\) and \(x\) strictly revealed preferred to \(x'\), contradicting WARP. Therefore \((p'-p)\cdot(x'-x)\le 0\).

title: A Three-Observation GARP Cycle
learning_objective: Detect a generalized axiom of revealed preference violation through a revealed preference chain.
preliminaries: Bundle \(x^t\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^s\) if \(p^t\cdot x^t\ge p^t\cdot x^s\), and strictly directly revealed preferred if the inequality is strict. GARP rules out a chain \(x^1 R x^2 R \cdots R x^n\) together with \(x^n\) strictly directly revealed preferred to \(x^1\).
question: Consider three observations: \(p^1=(1,0.4)\), \(x^1=(1,0)\); \(p^2=(0.4,1)\), \(x^2=(0,1)\); and \(p^3=(1,1)\), \(x^3=(0.6,0.6)\). Test GARP.
solution: At observation 1, wealth is \(1\). The cost of \(x^3\) is \(1\cdot0.6+0.4\cdot0.6=0.84\), so \(x^1\) is strictly directly revealed preferred to \(x^3\). At observation 3, wealth is \(1.2\). The cost of \(x^2\) is \(1\), so \(x^3\) is strictly directly revealed preferred to \(x^2\). At observation 2, wealth is \(1\). The cost of \(x^1\) is \(0.4\), so \(x^2\) is strictly directly revealed preferred to \(x^1\). Thus there is a strict revealed preference cycle \(x^1 R x^3 R x^2 R x^1\). This violates GARP and hence cannot be rationalized by a locally nonsatiated utility function that chooses these bundles from the corresponding budgets.

title: Laspeyres and Paasche Quantity Indexes
learning_objective: Interpret quantity indexes using revealed preference inequalities.
preliminaries: For periods 0 and 1, let \(p^t\) be prices and \(x^t\) chosen quantities. The Laspeyres quantity index is \(L_q=p^0\cdot x^1/(p^0\cdot x^0)\), and the Paasche quantity index is \(P_q=p^1\cdot x^1/(p^1\cdot x^0)\).
question: Prove: if \(L_q<1\), then \(x^0\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^1\). If \(P_q>1\), then \(x^1\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^0\).
solution: If \(L_q<1\), then \(p^0\cdot x^1<p^0\cdot x^0\). At period 0 prices, bundle \(x^1\) cost strictly less than the chosen bundle \(x^0\), so \(x^1\) was affordable when \(x^0\) was chosen. Therefore \(x^0\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^1\), strictly if the definition records strict affordability. If \(P_q>1\), then \(p^1\cdot x^1>p^1\cdot x^0\). At period 1 prices, bundle \(x^0\) was affordable when \(x^1\) was chosen. Hence \(x^1\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^0\). These conclusions use only observed choices and budget inequalities; they do not require a utility representation or price index theory beyond the definitions.

title: Testing WARP with Two Observations
learning_objective: Use price and quantity observations to test the weak axiom of revealed preference.
preliminaries: Observation \(t\) consists of prices \(p^t\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^L\), chosen bundle \(x^t\in\mathbb{R}_+^L\), and wealth \(w^t=p^t\cdot x^t\). Bundle \(x^t\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^s\) if \(p^t\cdot x^t\ge p^t\cdot x^s\). WARP is violated if \(x^t\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^s\), \(x^s\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^t\), and the two bundles differ.
question: A consumer has two observations: \(p^1=(1,2)\), \(x^1=(4,1)\), and \(p^2=(2,1)\), \(x^2=(1,4)\). Test WARP and state the revealed preference relations.
solution: Compute expenditures. At prices \(p^1\), the chosen bundle costs \(p^1\cdot x^1=1\cdot4+2\cdot1=6\). The other bundle costs \(p^1\cdot x^2=1\cdot1+2\cdot4=9\). Since \(x^2\) was not affordable in observation 1, \(x^1\) is not directly revealed preferred to \(x^2\). At prices \(p^2\), the chosen bundle costs \(p^2\cdot x^2=2\cdot1+1\cdot4=6\). The other bundle costs \(p^2\cdot x^1=2\cdot4+1\cdot1=9\). Thus \(x^1\) was not affordable in observation 2, so \(x^2\) is not directly revealed preferred to \(x^1\). There is no revealed preference relation between the two observations and hence no WARP violation.

title: A Numeric WARP Nonviolation
learning_objective: Distinguish a WARP violation from one-way revealed preference using expenditure inequalities.
preliminaries: Use the WARP definitions from the canonical WARP problem. A WARP violation occurs when each chosen bundle was affordable when the other was chosen and the bundles differ.
question: A consumer has observations \(p^1=(1,1)\), \(x^1=(2,2)\), and \(p^2=(2,1)\), \(x^2=(1,1)\). Does the data violate WARP?
solution: At observation 1, wealth is \(p^1\cdot x^1=4\). The second bundle would have cost \(p^1\cdot x^2=2\), so \(x^2\) was affordable when \(x^1\) was chosen. Therefore \(x^1\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^2\). At observation 2, wealth is \(p^2\cdot x^2=3\). The first bundle would have cost \(p^2\cdot x^1=2\cdot2+1\cdot2=6\), so \(x^1\) was not affordable when \(x^2\) was chosen. Hence \(x^2\) is not directly revealed preferred to \(x^1\). The data do not violate WARP. A common error is to compare coordinatewise bundle sizes; WARP depends on affordability at the observed prices, not on whether one bundle contains more of every good.

title: Constructing a WARP Violation
learning_objective: Construct and verify a two-observation dataset that violates WARP.
preliminaries: For observations \((p^t,x^t)\), let wealth equal observed expenditure \(p^t\cdot x^t\). WARP forbids choosing \(x^t\) over an affordable distinct \(x^s\) and also choosing \(x^s\) over an affordable distinct \(x^t\).
question: Consider \(p^1=(1,1)\), \(x^1=(2,0)\), \(p^2=(1,1)\), and \(x^2=(0,2)\). Show that these observations violate WARP. Then explain why the violation is not removed by the fact that prices are identical.
solution: At prices \(p^1=(1,1)\), observed wealth is \(2\), and \(p^1\cdot x^2=2\). Thus \(x^2\) was affordable when \(x^1\) was chosen, so \(x^1\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^2\). At prices \(p^2=(1,1)\), observed wealth is also \(2\), and \(p^2\cdot x^1=2\). Thus \(x^1\) was affordable when \(x^2\) was chosen, so \(x^2\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^1\). The bundles are distinct. Therefore WARP is violated: the same budget set generated two different single-valued choices. Identical prices make the violation especially stark; if the demand function is single-valued, one budget cannot rationalize two different chosen bundles.

title: Observing Full Insurance and the Fair Premium
learning_objective: Infer the insurance price from an observed full-insurance choice by a strictly risk-averse expected utility maximizer.
preliminaries: An individual with wealth \(w>L>0\) loses \(L\) with probability \(\pi\in(0,1)\). One unit of insurance costs \(p\) and pays 1 only in the loss state. The individual can choose any real amount \(x\) of insurance and has strictly increasing, strictly concave expected utility.
question: If the individual chooses \(x=L\) units of insurance, what can be inferred about \(p\) versus \(\pi\)? Prove and give intuition.
solution: Expected utility is \[V(x)=\pi u(w-L+x-px)+(1-\pi)u(w-px).\] At \(x=L\), wealth in both states is \(w-pL\). The derivative is \[V'(x)=\pi(1-p)u'(w-L+(1-p)x)-(1-\pi)p u'(w-px).\] Evaluating at \(x=L\) gives \[V'(L)=u'(w-pL)\left[\pi(1-p)-(1-\pi)p\right]=u'(w-pL)(\pi-p).\] Since utility is strictly concave, the objective is strictly concave, and an interior optimum at \(x=L\) requires \(V'(L)=0\). Therefore \(p=\pi\). Intuitively, full insurance equalizes wealth across states. A strictly risk-averse person chooses exactly full insurance only when insurance is actuarially fair. If \(p<\pi\), insurance is cheap and the person would overinsure; if \(p>\pi\), insurance is expensive and the person would underinsure.

source.original_question: If the individual chooses L units of insurance, what can you infer about p versus pi?
source.notes: Converted from the answer key; equality conclusion stated using the interior unrestricted-choice FOC.

validation.notes: The derivative at full insurance is proportional to pi minus p, so unrestricted interior choice implies equality.

title: Insurance Demand with Log Utility
learning_objective: Derive closed-form insurance demand for a log-utility expected utility maximizer.
preliminaries: An individual has wealth \(w>L>0\), loss probability \(\pi\), and can buy any real amount \(x\) of insurance. One unit costs \(p\in(0,1)\) and pays 1 in the loss state. Bernoulli utility is \(u(W)=\ln W\). Assume the optimum keeps state-contingent wealth positive.
question: How much insurance will this individual purchase? Derive the optimal \(x\); do not assume it equals \(L\).
solution: Expected utility is \[V(x)=\pi\ln(w-L+(1-p)x)+(1-\pi)\ln(w-px).\] The first-order condition is \[\frac{\pi(1-p)}{w-L+(1-p)x}-\frac{(1-\pi)p}{w-px}=0.\] Cross-multiplying, \[\pi(1-p)(w-px)=(1-\pi)p(w-L+(1-p)x).\] Solving for \(x\) gives \[x^*=\frac{p(1-\pi)L+w(\pi-p)}{p(1-p)}.\] The second derivative is negative wherever both state-contingent wealth levels are positive, so this first-order condition is sufficient for an interior optimum. When \(p=\pi\), the expression reduces to \(x^*=L\), the full-insurance result.

source.original_question: Suppose utility is ln. How much insurance will this individual purchase?

title: Why Non-Affine Transformations Change Expected Utility Choices
learning_objective: Explain why expected utility representations are unique only up to positive affine transformations, not arbitrary increasing transformations.
preliminaries: A Bernoulli utility function represents preferences over lotteries through expected utility. Two Bernoulli utility functions represent the same von Neumann-Morgenstern preferences over lotteries if and only if they are related by a positive affine transformation \(\tilde u=a u+b\), where \(a>0\).
question: A colleague says the log-utility insurance demand cannot be correct because any strictly increasing transformation represents the same preferences, and a square-root utility gives a different demand. Do you agree? Explain.
solution: Disagree. For preferences over certain wealth levels, any strictly increasing transformation preserves the ordinal ranking of sure amounts. But expected utility preferences are preferences over lotteries, and the Bernoulli utility representation is cardinal up to positive affine transformations only. A nonlinear increasing transformation, such as transforming \(\ln W\) into \(\sqrt{W}\), generally changes attitudes toward risk and therefore changes lottery rankings. Thus a square-root utility function need not represent the same expected utility preferences as log utility. It is not a contradiction that it gives a different insurance demand. The log-utility demand is correct for the expected utility preference whose Bernoulli utility is \(\ln W\); a different non-affine Bernoulli utility describes a different risk preference.

source.original_question: Do you agree that a different answer under square-root utility means the log answer cannot be correct?

validation.notes: The distinction between ordinal utility over sure outcomes and vNM utility over lotteries is correctly stated.

title: Threshold for Buying a Uniform Asset-Payoff Upgrade
learning_objective: Use indirect utility to compute the minimum payoff multiplier that compensates for a reduction in initial wealth.
preliminaries: In the state-asset problem, indirect utility can be written as \(V=(1+\delta)\ln w+K(r,q,p,\delta)\), where \(K\) collects terms independent of wealth except for the payoff vector. The individual can pay 1 unit of wealth, reducing wealth from \(w>1\) to \(w-1\), to multiply every payoff \(r(s)\) by \(\alpha>1\).
question: What is the smallest value of \(\alpha\) for which the individual accepts the opportunity?
solution: Multiplying every payoff \(r(s)\) by \(\alpha\) adds \(\delta\sum_s p(s)\ln\alpha=\delta\ln\alpha\) to indirect utility. The individual accepts if \[(1+\delta)\ln(w-1)+K+\delta\ln\alpha\ge (1+\delta)\ln w+K.\] Cancelling \(K\) and rearranging, \[\delta\ln\alpha\ge (1+\delta)\ln\left(\frac{w}{w-1}\right).\] Therefore the smallest acceptable multiplier is \[\alpha^*=\left(\frac{w}{w-1}\right)^{(1+\delta)/\delta}.\] At this value the individual is exactly indifferent; any larger \(\alpha\) is strictly preferred.

source.original_question: What is the smallest alpha for which the individual accepts spending 1 unit of wealth to multiply all asset payoffs by alpha?
source.notes: Converted from the answer key's indirect-utility comparison.

validation.notes: The threshold follows from cancelling common indirect-utility terms and exponentiating.

title: Log Utility Demand for State-Contingent Assets
learning_objective: Solve for optimal current consumption and purchases of Arrow-style state assets with log utility.
preliminaries: At date 0 the individual has wealth \(w>0\). Current consumption \(x_0\) has price 1. There are states \(s=1,\ldots,S\) with probabilities \(p(s)>0\) summing to one. Asset \(s\) costs \(q(s)>0\), pays \(r(s)>0\) only in state \(s\), and the individual buys \(x(s)>0\). Utility is \(\ln x_0+\delta\sum_s p(s)\ln(x(s)r(s))\), with \(0<\delta\le 1\).
question: Find optimal current consumption \(x_0\) and optimal asset purchases \(x(s)\).
solution: The budget constraint is \[x_0+\sum_{s=1}^S q(s)x(s)=w.\] The Lagrangian is \[\mathcal{L}=\ln x_0+\delta\sum_s p(s)\ln(x(s)r(s))+\lambda\left(w-x_0-\sum_s q(s)x(s)\right).\] The first-order conditions are \(1/x_0=\lambda\) and \(\delta p(s)/x(s)=\lambda q(s)\) for each state \(s\). Hence \[x(s)=\frac{\delta p(s)x_0}{q(s)}.\] Substituting into the budget gives \[x_0+\sum_s q(s)\frac{\delta p(s)x_0}{q(s)}=x_0+\delta x_0\sum_s p(s)=(1+\delta)x_0=w.\] Thus \[x_0^*=\frac{w}{1+\delta},\qquad x^*(s)=\frac{\delta p(s)w}{(1+\delta)q(s)}.\] The payoff multiplier \(r(s)\) affects utility levels but not asset quantities because it enters the logarithm additively as \(\ln r(s)\).

source.original_question: Find optimal consumption at date 0 and optimal asset purchases at date 0.

title: Indirect Utility and Marginal Utility of Wealth with State Assets
learning_objective: Compute indirect utility and identify marginal utility of wealth after solving a state-contingent asset allocation problem.
preliminaries: Use the optimal choices \(x_0^*=w/(1+\delta)\) and \(x^*(s)=\delta p(s)w/((1+\delta)q(s))\) from the log-utility state-asset problem. State probabilities sum to one and all prices, payoffs, and probabilities are strictly positive.
question: Compute the indirect utility function as a function of wealth, prices, probabilities, and parameters. What is the marginal utility of wealth?
solution: Substituting the optimal choices into utility gives \[V(w,q,r,p,\delta)=\ln\left(\frac{w}{1+\delta}\right)+\delta\sum_s p(s)\ln\left(\frac{\delta p(s)w r(s)}{(1+\delta)q(s)}\right).\] This can be rearranged as \[V=(1+\delta)\ln w-(1+\delta)\ln(1+\delta)+\delta\ln\delta+\delta\sum_s p(s)\left[\ln p(s)+\ln r(s)-\ln q(s)\right].\] The only terms depending on \(w\) are summarized by \((1+\delta)\ln w\), so \[\frac{\partial V}{\partial w}=\frac{1+\delta}{w}.\] This is also the Lagrange multiplier from the budget constraint because \(\lambda=1/x_0^*=(1+\delta)/w\).

title: Strictly Positive Investment in a Favorable Risky Asset
learning_objective: Show that a risky asset with positive expected return receives a strictly positive optimal investment.
preliminaries: An investor with wealth \(w>0\) chooses \(z\in[0,w]\). The asset return is \(H>0\) in the high state and \(L\in(-1,0)\) in the low state, with probabilities \(p\) and \(1-p\). Expected return is positive: \(pH+(1-p)L>0\). Utility \(u\) is strictly increasing, strictly concave, twice continuously differentiable, and satisfies the Inada condition at zero.
question: Show that the individual's optimal investment \(z^*\) in the risky asset is strictly positive.
solution: Final wealth is \(w+zH\) in the high state and \(w+zL\) in the low state. Expected utility is \[F(z)=p u(w+zH)+(1-p)u(w+zL).\] Its right derivative at \(z=0\) is \[F'(0)=pH u'(w)+(1-p)L u'(w)=u'(w)\left[pH+(1-p)L\right]>0.\] Thus moving a small amount from \(z=0\) into the risky asset strictly raises expected utility. Therefore \(z=0\) cannot be optimal, and every optimum satisfies \(z^*>0\). Concavity of \(F\) ensures this local improvement argument is consistent with the global problem.

title: Expected Utility Maximization with Actuarially Fair Insurance
learning_objective: Formulate the insurance choice problem with an actuarially fair premium.
preliminaries: An individual has initial wealth \(w\), faces loss \(L>0\) with probability \(p\in(0,1)\), and has no loss with probability \(1-p\). Insurance covers fraction \(\alpha\in[0,1]\) of the loss and costs premium \(\alpha pL\). Utility \(u\) is increasing, strictly concave, and twice continuously differentiable.
question: Write the individual's maximization problem.
solution: If the loss occurs, final wealth is initial wealth minus the premium, minus the uncovered part of the loss: \[W_L(\alpha)=w-\alpha pL-(1-\alpha)L.\] If no loss occurs, final wealth is initial wealth minus the premium: \[W_N(\alpha)=w-\alpha pL.\] The expected utility maximization problem is therefore \[\max_{\alpha\in[0,1]}\; p u\left(w-\alpha pL-(1-\alpha)L\right)+(1-p)u\left(w-\alpha pL\right).\]

title: Full Insurance under Actuarially Fair Pricing
learning_objective: Prove that a risk-averse expected utility maximizer fully insures at an actuarially fair premium.
preliminaries: Use the insurance problem \(\max_{\alpha\in[0,1]} p u(W_L(\alpha))+(1-p)u(W_N(\alpha))\), where \(W_L(\alpha)=w-\alpha pL-(1-\alpha)L\) and \(W_N(\alpha)=w-\alpha pL\). Utility is strictly increasing and strictly concave.
question: What is the optimal insurance coverage fraction \(\alpha\)? Prove your answer.
solution: The derivative of expected utility with respect to \(\alpha\) is \[p(1-p)L u'(W_L(\alpha))-(1-p)pL u'(W_N(\alpha))=p(1-p)L\left[u'(W_L(\alpha))-u'(W_N(\alpha))\right].\] For \(\alpha<1\), \(W_L(\alpha)=W_N(\alpha)-(1-\alpha)L<W_N(\alpha)\). Since \(u\) is strictly concave, \(u'\) is strictly decreasing, so \(u'(W_L(\alpha))>u'(W_N(\alpha))\). Thus expected utility is strictly increasing for every \(\alpha<1\). The optimal choice is therefore the boundary \[\alpha^*=1.\] At \(\alpha=1\), final wealth equals \(w-pL\) in both states, eliminating all risk. This is exactly what risk aversion implies under actuarially fair insurance.

title: Loss Reduction with Fair Insurance
learning_objective: Show how actuarially fair insurance separates the insurance decision from the efficient loss-reduction decision.
preliminaries: After choosing loss-reduction spending \(x\ge 0\), the loss is \(L(x)\), where \(L(x)>0\), \(L'(x)\le 0\), and \(L''(x)\ge 0\). Insurance covers fraction \(\alpha\in[0,1]\) at premium \(\alpha pL(x)\). Utility is strictly increasing and strictly concave.
question: What can be said about the optimal choices of loss-reduction spending \(x\) and insurance coverage \(\alpha\)? Provide formal analysis and intuition.
solution: For fixed \(x\), the insurance problem is the same fair-insurance problem with loss \(L(x)\). If \(L(x)>0\), strict risk aversion and actuarially fair pricing imply \(\alpha^*(x)=1\). If \(L(x)=0\), insurance is payoff-irrelevant. With full insurance, final wealth is the same in both states and equals \[w-x-pL(x).\] Therefore the choice of \(x\) maximizes \(u(w-x-pL(x))\), which is equivalent, because \(u\) is strictly increasing, to minimizing \[x+pL(x).\] An interior optimum satisfies \[1+pL'(x^*)=0.\] Because \(L''(x)\ge 0\), the function \(x+pL(x)\) is convex, so this first-order condition is sufficient when an interior solution exists. If no interior solution exists, the optimum is at a boundary, for example \(x^*=0\) when \(1+pL'(0)\ge 0\). Intuitively, full insurance removes risk, while loss reduction is chosen only up to the point where one more dollar of prevention equals the expected reduction in loss, \(-pL'(x)\).
